ray-project/ray#49292.body:
### Description

The [architecture docs](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/serve/architecture.html) state:

```
Each DeploymentHandle continuously polls the controller to check for new deployment replicas. Whenever new replicas are discovered, it sends any buffered or new queries to the replica until max_ongoing_requests is reached. Queries are sent to replicas in round-robin fashion, subject to the constraint that no replica is handling more than max_ongoing_requests requests at a time.
```

This is outdated. My understanding is in recent versions of Ray Serve a power of two scheduler is now used to determine which replicas queries get sent to.


Suggested correction:

```
Each DeploymentHandle continuously polls the controller to check for new deployment replicas. Whenever new replicas are discovered, it sends any buffered or new queries to the replica until max_ongoing_requests is reached. Queries are sent to replicas using a power of two scheduler, subject to the constraint that no replica is handling more than max_ongoing_requests requests at a time.
```




### Link

_No response_
ray-project/ray#49292.title: [serve] Architecture docs mention round-robin and not pow-of-two scheduler

ray-project/ray#49454.body:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3fcf6fc0-003c-4a8c-b601-234e300b7ff6) # How do I use this migration guide? If you came here from a deprecation warning for a specific API, please search up that API and jump to the relevant section! # Background Ray Train V2 is a slimmed version of Ray Train that is fully decoupled from the Ray Tune library and provides usability and stability improvements, while also enabling feature development. [Please see the REP for more motivation and context behind this effort.](https://github.com/ray-project/enhancements/blob/main/reps/2024-10-18-train-tune-api-revamp/2024-10-18-train-tune-api-revamp.md) If you have any questions or feedback, please reply to this issue! We understand that API changes can be disruptive -- we're here to help you through the process, and we believe that the benefits will be worth it. # Migration Timeline | Target Ray Release and Date | Actual Ray Release | Migration Description | | ----------- | ----------- | --------------------- | | 2.43 (February 27, 2025) | 2.43 | New version of Ray Train with the proposed changes will be available by setting an environment variable `RAY_TRAIN_V2_ENABLED=1`. API deprecation messages that aid in upgrading will be added to the old versions of the APIs. Note that feature development on the old version will be stopped. | | 2.43 + 6 months (August 31, 2025)| 2.51 | Feature flag will be enabled by default, which forces users to migrate, but falling back is still an option with `RAY_TRAIN_V2_ENABLED=0`. | | TBD | TBD | Old code path is deleted. | # How do I enable Train V2? The environment variable needs to be set before importing any Ray Train or Ray Tune code. Here are the recommended ways to do this: 1. Option 1: Set the environment variable in your Ray environment (Docker image, dotenv, etc.). ```bash export RAY_TRAIN_V2_ENABLED=1 ``` 2. Option 2: Set the environment variable in your shell before running your script. This is useful if you want to test the new API on a subset of workloads rather than across the board. ```bash RAY_TRAIN_V2_ENABLED=1 python my_ray_train_entrypoint.py ``` 3. Option 3: Set the environment variable at the very top of your script. This is the least recommended option, but it useful if you're primarily running in a notebook. ```python import os os.environ["RAY_TRAIN_V2_ENABLED"] = "1" import ray.train ``` # Ray Train Migrations ## Job-level Restoration (`Trainer.restore`, `Trainer.can_restore`) ```diff -Trainer.restore(path_or_uri) -Trainer.can_restore(path_or_uri) +Trainer( + ..., + run_config=RunConfig( + name=, storage_path=... + ) + ) ``` This API previously accepted a Train run directory path and loaded state (including user training code) partially from `.pkl` file and partially from new arguments passed to the constructor. This was confusing, error-prone, and the API design relied on a bad practice of serializing and saving user code. Now, trainers only have a single constructor codepath, which takes in all arguments needed to construct the trainer (with no more brittle serialization/deserialization logic). Ray Train will auto-detect if an existing run snapshot exists at the path configured by `RunConfig(storage_path, name)` and will populate `ray.train.get_checkpoint` with the latest checkpoint, accessible by all Train workers. If you want to start a brand new training run without any prior checkpoint history, please specify a new, unique `RunConfig(storage_path, name)`. Previously, it was possible to start a new training run with an existing run `RunConfig(name)`. However, this was not recommended, since it would overwrite the previous run's metadata, and the old run outputs would be corrupted. ⚠️ Launch a brand new new training runs by assigning it a unique name. We recommend generating this unique name as a uuid before submitting the job. Then, upon any driver failures, a job retry would re-initialize the run with the same name, which will load the latest checkpoint information from persistent storage. Trainer-level restoration can still be achieved, as shown below:
Before After
```python def train_fn_per_worker(config): checkpoint = ray.train.get_checkpoint() # Perform your training-specific checkpoint recovery here... storage_path = "s3://bucket/" name = "" run_path = f"{storage_path}/{name}" if TorchTrainer.can_restore(run_path): # Some parameters are optionally re-specified. trainer = TorchTrainer.restore(run_path, datasets={...}) result = trainer.fit() else: trainer = TorchTrainer( train_fn_per_worker, datasets={...}, scaling_config=train.ScalingConfig(num_workers=2), run_config=train.RunConfig(storage_path=storage_path, name=name), ) result = trainer.fit() ``` ```python def train_fn_per_worker(config): # `ray.train.get_checkpoint` will be populated as long as your run # is pointing to the same directory. checkpoint = ray.train.get_checkpoint() # Perform your training-specific checkpoint recovery here... storage_path = "s3://bucket/" name = "" # The second run will automatically find the snapshot saved by the first run # at (storage_path, name). trainer = TorchTrainer( train_fn_per_worker, datasets={...}, scaling_config=train.ScalingConfig(num_workers=2), run_config=train.RunConfig(storage_path=storage_path, name=name), ) result = trainer.fit() ```
See the [new Ray Train fault tolerance user guide](https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/user-guides/fault-tolerance.html) for more details. ## `RunConfig(callbacks)` ```diff -ray.train.RunConfig(callbacks: List[ray.tune.Callback]) +ray.train.RunConfig(callbacks: List[ray.train.UserCallback]) ``` Ray Tune callbacks are no longer supported in Ray Train. If your application relies on Ray Tune callbacks, you can still use them by running Ray Train as a single Ray Tune trial. See [this user guide section](https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/user-guides/hyperparameter-optimization.html#advanced-using-ray-tune-callbacks). ### New `UserCallback` interface Ray Train provides a `UserCallback` interface that can be used to access some Ray Train-specific functionality. For now, this [API](https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/api/doc/ray.train.UserCallback.html#ray.train.UserCallback) is still experimental (`DeveloperAPI`) and may change in the future. Ray Train provides some default callback implementations, including the `TuneReportCallback` that propagates reported metrics from Ray Train to Ray Tune, when using them together. See [this user guide section](https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/user-guides/hyperparameter-optimization.html#reporting-metrics-and-checkpoints) for more details. ## `RunConfig(sync_config)` ```diff -ray.train.RunConfig(sync_config) -ray.train.SyncConfig ``` The `SyncConfig` settings previously controlled some Ray Tune implementation details (such as driver log syncing) that are no longer relevant in Ray Train V2. This API also configured the [training worker artifact syncing feature, which is now deprecated](https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/user-guides/persistent-storage.html#deprecated-persisting-training-artifacts). Note that this API was turned off by default. ## `RunConfig(stop)` ```diff ray.train.RunConfig( - stop: Optional[Union[Mapping, ray.tune.Stopper]] ) ``` The `stop` argument took in a Ray Tune `Stopper` object, which is no longer supported. Early stopping can be configured within your own training loop directly, and it is already provided out of the box by many libraries (ex: [Lightning](https://lightning.ai/docs/pytorch/stable/common/early_stopping.html)). ## Ray Train Console Output (`RunConfig(verbose, progress_reporter)`) ```diff ray.train.RunConfig( - verbose: Optional[Union[int, "AirVerbosity", "Verbosity"]], - progress_reporter: Optional[ray.tune.progress_reporter.ProgressReporter], ) ``` Ray Train no longer inherits [Ray Tune's console output and progress reporting](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/tune/api/reporters.html). Ray Train will still emit system logs (ex: when the worker group is starting up), but otherwise, the only logs that will be emitted are those from the training workers (your user-defined training code). We may have plans in the future to re-introduce some logging features -- feel free to leave suggestions in this issue! If you rely on Ray Tune's progress reporting, you can still use it by running Ray Train as a single Ray Tune trial. See [this user guide section](https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/user-guides/hyperparameter-optimization.html#advanced-using-ray-tune-callbacks). ## `RunConfig(log_to_file)` ```diff ray.train.RunConfig( - log_to_file: Union[bool, str, Tuple[str, str]] ) ``` Logs from workers are already dumped via Ray worker logs and can be [viewed in the Ray Dashboard](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/ray-observability/getting-started.html#logs-view). Also, this configuration has never worked to capture log output from the training workers. ## `ScalingConfig(trainer_resources)` ```diff ray.train.ScalingConfig( num_workers: int, use_gpu: bool, resources_per_worker: Dict, accelerator_type: Optional[str], placement_strategy: str, - trainer_resources: Optional[Dict], ) ``` `trainer_resources` was previously used to specify the resources of the `ray.tune.Trainable` actor that launched the Ray Train driver. This defaulted to 1 CPU, which users often needed to manually override with `trainer_resources={"CPU": 0}`. Ray Train V2 now schedules the Ray Train driver with 0 resources on the node that calls `trainer.fit`. There's no need for this to be customized, so we are removing the API to avoid confusion between this and `resources_per_worker`. ## `FailureConfig(fail_fast)` ```diff ray.train.FailureConfig( max_failures: int, - fail_fast: Union[bool, str], ) ``` `fail_fast` is a parameter that only makes sense in Ray Tune when there are multiple trials running concurrently. If one of many trials fails and `fail_fast=True`, then the whole run exits. This parameter has no effect in Ray Train. ## `CheckpointConfig(checkpoint_frequency, checkpoint_at_end)` ```diff ray.train.CheckpointConfig( num_to_keep: int, checkpoint_score_attribute: Optional[str], checkpoint_score_order: Optional[str], - checkpoint_frequency: Optional[int], - checkpoint_at_end: Optional[bool], ) ``` `checkpoint_frequency` and `checkpoint_at_end` are no longer supported, since checkpointing frequency is now determined by user code for all frameworks. For example, a PyTorch training loop can save and report a checkpoint every `N` batches, or every epoch. See the [Ray Train checkpointing user guide](https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/user-guides/checkpoints.html). ## Reporting metrics without checkpoints (`ray.train.report({...}, checkpoint=None)`) Free-floating metrics are no longer automatically saved. Free floating metrics were previously saved to a `progress.csv` or `result.json` file when all workers call `train.report(metrics, checkpoint=None)` without an attached checkpoint. See this [user guide section](https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/user-guides/monitoring-logging.html#train-metric-only-reporting-deprecation) for more details on potential workarounds. ## Default metrics (`training_iteration`, `timesteps_total`, `episodes_this_iter`, etc.) Default metrics such as `time_this_iter_s` and `training_iteration` will no longer be automatically reported when calling `ray.train.report`. The definition of an “iteration” is often subject to the user code, so reporting these default values is confusing in most cases. For example, an iteration usually corresponds to a single batch of training, but if the user calls `train.report` once per epoch, then the default `training_iteration` tracks the epoch index instead. Also, there are many irrelevant metrics (ex: RLlib metrics) that clutter the output. ## `Trainer(resume_from_checkpoint)` ```diff -Trainer(resume_from_checkpoint: Optional[Checkpoint]) ``` `Trainer(resume_from_checkpoint)` was a common source of confusion and will be deprecated. `resume_from_checkpoint` was meant to provide a starting checkpoint for a new run, rather than restore all state (such as the latest checkpoint reported to Ray Train) from the previous run. See the following example for a more transparent way to accomplish the same thing:
Before After
```python def train_fn_per_worker(config: dict): # This is the checkpoint passed to `resume_from_checkpoint` # if no other checkpoints have been saved. # Otherwise this is the latest reported checkpoint. checkpoint = ray.train.get_checkpoint() trainer = TorchTrainer( train_fn_per_worker, ..., resume_from_checkpoint=ray.train.Checkpoint(...) ) ``` ```python def train_fn_per_worker(config: dict): # Equivalent behavior that is explicit and more flexible. checkpoint = ( ray.train.get_checkpoint() or config.get("resume_from_checkpoint") ) trainer = TorchTrainer( train_fn_per_worker, train_loop_config={"resume_from_checkpoint": ray.train.Checkpoint(...)}, ) ```
## `ray.train.get_context().get_trial_*` ```diff context: TrainContext = ray.train.get_context() context.get_world_size() context.get_local_world_size() context.get_world_rank() context.get_local_rank() context.get_node_rank() -context.get_trial_dir() -context.get_trial_id() -context.get_trial_name() -context.get_trial_resources() context.get_experiment_name() context.get_storage() ``` The `TrainContext` should no longer be used to access Tune-specific information. If using Ray Tune with Ray Train, you can still pass this context through to the Train workers. See this [quickstart for a usage example](https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/user-guides/hyperparameter-optimization.html#quickstart). ## `Trainer(metadata)`, `ray.train.get_context().get_metadata()` ```diff -Trainer(metadata: Dict) -ray.train.get_context().get_metadata() ``` This field was an experimental API introduced by [this REP](https://github.com/ray-project/enhancements/blob/main/reps/2023-06-06-simplify-sync.md), with the main purpose of allowing users to save data preprocessor state (ex: statistics computed over the entire dataset for normalization). Instead of saving this information by passing it as `metadata`, users can save it separately before training, or attach it explicitly as part of checkpoint state. ```python metadata = ... def train_fn_per_worker(config): metadata = config["metadata"] with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as temp_dir: # torch.save(...) # Attach metadata to a separate file in the checkpoint directory. with open("metadata.json", "w") as f: json.dump(metadata, f) checkpoint = ray.train.Checkpoint.from_directory(temp_dir) trainer = TorchTrainer( train_fn_per_worker, train_loop_config={"metadata": metadata} ) ``` # Ray Tune Migrations ## Tune + Train Integration (`Tuner(trainer)`) ```diff -Tuner( - trainable: Union[ - ray.tune.Trainable, ray.train.BaseTrainer - ] = TorchTrainer(...) -) +Tuner(trainable: ray.tune.Trainable = train_driver_fn) ``` Ray Tune for hyperparameter search over distributed training runs is still supported in a slightly modified but more intuitive way. Now, instead of passing in the Trainer instance into the Tuner, launch the Ray Train driver in a regular Tune function. This improves the hyperparameter tuning user experience [[1](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/47439), [2](https://ray.slack.com/archives/CSX7HVB5L/p1721169473392809)] and eliminates the awkward config overriding logic that happens in the old `Tuner(trainer)` integration [[1](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/33539), [2](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/36082), [3](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/33454)]. See the [new Ray Train hyperparameter tuning user guide](https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/user-guides/hyperparameter-optimization.html) for more details. ## `ray.train.get_context()` -> `ray.tune.get_context()` ```diff -context: TrainContext = ray.train.get_context() +context: TuneContext = ray.tune.get_context() -context.get_world_size() -context.get_local_world_size() -context.get_world_rank() -context.get_local_rank() -context.get_node_rank() context.get_trial_dir() context.get_trial_id() context.get_trial_name() context.get_trial_resources() context.get_experiment_name() context.get_storage() ``` The `TuneContext` should no longer be used to access distributed training related information that only makes sense in the context of Ray Train. ## Configs (`tune.RunConfig`, `tune.CheckpointConfig`, `tune.SyncConfig`, `tune.FailureConfig`) ```diff ray.tune.Tuner( - run_config: ray.train.RunConfig( - checkpoint_config: ray.train.CheckpointConfig, - sync_config: ray.train.SyncConfig, - failure_config: ray.train.FailureConfig, - ) + run_config: ray.tune.RunConfig( + checkpoint_config: ray.tune.CheckpointConfig, + sync_config: ray.tune.SyncConfig, + failure_config: ray.tune.FailureConfig, + ) ) ``` There is no change in functionality, but this change is made to decouple the Ray Train and Ray Tune APIs. ## `train.report` -> `tune.report` ```diff -ray.train.report(...) +ray.tune.report(...) ``` There is no change in functionality, but this change is made to decouple the Ray Train and Ray Tune APIs. ## `train.get_checkpoint()` -> `tune.get_checkpoint()` ```diff -ray.train.get_checkpoint(...) +ray.tune.get_checkpoint(...) ``` There is no change in functionality, but this change is made to decouple the Ray Train and Ray Tune APIs. ## `train.Checkpoint` -> `tune.Checkpoint` ```diff -ray.train.Checkpoint +ray.tune.Checkpoint ``` There is no change in functionality, but this change is made to decouple the Ray Train and Ray Tune APIs.

ray-project/ray#49983.body:
## Why are these changes needed?
When trying to utilize spot instance types, we noticed the Autoscaler would get stuck attempting to scale up a node type with no availability and never recover / move on to try other node types. This change introduces some randomness, with weighting, so that the Autoscaler will try different node types on each loop and prevent the described scenario.

## Related issue number

## Checks

- [ ] I've signed off every commit(by using the -s flag, i.e., `git commit -s`) in this PR.
- [ ] I've run `scripts/format.sh` to lint the changes in this PR.
- [ ] I've included any doc changes needed for https://docs.ray.io/en/master/.
    - [ ] I've added any new APIs to the API Reference. For example, if I added a 
           method in Tune, I've added it in `doc/source/tune/api/` under the 
           corresponding `.rst` file.
- [ ] I've made sure the tests are passing. Note that there might be a few flaky tests, see the recent failures at https://flakey-tests.ray.io/
- Testing Strategy
   - [ ] Unit tests
   - [ ] Release tests
   - [ ] This PR is not tested :(

ray-project/ray#50042.body:
## Context We are deprecating the legacy `XGBoostTrainer` and `LightGBMTrainer` APIs in favor of a new, more flexible `DataParallelTrainer` API. This migration unifies the training interface across frameworks, which takes in a custom training function. [Train V2](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/49454) only supports this new API. ## ⭐ Motivation * Improve flexibility for training logic * The old API wrapped a limited subset of the XGBoost/LightGBM APIs. For example, users previously could not use `xgboost`'s [`scikit-learn` interface](https://xgboost.readthedocs.io/en/stable/python/sklearn_estimator.html) in order to use `scikit-learn`'s cross validation utility. * The new design allows full access to the native APIs (`xgboost.train`, `xgboost.XGBRegressor`, `lightgbm.train`, etc.), making it easier to adopt new upstream features. * Increase transparency * The new API clearly shows the separation of responsibility between Ray Train’s distributed XGBoost setup logic and user training code. * The new API also makes data ingestion clearer, since the user now explicitly triggers the dataset materialization and wraps it in a framework native `DMatrix` (or `Dataset` for LightGBM). * Simplify migration from non-distributed code * Users can now wrap their existing single-node training functions in `XGBoostTrainer` or `LightGBMTrainer` with minimal changes. * Remove deprecated dependencies * The old trainers relied on `xgboost_ray` / `lightgbm_ray` external packages, which are no longer maintained. ## 🚀 Quickstart Find runnable examples here, which showcase the new APIs: * [XGBoostTrainer quickstart](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/train/getting-started-xgboost.html#quickstart) * [LightGBMTrainer quickstart](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/train/getting-started-lightgbm.html#quickstart) ## 🔁 Migration guide We show the XGBoost migration examples, but the same migrations apply for the `LightGBMTrainer` with the corresponding LightGBM APIs. V1 Argument | Migration Strategy (V2) | Example -- | -- | -- `datasets` | Continue to pass your Ray Datasets into the Trainer, but you should now access them from your training function with `ray.train.get_dataset_shard("train")` and convert them to an input data format that is compatible with XGBoost/LightGBM. | `ds_shard = ray.train.get_dataset_shard("train")`
`shard_df = ds_shard.materialize().to_pandas()`
`dtrain = xgboost.DMatrix(shard_df)` `label_column` | Define in your training function and drop the column from your input features. | `X, y = df.drop("target", 1), df["target"]` `params` | Pass directly into the framework’s training API (`xgboost.train` / `lightgbm.train`). | `xgboost.train(params, ...)` `dmatrix_params` | Pass into the `xgboost.DMatrix()` constructor. | `dtrain = xgboost.DMatrix(..., **dmatrix_params)` `num_boost_round` | Pass as a positional argument to the framework training API. | `xgboost.train(..., num_boost_round=100)` `**train_kwargs` | Pass directly into the framework API call. | `xgboost.train(..., **train_kwargs)` ### LightGBM specific migrations You need to append `ray.train.lightgbm.get_network_params()` to your LightGBM parameters. [See the quickstart for a full example.](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/train/getting-started-lightgbm.html#quickstart) ## References * [`XGBoostTrainer` user guide](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/train/getting-started-xgboost.html) * [XGBoost docs](https://xgboost.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) * [`LightGBMTrainer` user guide](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/train/getting-started-lightgbm.html) * [LightGBM docs](https://lightgbm.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)

ray-project/ray#50186.body:
### Description

WandB default fps is 4 in the `Video` class. Currently there is no parameter to increase this and videos are therefore very slow when watched on WandB.

I would welcome a parameter on the `WandbLoggerCallback` or alternatively, as it will be easier to implement, a global/environment variable to pass an argument to the `fps` parameter of `wandb.Video`.

### Use case

_No response_
ray-project/ray#50186.title: [Train] Allow customization of FPS for wandb logger; instead of slow 4 FPS

ray-project/ray#50259.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

With KubeRay, setting `upscalingMode: Conservative` in the autoscaling config of a RayCluster limits the number of allowed pending launches at any time to the size of the RayCluster (i.e. the number of running worker Pods), with a minimum of 5. The V2 autoscaler determines the number of nodes to launch as follows in [reconciler.py](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/eb80f8ad76db250ca6fa42448cd1adc82232c8b4/python/ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/reconciler.py#L833):
```
num_desired_to_upscale = max(
                1,
                math.ceil(
                    upscaling_speed
                    * (
                        len(requested_instances_for_type)
                        + len(allocated_instances_for_type)
                    )
                ),
            )
```
whereas the v1 autoscaler has this block of code in [resource_demand_scheduler.py](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/85733ab60f2f5a16e92fcf046c7af485fa11c3f2/python/ray/autoscaler/_private/resource_demand_scheduler.py#L437) which determines max concurrent workers to launch for each node type:
```
max_allowed_pending_nodes = max(
                UPSCALING_INITIAL_NUM_NODES,
                int(self.upscaling_speed * max(running_nodes[node_type], 1)),
            )
```
it seems like the V2 autoscaler should check for the number of running nodes (so just `allocated_instances_for_type`), rather than including `requested_instances_for_type`. I believe this is the cause of the test failure (passes for V1 but not for V2 when checking if the # pending Pods is ever > 5 and > # running Pods) in this PR: https://buildkite.com/ray-project/ray-ecosystem-ci-kuberay-ci/builds/6525#0194d25f-22ea-4d08-87d3-e035d25eb9f4

### Versions / Dependencies

- Ray 2.41
- KubeRay nightly image

### Reproduction script

Create a RayCluster with the v2 autoscaler and a ConfigMap:
```
apiVersion: ray.io/v1
kind: RayCluster
metadata:
  name: raycluster-autoscaler
spec:
  rayVersion: '2.41.0'
  enableInTreeAutoscaling: true
  autoscalerOptions:
    upscalingMode: Conservative
    idleTimeoutSeconds: 60
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
    # Optionally specify the Autoscaler container's securityContext.
    securityContext: {}
    env: []
    envFrom: []
    resources:
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "512Mi"
      requests:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "512Mi"
  headGroupSpec:
    rayStartParams:
      num-cpus: "0"
    template:
      spec:
        containers:
        # The Ray head container
        - name: ray-head
          image: rayproject/ray:2.41.0
          ports:
          - containerPort: 6379
            name: gcs
          - containerPort: 8265
            name: dashboard
          - containerPort: 10001
            name: client
          resources:
            limits:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: "2G"
            requests:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: "2G"
          env:
            - name: RAY_enable_autoscaler_v2 # Pass env var for the autoscaler v2.
              value: "1"
          volumeMounts:
            - mountPath: /home/ray/samples
              name: ray-example-configmap
        volumes:
          - name: ray-example-configmap
            configMap:
              name: ray-example
              defaultMode: 0777
              items:
                - key: detached_actor.py
                  path: detached_actor.py
                - key: terminate_detached_actor.py
                  path: terminate_detached_actor.py
        restartPolicy: Never # No restart to avoid reuse of pod for different ray nodes.
  workerGroupSpecs:
  - replicas: 0
    minReplicas: 0
    maxReplicas: 10
    groupName: small-group
    rayStartParams: {}
    # Pod template
    template:
      spec:
        containers:
        - name: ray-worker
          image: rayproject/ray:2.41.0
          resources:
            limits:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: "1G"
            requests:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: "1G"
        restartPolicy: Never # Never restart a pod to avoid pod reuse
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: ray-example
data:
  detached_actor.py: |
    import ray
    import sys

    @ray.remote(num_cpus=1)
    class Actor:
      pass

    ray.init(namespace="default_namespace")
    for i in range (10): 
      Actor.options(name=sys.argv[1]+str(i), lifetime="detached").remote()

  terminate_detached_actor.py: |
    import ray
    import sys

    ray.init(namespace="default_namespace")
    for i in range (10): 
      detached_actor = ray.get_actor(sys.argv[1]+str(i))
      ray.kill(detached_actor)
```

Scale up multiple workers to test upscaling is rate limited to size of RayCluster:
```
export HEAD_POD=$(kubectl get pods --selector=ray.io/node-type=head -o custom-columns=POD:metadata.name --no-headers)
kubectl exec -it $HEAD_POD -- python3 /home/ray/samples/detached_actor.py actor
```

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#50259.title: [Autoscaler][V2] Autoscaler V2 does not honor 'Conservative' upscaling mode

ray-project/ray#51017.body:
## Why are these changes needed?

At the moment `AggregatorActor`s are scheduled onto the same nodes `Learner`s are residing, but not into the same bundles `Learner`s were scheduled to. This PR proposes a way to schedule `AggregatorActor`s directly into the `Learner` bundles. This procedure should also avoid some "PENDING" scenarios encountered when running on multi-node clusters.

## Related issue number

<!-- For example: "Closes #1234" -->

ray-project/ray#51080.body:
### Description

Our raylet cython file is a giant: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/_raylet.pyx, which spans for over 5K LOC.

Two issues:
- (minor) the file is too big, and hard to manage (i.e. add new code, delete old code, and glance over)
- (major) a single file is compiled in single thread for compilers; for now it takes 5-10 minutes every time to rebuild the target

Proposed solution:
- Split the raylet cython file into smaller files
- cython here is used to expose C++ impl for python usage, so all imports should be updated accordingly

This issue doesn't require people to split the target cleanly, even separating several functions out is good.

### Use case

_No response_
ray-project/ray#51080.title: [core] Split raylet  cython file into multiple files

ray-project/ray#51959.body:
### Description

Currently, (Py)Arrow extension types are not generally supported.
For example the built-in `FixedShapeTensorType` is not supported, because it extends the `BaseExtensionArray`, not the `ExtensionArray`. Moreover, the [deserialization logic](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/5b37f1cfbfaa519b251144ec1226e1d3cd5335ed/python/ray/_private/arrow_serialization.py#L237) does not work for all arrays.

For example, if an ExtensionArray has a FixedSizeListType as storage ( as is the case with the built-in `FixedShapeTensorType`), the payload is deserialized into having a child with type scalar Array, so that neither `pa.Array.from_buffers` nor `pa.ExtensionArray.from_storage` works. 

One could imagine a plugin system, in which uses can register their own ExtensionTypes which custom (de)serialization logic.

### Use case

I have a pre-existing code-base that heavily uses tensor types that are different from the types in `ray/air/util/tensor_extensions/arrow.py`. Adopting Ray into this code base is made difficult this way.
ray-project/ray#51959.title: [Data] Support general Arrow ExtensionTypes

ray-project/ray#52133.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Ray's fractional GPU resource calculation is detailed [here](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/20933#issuecomment-992198397). I expect that actors are able to be scheduled but the program hangs indicating that actors are unable to be scheduled.

In the repro script, we schedule 80 actors annotated with 0.18 gpus each over 2 nodes with 8 gpus each. I would expect that the following actors are scheduled the following way `[GPU0: 0.18*5, GPU1: 0.18*5, ..., GPU16: 0.18*5]`

### Versions / Dependencies

ray, version 2.43.0


### Reproduction script
```
import ray
import time
from ray.cluster_utils import Cluster

# Note that if we instead have the same script but just have one node with 16 gpus, the script passes.
cluster = Cluster()
cluster.add_node(
    num_cpus=1,
    num_gpus=8,
)
cluster.add_node(
    num_cpus=1,
    num_gpus=8,
)
cluster.wait_for_nodes()

ray.init(address=cluster.address)

# Note that if we instead have the same script but just change num_gpus to 0.20, the script passes.
@ray.remote(num_gpus=0.18, num_cpus=0)
class NoopActor:
    def __init__(self):
        pass

print('before scheduled')
actors = [NoopActor.remote() for i in range(80)]
ray.get([actor.__ray_ready__.remote() for actor in actors])
print('scheduled')
```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#52133.title: [Core] Ray fractional GPU unable to be scheduled

ray-project/ray#52212.body:
> We could consider changing the metric tag for the proxy metrics to `route_prefix` for clarity, but that introduces a migration for what seems to me like a very minor improvement. 

 _Originally posted by @edoakes in [#47999](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/47999#issuecomment-2417522581)_

the `route` tag on proxy metrics is causing confusion to some users. Let's migrate it to `route_prefix` incrementally
1. dual write values to both `route_prefix` and `route` for 2 versions.
2. update doc indicating the users should be using `route_prefix` and support for `route` will in dropped in 2 versions
3. update ray/serve dashboards that depend on `route`
4. after two version stop emitting `route` tag from proxy metrics

ray-project/ray#52476.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

In a Parent/Child deployment application, the LongPollHost will crash if `DeploymentResponse.cancel()` is called too quickly after calling the Parent (see code below). For simplicity, I called the Parent `DeploymentHandle` directly though the crash can be repro'd by issuing HTTP requests as well. Of note, this issue seems to be mitigated if you wait >1.1 s before calling `.cancel()` though it's hard to know exactly why (is the default Poll interval is 1s?).

**stacktrace**

```bash

INFO 2025-04-20 10:46:13,553 serve 67278 -- Started <ray.serve._private.router.SharedRouterLongPollClient object at 0x110a9df40>.
(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300) INFO 2025-04-20 10:46:13,674 default_Parent qomw9wfn 2ed64fab-aeb9-4080-b292-82a2f0a70aa8 -- Started <ray.serve._private.router.SharedRouterLongPollClient object at 0x120c7ff20>.

(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300)     raise CancelledError()

(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300)

(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300) 
(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300) Traceback (most recent call last):
(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300)   File "python/ray/_raylet.pyx", line 1888, in ray._raylet.execute_task
(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300)   File "python/ray/_raylet.pyx", line 1996, in ray._raylet.execute_task
(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300)   File "python/ray/_raylet.pyx", line 1895, in ray._raylet.execute_task
(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300)   File "python/ray/_raylet.pyx", line 1829, in ray._raylet.execute_task.function_executor
(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300)   File "python/ray/_raylet.pyx", line 4615, in ray._raylet.CoreWorker.run_async_func_or_coro_in_event_loop

(ServeReplica:default:Parent pid=67300) 
(ServeReplica:default:Child pid=67305) INFO 2025-04-20 10:46:13,808 default_Child 5mpxpitv 2ed64fab-aeb9-4080-b292-82a2f0a70aa8 -- CALL __call__ CANCELLED 126.6ms
>> RESULTS:  [RequestCancelledError('2ed64fab-aeb9-4080-b292-82a2f0a70aa8')]
```


**repro**
```Python
import asyncio

from ray import serve
from ray.serve import Application

TIMEOUT, SLEEP_S = 10., .25

@serve.deployment(max_ongoing_requests=1)
class Child:
    async def __call__(self) -> None:
        await asyncio.sleep(TIMEOUT)


@serve.deployment
class Parent:
    def __init__(self, child: Application) -> None:
        self.child = child

    async def __call__(self) -> None:
        return await self.child.remote()


app = Parent.bind(Child.bind())
app_dh = serve.run(app)


async def run() -> None:
    d_response = app_dh.remote()
    await asyncio.sleep(SLEEP_S)
    d_response.cancel()
    return await asyncio.gather(d_response, return_exceptions=True)

print(">> RESULTS: ", asyncio.run(run()))
```

### Versions / Dependencies

```bash
ray==2.44.1
python==3.12
```

### Reproduction script

see above!

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.

ray-project/ray#52493.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/56ac1886-4fdf-4770-846d-a005859e6b5d)

  Attribute "result" is unknown Pylance[reportAttributeAccessIssue]

If `DeploymentHandle` we generic in the return type of `remote` it could return the right type.

### Versions / Dependencies

ray 2.44.1

### Reproduction script

```python
# File name: chain.py
from ray import serve
from ray.serve.handle import DeploymentHandle, DeploymentResponse


@serve.deployment
class Adder:
    def __init__(self, increment: int):
        self._increment = increment

    def __call__(self, val: int) -> int:
        return val + self._increment


@serve.deployment
class Multiplier:
    def __init__(self, multiple: int):
        self._multiple = multiple

    def __call__(self, val: int) -> int:
        return val * self._multiple


@serve.deployment
class Ingress:
    def __init__(self, adder: DeploymentHandle, multiplier: DeploymentHandle):
        self._adder = adder
        self._multiplier = multiplier

    async def __call__(self, input: int) -> int:
        adder_response: DeploymentResponse = self._adder.remote(input)
        # Pass the adder response directly into the multiplier (no `await` needed).
        multiplier_response: DeploymentResponse = self._multiplier.remote(adder_response)
        # `await` the final chained response.
        return await multiplier_response


# pyright: reportFunctionMemberAccess=false
# see https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/52483

app = Ingress.bind(
    Adder.bind(increment=1),
    Multiplier.bind(multiple=2),
)

handle: DeploymentHandle = serve.run(app)
response = handle.remote(5)
assert response.result() == 12, "(5 + 1) * 2 = 12" # Cannot access attribute "result" for class "DeploymentResponseGenerator"
```

### Issue Severity

Low: It annoys or frustrates me.
ray-project/ray#52493.title: [serve] Cannot access attribute "result" for class "DeploymentResponseGenerator"   Attribute "result" is unknown

ray-project/ray#52573.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Ray autoscaling behaves unexpectedly when executing `Dataset.map(f, concurrency=N)`:
- It does not immediately provision the amount of nodes necessary for the requested concurrency of N - it only provisions a single node.
- Only when the first node is up and running, it provisions another node.
- The above repeats indefinitely, provisioning more and more nodes, seemingly bounded only by the cluster configuration, rather than the application needs - the application only uses concurrency up to N and additional nodes remain unused.

The same happens when using `ray.data.DataContext.get_current().execution_options.resource_limits.cpu` instead of the `concurrency` option of `Dataset.map`.

The same also happens when using `ray.data.DataContext.get_current().scheduling_strategy = PlacementGroupSchedulingStrategy(...)`. The `ray.data` tasks consume placement group resources, but more nodes keep getting added outside the placement group.

### Example

For example, in the reproduction script below
- we work with nodes with 2 CPUs each
- we request a Dataset.map concurrency of 2
- each execution of `f` requires only 1 CPU
- so we expect only 1 nodes to be provisioned.

Instead, the dashboard shows the following sequence of updates:
```
---
Active: 1 ray.head.default
Usage: 2.0/2.0 CPU
Demands: {'CPU': 1}: 3+ from request_resources()
---
Active: 1 ray.worker, 1 ray.head.default
Usage: 2.0/4.0 CPU
Demands: {'CPU': 1}: 5+ from request_resources()
---
Active: 2 ray.worker, 1 ray.head.default
Usage: 2.0/6.0 CPU
Demands: {'CPU': 1}: 8+ from request_resources()
---
```
... and so on, adding nodes indefinitely. I stopped it at 9 worker nodes.

Maybe related: I noticed that the log messages reporting progress of the `map` remains at 0 throughout the execution even as some tasks are completing, for example:


ray-project/ray#52864.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

### Summary:

We have set up Ray with a custom external node provider and are running the autoscaler as a separate process. The goal is for the autoscaler to detect resource demands from a Ray Serve deployment and request a specific node type from the external node provider accordingly.

### Configuration:
- The cluster config defines multiple node types, including ray.worker.4090.standard, ray.worker.4090.highmem, and ray.worker.4090.ultra.
	- A Ray Serve deployment requests the following resources:

#### autoscaler-config.yaml
```yaml
available_node_types:
  ray.worker.4090.standard:
    min_workers: 0
    max_workers: 5
    resources: {"CPU": 16, "GPU": 1, "memory": 30107260928 , "gram": 24 }
    node_config: {}

  ray.worker.4090.highmem:
    min_workers: 0
    max_workers: 5
    resources: {"CPU": 16, "GPU": 1, "memory": 62277025792 , "gram": 24}
    node_config: {}

  ray.worker.4090.ultra:
    min_workers: 0
    max_workers: 5
    resources: {"CPU": 32, "GPU": 1, "memory": 130997290496 , "gram": 24 }
    node_config: {}
```
#### serve code
```python
# for ray.worker.4090.standard
@serve.deployment(ray_actor_options={"num_cpus": 16, "num_gpus": 1, "memory": 30107260928 ,"resources": {"gram": 24}})
def CustomResourceTask(*args):
    return "ray.worker.4090.standard"


serve.run(CustomResourceTask.bind())

print("Requested additional resources...")
```


## Expected Behavior:
- When the Python code is executed, the autoscaler detects the demand and requests a single node of type ray.worker.4090.standard({"num_cpus": 16, "num_gpus": 1, "memory": 30107260928 ,"resources": {"gram": 24}}) from the node provider.
- Only that node type should be launched, as it satisfies the resource requirements.

## Actual Behavior:
- The autoscaler does initially request a ray.worker.4090.standard node as expected.
- However, immediately after, it also sends a launch request for a higher-spec node type (ray.worker.4090.highmem), even though the demand was already satisfied by the standard node.
  - When the deployment directly requests the highest-spec node type (e.g., ultra), two nodes are requested by the autoscaler.

## Observation:
- This issue does not occur if the appropriate node (ray.worker.4090.standard) already exists in the cluster before the actor is scheduled.
- In that case, the actor gets scheduled correctly, and no additional autoscaling occurs.


Could this be a race condition between the autoscaler and the node provider, where the updated resource availability has not yet propagated before the scheduler makes a decision?
Or is it a scheduling policy issue, where the autoscaler aggressively launches additional nodes before confirming that the demand has been fulfilled?

Any insights on how to avoid this type of over-provisioning (e.g., through autoscaler delay settings, demand evaluation thresholds, or conservative scheduling options) would be greatly appreciated.

### Versions / Dependencies

ray 2.45.0

### Reproduction script

## Actor list (After Serve Deployment)

ray-project/ray#52967.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

I found some issues when using `dataset.write_iceberg`.
This is my test data file `data.csv`
```
id,data
1,1
2,2
3,3
4,4
```
This is my job, I use two tasks, each consumes two rows.
```
INPUT = "./data.csv"
TABLE = "test_iceberg.table_1"
OUTPUT = "./output.csv"
ds = ray.data.read_csv(paths=INPUT).repartition(2)
ds.write_iceberg(
    table_identifier=TABLE,
    concurrency=2,
)
ds1 = ray.data.read_iceberg(
    table_identifier=TABLE,
    selected_fields=("id",),
)
ds2.write_csv(path=OUTPUT, num_rows_per_file=100)
```
I write the test data into the iceberg table, and then read it from iceberg table. The following is the output result, `3, 3` and `4, 4` disappeared.
```
id,data
1,1
2,2
1,1
2,2
```
I guess the data written to iceberg is wrong, so printed some logs when write to iceberg, and found two tasks wrote to the same file. 
```
# task1 wrote to 
s3://metastore/ab41d4e8f7d13aed/catalogs/a33d63478c6a8d5c/schemas/9dfb0fd4607250b3/tables/a1ccb264901f053d/data/category=BsC0tAlRF12eZjY2FFYLLddPhEv3oXsLOVNTnd0efuEQeR5zz7/00000-0-2cb19c81-42d2-4dbb-9143-3066b499e1cd.parque
# task2 wrote to 
s3://metastore/ab41d4e8f7d13aed/catalogs/a33d63478c6a8d5c/schemas/9dfb0fd4607250b3/tables/a1ccb264901f053d/data/category=BsC0tAlRF12eZjY2FFYLLddPhEv3oXsLOVNTnd0efuEQeR5zz7/00000-0-2cb19c81-42d2-4dbb-9143-3066b499e1cd.parque
```
The file written by the task is determined by the `uuid`. Using the same uuid for all tasks will cause all tasks to write to the same file, and only the data read by one task will be written.
So when writing to iceberg, I fixed it by using different uuid for each task. 

Here is my fix. https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/52956

### Versions / Dependencies

version: 2.44.1
pyiceberg==0.8.0

### Reproduction script

```
INPUT = "./data.csv"
TABLE = "test_iceberg.table_1"
OUTPUT = "./output.csv"
ds = ray.data.read_csv(paths=INPUT).repartition(2)
ds.write_iceberg(
    table_identifier=TABLE,
    concurrency=2,
)
ds1 = ray.data.read_iceberg(
    table_identifier=TABLE,
    selected_fields=("id",),
)
ds2.write_csv(path=OUTPUT, num_rows_per_file=100)
```

### Issue Severity

None

### What happened + What you expected to happen

More minimal & general version of: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/51408

We invalidate local references while still allowing tasks to run to completion.

### Versions / Dependencies

master

### Reproduction script

```python
import os
import signal
from typing import List

import ray
from ray._private.test_utils import SignalActor

s = SignalActor.remote()

@ray.remote(max_concurrency=2)
class A:
    def hang(self, ref: List[ray.ObjectRef]):
        print("Hanging until signal...")
        ray.get(s.wait.remote())
        print("Got signal, calling ray.get on ref")
        return ray.get(ref[0])

    def exit(self):
        os.kill(os.getpid(), signal.SIGTERM)

a = A.remote()
hang_ref = a.hang.remote([ray.put("hi")])
ray.get(a.exit.remote())
ray.get(s.send.remote())
ray.get(hang_ref)
```

The `ray.get(ref[0])` causes the worker to crash on a check failure:

ray-project/ray#53023.body:
Consolidate FastAPI Docs Path Logic in Ray Serve. This is a code refactor and has effect on user experiance.

### Goal
After [this PR](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/52892) gets merged, unify the way FastAPI docs paths are set by removing the use of the `__fastapi_docs_path__` class attribute and relying solely on the FastAPI app’s `docs_url` property evaluated on the replica to update the `docs_path` attribute on application state.

### Action Items

- Delete `__fastapi_docs_path__` assignment in `ray/serve/api.py`. https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/fe7c0c51d61ead4bb2939ac81a22812a538bc345/python/ray/serve/api.py#L239-L240

 - Eliminate checks for `docs_path` attribute from `Deployment` https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/fe7c0c51d61ead4bb2939ac81a22812a538bc345/python/ray/serve/deployment.py#L109-L115

- Remove `docs_path` from deploymentinfo class https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/fe7c0c51d61ead4bb2939ac81a22812a538bc345/python/ray/serve/_private/deployment_info.py#L42

- In the process refactor the dependent code path and update tests

ray-project/ray#53027.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

#### 1. What happened (the bug)
```yaml
available_node_types:
  ray.worker.4090.standard:
    min_workers: 0
    max_workers: 5
    resources: {"CPU": 16, "GPU": 1, "memory": 30107260928 , "gram": 24 }
    node_config: {}

  ray.worker.4090.highmem:
    min_workers: 0
    max_workers: 5
    resources: {"CPU": 16, "GPU": 1, "memory": 62277025792 , "gram": 24}
    node_config: {}
```
When submitting a resource request that exactly matches the declared resource spec of a node type (e.g., ray.worker.4090.standard), the autoscaler does not select that node type as a launch candidate — even though:
- There is no available node of that type with free resources (available resources = 0),
- The node type config allows for more nodes (within max_worker_nodes),
- The request matches the node type’s resource spec exactly.

Instead, a different node type (e.g., ray.worker.4090.highmem) is selected and launched.

#### 2. Expected behavior

If a node type’s declared resource spec matches a resource request exactly, and the current node is fully used (zero available), then the autoscaler should:
- Still consider the same node type as a valid launch candidate,
- Attempt to create a new instance of that type,

#### 3. Useful information
https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/e91c051e5813c0a9f69d075e9cdd0d8ca8abcbd4/python/ray/autoscaler/v2/scheduler.py#L430-L445

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray-2.46.0

### Reproduction script

#### Workflow 1: One Replica - Expected Node Type Selected

```py
import ray
from ray import serve
from ray.autoscaler.sdk import request_resources

ray.init(address="auto")
serve.start()

# Deploy tasks with specific resource requirements

def CustomResourceTask(*args):
    return "ray.worker.4090.standard"

serve.run(CustomResourceTask.bind())
print("Requested additional resources...")
```

- This behavior is unexpected and could indicate a bug or scoring issue in node selection logic.

ray-project/ray#53071.body:
### Description

The [current implementation for ray.serve.batch](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/58c081a72aecc33ae31797320ab3a4e17ef02b7f/python/ray/serve/batching.py#L277) executes the batches synchronously. This throttles throughput for asynchronous methods wrapped in `ray.serve.batch`.


### Use case

This could significantly improve usability when doing I/O calls to an endpoint that expects batching. It also would increase throughput for router-style composed actor where the sub-actors by avoiding the bubble that arises from waiting for the slowest sub-actor.
ray-project/ray#53071.title: [Serve] concurrency in ray.serve.batch

ray-project/ray#53169.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Currently, when actor's go out of scope we just exit the interpreter which skips `__del__` method invocation leading to inability to do any of the clean ups in a straightforward fashion.

This is causing issues like https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/53124

### Versions / Dependencies

2.46

### Reproduction script

```python
import ray

@ray.remote
class Actor:
    def __init__(self):
        print(">>> Actor.__init__")

    def echo(self):
        print(">>> Actor.echo")

    def __del__(self):
        print(">>> Actor.__del__")


actor = Actor.remote()

ray.wait(actor.echo.remote())

# Won't invoke __del__ on the actor class which creates a gap from pure Python-class perception
del actor
```

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#53169.title: [Core] Make sure Actor's `__del__` method invoked on Actor's destruction

ray-project/ray#53226.body:
### Description

Currently, both ray.init() and ray ctl rely on the same environment variable RAY_ADDRESS to determine the default address to connect to a Ray cluster. However, in practice:

ray.init() uses the ray:// protocol and connects via the GCS gRPC port (default 10001) 
ray ctl uses the HTTP protocol (http://) and connects via the Dashboard port (default 8265)
This leads to potential confusion when setting the RAY_ADDRESS environment variable, as it may not be valid for both tools simultaneously. For example, setting RAY_ADDRESS=http://localhost:8265 would work for ray ctl, but not for ray.init(), which expects a ray:// URI with the GCS port.

Suggestion
To improve usability and reduce ambiguity, I propose that we separate the environment variables used by these two interfaces:

Use RAY_ADDRESS for ray.init() (defaulting to ray://localhost:10001)
Use RAY_DASHBOARD_ADDRESS for ray ctl (defaulting to http://localhost:8265)
This change would allow users and applications to configure connection addresses independently based on their use case, avoiding unnecessary errors and improving clarity in environments where both tools are used.

Let me know if this proposal makes sense to the team or if there are existing plans to address this inconsistency.

Thanks!

generated by Qwen

### Use case

_No response_
ray-project/ray#53226.title: [Core]Separate Environment Variables for ray.init() and ray ctl to Reflect Different Protocols and Ports

ray-project/ray#53249.body:
## Why are these changes needed?

* The on_exit hook was introduced to allow users to perform cleanup.
* However, it triggers a race condition bug in fault tolerance - after on_exit is called and the UDF is deleted, and before the actor actually exits, another retry task is submitted to the actor.
* This PR disables it by default. Eventually this should be fixed in Ray Core https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/53169
ray-project/ray#53249.title: [data] disable on_exit hook

ray-project/ray#53257.body: ### Description Prefill/Decode disaggregation is a critical feature required for large scale LLM deployments to achieve good performance and SLAs. Currently, this is not well supported. This can be broken down into a couple core requirements: - Ability to specify prefill and decode and handle KV transfer - Ability to scale prefill and decode replicas independently - Integrate with NIXL for flexible and high performance communication ### Design Sketch This design is meant to offer a ray serve solution built on top of https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/17751 ([design doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1lDQt6hUXBoMnMabsuAMZAWdqit7TzuflsFGHLt2gZus/edit?tab=t.0)) To support this in Ray Serve, we are planning to create a proxy deployment that manages request routing between prefill and decode (passing request with the correct meta data to prefill and decode). We will also create separate deployments for prefill and decoding using the existing LLMServer deployment. Everything else will be done internally via Nixl. Architecture diagram We have a prototype on https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/53092 ~that we are hoping to merge soon.~ ### API We want the end to end experience to look like something like this: ```python prefill_config = LLMConfig( model_loading_config={"model_id": "deepseek-r1"}, engine_kwargs=dict( data_parallel_size=4, tensor_parallel_size=1, enable_expert_parallel=True, ), ) decode_config = LLMConfig( model_loading_config={"model_id": "deepseek-r1"}, engine_kwargs=dict( data_parallel_size=16, tensor_parallel_size=1, enable_expert_parallel=True, ), ) prefill_server = LLMServer.bind(prefill_config) decode_server = LLMServer.bind(decode_config) pd_proxy = LLMPDProxyServer.bind(prefill_server, decode_server) app = LLMRouter.bind([pd_proxy]) serve.run(app) ``` cc @lk-chen @richardliaw
ray-project/ray#53257.title: [RFC][llm] Prefill/Decode disaggregation with Ray Serve

ray-project/ray#53295.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Create Parent/Child Deployments, manually assign both the same name, in this case `"common"`, and pass the Child as a `__init__` arg to the Parent. Serve detects a name conflict and renames one of the deployments `"common_1"`, but `serve.run(...)` incorrectly binds to the Child app even though it's called with the Parent app as an explicit argument. I'd expect the return value of `has_child` to be `True`, but it's `False`.

**repro**
```Python
from ray import serve
from ray.serve import handle

class Repro:
    def __init__(self, child: handle.DeploymentHandle | None = None) -> None:
        self._child = child

    async def has_child(self) -> bool:
        return self._child is not None

@serve.deployment(name="common")
class Parent(Repro):
    ...

@serve.deployment(name="common")
class Child(Repro):
    ...

app_dh = serve.run(Parent.bind(child=Child.bind()), blocking=False)

# call
print(f">> has child: {await app_dh.has_child.remote()}")
```

**logs**
```bash
WARNING 2025-05-24 08:54:16,712 serve 12526 -- There are multiple deployments with the same name 'common'. Renaming one to 'common_1'.
...
(ServeController pid=19136) INFO 2025-05-24 08:54:17,868 controller 19136 -- Deploying new version of Deployment(name='common', app='default') (initial target replicas: 1).
```

ray-project/ray#53297.body:
### Description

To implement `stratify` option in `Dataset.train_test_split`

Currently, the non-stratified split is implemented random shuffle + split by index. The stratified version may require a groupby + train_test_split + merge back into train/test sets. It will be very great if these can be lazy.

scikit-learn:
* https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.model_selection.train_test_split.html
* https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/cross_validation.html#stratification

### Use case

The use cases are similar to `groupby.sample` in https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/53296

There are cases we cannot train-test-split uniformly if the group distribution matters (e.g., imbalanced dataset)
ray-project/ray#53297.title: [Data] Stratification in train_test_split

ray-project/ray#53435.body:
### Summary

I noticed that `hashlib.sha1()` is used in a few parts of the codebase.

Although these uses don't appear to be security-critical, SHA-1 is a legacy hash function with known collision vulnerabilities. It's generally recommended to avoid using SHA-1 where possible, even for non-security use cases, to prevent future misuse and improve forward compatibility.

The official Python documentation also notes that SHA-1 has known weaknesses:
https://docs.python.org/3/library/hashlib.html#hashlib.sha1

Several projects (e.g., Git, Streamlit, HuggingFace) have already moved away from SHA-1 to strengthen their default hashing strategies.

---

### Recommendation

It might be worth switching to a more modern alternative like SHA-256 or blake2b, especially for use cases that involve hashing user-provided input or identifiers. This change could help reduce potential security risks down the line and improve compatibility with environments that enforce stricter cryptographic standards, such as FIPS.

I'm happy to put together a PR if this seems reasonable.

Thanks.
ray-project/ray#53435.title: Replace SHA-1 usage with safer alternatives (e.g., SHA-256)

We’re migrating internal utilities currently housed in `ray._private` to a new location: `ray._common`. ## Purpose The goal is to **isolate reusable utilities** shared across Ray Core and native libraries in a central, semantically correct location. The new `_common` directory acts as a **developer API**: - ✅ Safe for use across libraries within the Ray repo (Serve, Train, Data, etc.). - ❌ Not meant for public use or for external OSS dependencies. This migration will help reduce accidental coupling to `ray._private`, which is intended for Ray Core internals only. ## What You Can Help With We’ve compiled a list of functions and classes that are: - Frequently used across libraries and tests - Currently located in `ray._private` - Good candidates to move to `ray._common` Each function can be **migrated independently**, so feel free to pick one and open a PR! ## How to Contribute 1. Choose a function or class from the list below and claim it by leaving a comment. 2. Move it to an appropriate file inside `ray/_common/`. 3. Update all import paths and usage sites. 4. Ensure all tests pass 5. Submit a PR titled `[core] Migrate from _private to _common` Sample PRs for referance 1. https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/53457 2. https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/50609 ## Need Help? ray-project/ray#53533.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

There are several issues that are about the same thing: Our contract with vllm is from EngineClient perspective so we are missing any upstreamed feature that seat at the OpenAIChatServing layer. Historically we have recreated those logics inside LLMServer but it is hard lift to keep upgrading those logics to keep compatibility with vllm serve endpoints


Ideal state is that vllm serve xyz and serve run should be able to produce 100% compatible api servers but that is not true today. We should think of a better modularization so that we only inherit the new stuff implemented at the api server layer of the engine providers (vLLM) but also keep the independent scalability component of serve. 

The issue that we cannot inherit the api server directly is that it is tightly coupled with the engine client, so we might need to decouple them in vllm first.


Related issues: 
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/52934
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/52979
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/53324
- slack: https://anyscale-external.slack.com/archives/C08H0M37WLQ/p1748958666214349

### Versions / Dependencies

N/A

### Reproduction script

N/A

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#53533.title: [Serve][llm] Make Serve LLM endpoint 100% compatible with the engine's native server.

ray-project/ray#53597.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Ever since the end-of-life of Python 2, many Linux distributions (including Ubuntu) do not have a command by default named "python". Oftentimes python has to be invoked with "python3" since "python" is historically an alias to Python 2 on systems.

When using the ray `runtime_env` with `"nsight": "default"` the program fails if there is no existing "python" program on the machine.

On my machine I can get this to work only after I run `sudo ln -s /usr/bin/python3 /usr/bin/python`

### Versions / Dependencies

OS: Ubuntu 22.04

Python: 3.10

Ray: 2.30.0

### Reproduction script

# This is the code directly from the Ray docs

import torch
import ray

ray.init()

@ray.remote(num_gpus=1, runtime_env={ "nsight": "default"})
class RayActor:
    def run():
    a = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0]).cuda()
    b = torch.tensor([4.0, 5.0, 6.0]).cuda()
    c = a * b

    print("Result on GPU:", c)

ray_actor = RayActor.remote()
ray.get(ray_actor.run.remote())

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#53597.title: Nsight GPU profiling unable to run on systems that don't have a "python" on the PATH

ray-project/ray#53603.body:
Would it be interesting to have a CPU only mode supported? E.g., for local testing on macOS

Currently while trying Ray Serve for LLMs on macOS, I am not able to do so. Configuring resources_per_bundle as below still requires a GPU:

```python
resources_per_bundle=dict(
    CPU=1,
    GPU=0,
),
```

Which is due to the accelerators on this line, which if not set always defaults to True:

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/0690521dda6d43d0563de760b40c0182d553f0d4/python/ray/llm/_internal/serve/deployments/llm/vllm/vllm_models.py#L162

## Proposal

1. Check if `self.resources_per_bundle` is explicitely set, and return false if `GPU` is 0 

2. Allow overriding `use_gpu` in the config as below:

```python
llm_config = LLMConfig(
    model_loading_config=dict(
        model_id="qwen-0.5b", 
        model_source="Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct"
    ),
    # Disable GPU usage
    use_gpu=False,
)
```
ray-project/ray#53603.title: Allow CPU Only Run

ray-project/ray#53623.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The GPU objects POC PR (https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/52938) causes a performance degradation because it passes ObjectRef for small objects.

* Before GPU objects POC PR, `mutable_arg->clear_object_ref();` will be called. After GPU objects POC PR, the whole object ref will be transferred which slow down a very niche [test stage2 in test_many_tasks.py](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/0e53e1ddc84662adbffbddcb7e3ff5d845747838/release/nightly_tests/stress_tests/test_many_tasks.py#L77). (See https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/53575 for more details)
  * Before GPU objects POC PR: stage_2_time 168.2283821105957 seconds
  * After GPU objects POC PR: stage_2_time 287.988343000412 seconds

* Clear owner_address (https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/53590) to reduce the size of data transfer because we only need object id. 
  * `stage_2_time` = 198.5783519744873
 
Solution is to clear object ref if the object ref is not a GPU object ref.

### Versions / Dependencies

nightly

### Reproduction script

Trigger release tests with `name:stress_test_many_tasks.aws`

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#53623.title: [core][gpu-objects] Performance regression caused by transferring object references for small objects

ray-project/ray#53648.body:
### Description

Support a .rayignore file to specify files to ignore in the working directory of a runtime env.

This would be more convenient than `excludes` because its lives with the directory, rather than where the  runtime env is defined (eg: in a job or ray.remote decorator). 

It follows the standard convention set by .gitignore and .dockerignore.

### Use case

Ignore large files when uploading the working directory to the cluster.

ray-project/ray#53655.body:
### Description

Current ray's doc has a description of [how to enable autoscaler V2](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/cluster/kubernetes/user-guides/configuring-autoscaling.html#autoscaler-v2-with-kuberay). However, the `ray-cluster.autoscaler-v2.yaml` example to the `https://github.com/ray-project/kuberay/blob/master/ray-operator/config/samples/ray-cluster.autoscaler-v2.yaml` which does not have related configuration of `RAY_enable_autoscaler_v2`. This is because that [commit-a9c48c](https://github.com/ray-project/kuberay/commit/a9c48c150b3cb99aec4b29e434d7a1861c96d36a) introduced a new feature, which has not been released yet.

To reduce such problem, we should make the ray's doc bind to a specific released kuberay version.

### Link

https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/cluster/kubernetes/user-guides/configuring-autoscaling.html#autoscaler-v2-with-kuberay
ray-project/ray#53655.title: [Kuberay] The reference of Kuberay code link should bind to a release version

ray-project/ray#53677.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The method [`ray.serve._private.replica.ReplicaActor._save_cpu_profile_data`](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/serve/_private/replica.py#L1141-L1160) accesses two non-existent instance attributes, `self.cpu_profiler` and `self.cpu_profiler_log`. It seems there're a few options:
1. change these references to `self._replica_impl.cpu_profiler/cpu_profiler_log`
2. move `_save_cpu_profile_data` to `ReplicaBase`
3. deprecate and remove this entirely as it's likely not in use (because it's broken)

### Versions / Dependencies

```bash
ray==2.46.0
python==3.12
```

### Reproduction script

see above.

### Issue Severity

Low: It annoys or frustrates me.
ray-project/ray#53677.title: [Serve] Serve-native CPU profiling in Replicas is broken

ray-project/ray#53727.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Currently arguments for actor creation tasks in plasma don't have the correct `lineage_ref_count`. This means they can be evicted or the task specs needed to reconstruct them can be evicted. That means the max_restarts # on the actor will not be honored and we'll hit a `ray.exceptions.ReferenceCountingAssertionError`, when the actor tries to restart.

The solution will be to have an actor manager on the owner worker to handle actors owned by that worker. Behavior for detached actors will remain the same as it is today. Context for solution decision https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/51653#issuecomment-2960275776

Related:
https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/53713
https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/51653


### Versions / Dependencies

Any Ray version

### Reproduction script

```
cluster = Cluster()
cluster.add_node(num_cpus=0)  # head
ray.init(address=cluster.address)
worker1 = cluster.add_node(num_cpus=1)

@ray.remote(num_cpus=1, max_restarts=1)
class Actor:
    def __init__(self, config):
        self.config = config

    def ping(self):
        return self.config

# Arg is >100kb so will go in the object store
actor = Actor.remote(np.zeros(100 * 1024 * 1024, dtype=np.uint8))
ray.get(actor.ping.remote())

worker2 = cluster.add_node(num_cpus=1)
cluster.remove_node(worker1, allow_graceful=True)

# This line will break
ray.get(actor.ping.remote())
```

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#53727.title: [core] Actor restarts don't work when an actor creation arg is evicted from plasma

### Reproduction script

```
import shutil
import sys
import os
import logging

import pytest

import ray
from ray.data.llm import build_llm_processor, vLLMEngineProcessorConfig

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@ray.remote(num_gpus=1)
def delete_torch_compile_cache_on_worker(path: str = "~/.cache/vllm/torch_compile_cache"):
    """Delete torch compile cache on worker.
    Avoids AssertionError due to torch compile cache corruption
    TODO(seiji): check if this is still needed after https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/18851 is fixed
    """
    torch_compile_cache_path = os.path.expanduser(path)
    if os.path.exists(torch_compile_cache_path):
        shutil.rmtree(torch_compile_cache_path)
        logger.warning(f"Deleted torch compile cache at {torch_compile_cache_path}")


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "model_source,tp_size,pp_size,concurrency,sample_size",
    [
        # LLaVA model with TP=1, PP=1, concurrency=1
        # ("llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf", 1, 1, 1, 60),
        # Qwen2.5 VL model with TP=2, PP=1, concurrency=2
        ("mistral-community/pixtral-12b", 2, 1, 2, 60),
    ],
)
def test_vllm_vision_language_models(
    model_source, tp_size, pp_size, concurrency, sample_size
):
    """Test vLLM with vision language models using different configurations."""

    cache_dir_template = "./torch_compile_cache/${HOSTNAME:-$(hostname)}/${CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES%,*}"
    ray.get(delete_torch_compile_cache_on_worker.remote(cache_dir_template))

    # vLLM v1 does not support decoupled tokenizer,
    # but since the tokenizer is in a separate process,
    # the overhead should be moderated.
    tokenize = False
    detokenize = False

    # TODO(seiji): see if we can remove this once https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/18851 is fixed
    # Use shell variable expansion to ensure each GPU gets its own cache directory
    # Similar to: export TORCHINDUCTOR_CACHE_DIR=./torch_compile_cache/${HOSTNAME}/${dev}

    processor_config = vLLMEngineProcessorConfig(
        model_source=model_source,
        task_type="generate",
        engine_kwargs=dict(
            tensor_parallel_size=tp_size,
            pipeline_parallel_size=pp_size,
            max_model_len=4096,
            enable_chunked_prefill=True,
        ),
        apply_chat_template=True,
        tokenize=tokenize,
        detokenize=detokenize,
        batch_size=16,
        concurrency=concurrency,
        has_image=True,
    )

    processor = build_llm_processor(
        processor_config,
        preprocess=lambda row: dict(
            model=model_source,
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": "You are an assistant"},
                {
                    "role": "user",
                    "content": [
                        {
                            "type": "text",
                            "text": f"Say {row['id']} words about this image.",
                        },
                        {
                            "type": "image",
                            "image": "https://vllm-public-assets.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/vision_model_images/cherry_blossom.jpg",
                        },
                    ],
                },
            ],
            sampling_params=dict(
                temperature=0.3,
                max_tokens=50,
            ),
        ),
        postprocess=lambda row: {
            "resp": row["generated_text"],
        },
    )

    ds = ray.data.range(sample_size)
    ds = ds.map(lambda x: {"id": x["id"], "val": x["id"] + 5})
    ds = processor(ds)
    ds = ds.materialize()
    outs = ds.take_all()
    assert len(outs) == sample_size
    assert all("resp" in out for out in outs)
```

ray-project/ray#53836.body:
### What happened (the bug)

Running the official tuned PPO Atari example script (`rllib/tuned_examples/ppo/atari_ppo.py`) fails during initialization. The installation command `pip install "ray[rllib]" "gymnasium[accept-rom-license,atari]"` installs an incompatible combination of `gymnasium==1.0.0` and `ale-py`.

The training run crashes with an `IndexError: list index out of range` because the `EnvRunner` actors fail to start. The root cause, visible in the logs, is an `ImportError: cannot import name 'AutoresetMode' from 'gymnasium.vector'`. This happens because `gymnasium>=1.0.0` removed `AutoresetMode`, but the installed version of `ale-py` still requires it.

### Expected behavior

I expected the official example script to run successfully out-of-the-box after following the documented installation steps for RLlib and Atari environments.

### Useful information (logs)

The top-level error is an `IndexError`:
```
ray.exceptions.ActorDiedError: The actor died because of an error raised in its creation task, ray::PPO.__init__()
...
IndexError: list index out of range
```

The underlying root cause from the actor logs is an `ImportError`:
```
(PPO pid=8491) ImportError: cannot import name 'AutoresetMode' from 'gymnasium.vector' (/home/jovyan/.local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/gymnasium/vector/__init__.py))
```

```text
Defaulting to user installation because normal site-packages is not writeable

Requirement already satisfied: torch in /usr/local/lib/python3.11/dist-packages (2.0.1)
Requirement already satisfied: gymnasium[accept-rom-license,atari,mujoco] in ./.local/lib/python3.11/site-packages (1.0.0)
...
WARNING: gymnasium 1.0.0 does not provide the extra 'accept-rom-license'
...
Requirement already satisfied: ale-py>=0.9 in ./.local/lib/python3.11/site-packages (from gymnasium[accept-rom-license,atari,mujoco]) (0.11.1)
...
2025-06-15 15:00:08,012 ERROR tune_controller.py:1331 -- Trial task failed for trial PPO_env_5f694_00000
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/jovyan/.local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/air/execution/_internal/event_manager.py", line 110, in resolve_future

IndexError: list index out of range

...
(PPO pid=8491) 2025-06-15 15:00:08,004  ERROR actor_manager.py:873 -- Ray error (The actor died because of an error raised in its creation task, ray::SingleAgentEnvRunner.__init__() ...
(PPO pid=8491)   File "/home/jovyan/.local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ale_py/vector_env.py", line 14, in <module>
(PPO pid=8491)     from gymnasium.vector import AutoresetMode, VectorEnv
(PPO pid=8491) ImportError: cannot import name 'AutoresetMode' from 'gymnasium.vector' (/home/jovyan/.local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/gymnasium/vector/__init__.py)), taking actor 1 out of service.
```

### Versions / Dependencies


- **Ray Version:** `ray[rllib]==2.47.0`
- **Python Version:** `3.11`
- **OS:** Juypter Notebook
- **Other Dependencies:**
  - `gymnasium==1.0.0`
  - `ale-py==0.11.1`
  - `torch==2.0.1`

### Reproduction script

1. **Clone the Ray repository:**
   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/ray-project/ray.git
   cd ray
   ```

2. **Create a clean environment and install dependencies:**
   ```bash
   python3 -m venv venv
   source venv/bin/activate
   pip install "ray[rllib]" "gymnasium[accept-rom-license,atari]"
   ```

3. **Run the official PPO Atari example script:**
   ```bash
   python rllib/tuned_examples/ppo/atari_ppo.py
   ```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#53836.title: [rllib] [bug] Official PPO Atari example fails with IndexError

ray-project/ray#53841.body:
### Description

Currently, it says:

```
 raise ImportError(
                "Could not import pyspiel! Pygame is not a dependency of RLlib "
                "and RLlib requires you to install pygame separately: "
                "`pip install pygame`."
            )
```

This appears to be a copy-paste error. It should say:

```
 raise ImportError(
                "Could not import pyspiel! Pyspiel is not a dependency of RLlib "
                "and RLlib requires you to install pyspiel separately: "
                "`pip install open_spiel`."
            )
```

### Versions / Dependencies

Tested on ray 3.0.0.dev0 and 2.47.0, python 3.9 and 3.10

```python
from ray import ActorID
ActorId.nil().job_id
```

### What happened + What you expected to happen

I tried serving a few mistral models like https://huggingface.co/mistralai/Devstral-Small-2505 or https://huggingface.co/mistralai/Mistral-Small-3.1-24B-Base-2503. I've install are necessary dependencies (vllm==0.8.5.post1) but serving still wasn't working. From stack trace looks like it's from https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/llm/_internal/serve/deployments/utils/node_initialization_utils.py#L151-L154 where we are trying to load the tokenizer. Could be because mistral models requires different tokenizer. 

The suggestion vllm args are:
```
vllm serve mistralai/Devstral-Small-2505 --tokenizer_mode mistral --config_format mistral --load_format mistral --tool-call-parser mistral --enable-auto-tool-choice --tensor-parallel-size 2
```

ray-project/ray#53881.body:
Pylint scan found some Python code defects in ray data, such as:

ray/python/data/datasource/datasink.py:13:0: C0103: Type variable name "WriteReturnType" doesn't conform to predefined naming style (invalid-name)
ray/python/ray/data/datasource/file_based_datasource.py:276:20: R1730: Consider using 'num_threads = min(num_threads, len(read_paths))' instead of unnecessary if block (consider-using-min-builtin)
ray/python/ray/data/read_api.py:3214:4: R1705: Unnecessary "elif" after "return", remove the leading "el" from "elif" (no-else-return)
/python/ray/data/dataset.py:845:20: R1701: Consider merging these isinstance calls to isinstance(column, (pd.DataFrame, pd.Index, pd.Series)) (consider-merging-isinstance)
ray/python/ray/data/dataset.py:867:16: R1705: Unnecessary "else" after "return", remove the "else" and de-indent the code inside it (no-else-return)

   All these issues do not affect the logic of program execution, but we can fix these defects, improve code readability, reduce loop load, or remove useless code, and so on.
   For example, the corresponding source code for ray/python/ray/data/dataset.py: 845:20 above is:
“if (
isinstance(column, pd.Series)
or isinstance(column, pd.DataFrame)
or isinstance(column, pd.Index)
):
”
We can completely merge the above three judgments into one isinstance (column, (pd. DataFrame, pd. Index, pd. Series)) without affecting the logic, and the code is more concise.
ray-project/ray#53881.title: [Data]Pylint detection found some Python code defects in ray data
