ray-project/ray#54007.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

In actor tasks, the completion of ray.get by any thread can cause all pull requests generated by ray.get in the process (in the pull manager) to be canceled. At this point, other gets will retrigger pull requests, and the progress of the previously pulled objects will be completely reset to zero. This can cause gets to hang directly under the following circumstances.

### Versions / Dependencies

ray master: 385e000b8d10de6d1ccc0111621cb1bce6e74f6a

### Reproduction script

- test.py:
``` python
from threading import Thread
import ray; ray.init("auto")

@ray.remote(resources={"worker": 0.1})
class A:
    def run(self, refs):
        ref_largs = refs[0]
        ref_small = ray.put("1")
        def get_small():
            while True:
                ray.get(ref_small)
                import time
                time.sleep(0.1)
        t = Thread(target=get_small, daemon=True)
        t.start()
        print("start get")
        ray.get(ref_largs)
        print("done.")


@ray.remote(resources={"head": 0.1})
def main():
    refs = [ray.put(b"0" * 10 * 1024 ** 3)]
    a = A.remote()

    ray.get(a.run.remote(refs))

ray.get(main.remote())
```
- run:
-  step1: export RAY_object_manager_max_bytes_in_flight=12000000
- step2: ray start --head --resources '{"head": 10}'    
- step3: ray start --address='$YOUR_IP:6379' --resources '{"worker": 10}'
- step4: python test.py
will run forever....

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#54007.title: [Core] Multi-threaded ray.get can hang in certain situations.

ray-project/ray#54098.body:
### Description

Allow [parameterized queries](https://peps.python.org/pep-0249/#id20) when using `read_sql` to construct a `Dataset`.

### Use case

Currently, the query string passed to `read_sql` must be fully formed and not contain any parameter bindings. This limits its applicability both in dev (need to use raw python string formatting for parameters, opportunities for mistakes) and in prod (SQL injection). It should be possible to use a parameterized query string and pass a dict/list of parameters alongside the query.
ray-project/ray#54098.title: [Data] Allow parameterized queries in `read_sql`

ray-project/ray#54134.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

When using the `uv` backend of ray to install packages for my job, with a parent virtual environment that I created with `uv` I get the exception `No module named pip` when ray tries to install `uv`.

The issue is that the `_install_uv_packages()` method has a bug:

- The call in  [_install_uv_packages()](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/5f720f7517894e84d20ce39e3d453a5f2e724d4e/python/ray/_private/runtime_env/uv.py#L148-L154) to the function `_check_uv_existence()` passes the path to the python binary
- However, the code in [_check_uv_existence](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/5f720f7517894e84d20ce39e3d453a5f2e724d4e/python/ray/_private/runtime_env/uv.py#L109-L116) expects the path to the virtual environment. In turn, `_check_uv_existence()` appends a suffix to the path (e.g. "/bin/python" on a mac/linux) which is causing the followup check for the existence of `uv` to fail.

Next, the uv backed of ray tries to install `uv` using `python -m pip`. However in a typical uv venv there is no pip module. The result is that ray runs into a problem setting up  the runtime virtual environment.

The fix is for `_install_uv_packages()` to invoke `_check_uv_existence()` using the environment path, instead of the path to the python executable.

### Versions / Dependencies

Latest commit in master branch of ray, as well as ray==2.47.1

### Reproduction script

In a terminal build a virtual environment with venv and use it to start a local Ray Cluster: 
- uv venv /tmp/my-venv
- . /tmp/my-venv/bin/activate
- uv pip install "ray[default]"
- ray start --head

Now create a minimal ray_runtime.yaml file which uses uv to install any python package and use it to start any job. For example:

> Contents of ray_runtime.yaml

```yaml
uv:
  packages:
  - aim
```

```
ray job submit --runtime-env ray_runtime.yaml echo hi
```


You will get this exception:

```
Status message: runtime_env setup failed: Failed to set up runtime environment.
Could not create the actor because its associated runtime env failed to be created.
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/private/tmp/my-venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/runtime_env/agent/runtime_env_agent.py", line 388, in _create_runtime_env_with_retry
    runtime_env_context = await asyncio.wait_for(
                          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/opt/homebrew/Cellar/python@3.11/3.11.12/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.11/lib/python3.11/asyncio/tasks.py", line 489, in wait_for
    return fut.result()
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/private/tmp/my-venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/runtime_env/agent/runtime_env_agent.py", line 346, in _setup_runtime_env
    await create_for_plugin_if_needed(
  File "/private/tmp/my-venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/runtime_env/plugin.py", line 255, in create_for_plugin_if_needed
    size_bytes = await plugin.create(uri, runtime_env, context, logger=logger)
                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/private/tmp/my-venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/runtime_env/uv.py", line 316, in create
    uv_dir_bytes = await task
                   ^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/private/tmp/my-venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/runtime_env/uv.py", line 296, in _create_for_hash
    await UvProcessor(
  File "/private/tmp/my-venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/runtime_env/uv.py", line 206, in _run
    await self._install_uv_packages(
  File "/private/tmp/my-venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/runtime_env/uv.py", line 157, in _install_uv_packages
    await self._install_uv(path, cwd, pip_env, logger)
  File "/private/tmp/my-venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/runtime_env/uv.py", line 102, in _install_uv
    await check_output_cmd(uv_install_cmd, logger=logger, cwd=cwd, env=pip_env)
  File "/private/tmp/my-venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/runtime_env/utils.py", line 105, in check_output_cmd
    raise SubprocessCalledProcessError(
ray._private.runtime_env.utils.SubprocessCalledProcessError: Run cmd[12] failed with the following details.
Command '['/tmp/ray/session_2025-06-26_09-43-12_595415_43417/runtime_resources/uv/28736e23cd90921c407f2f8d543c22f0cdc4c226/virtualenv/bin/python', '-m', 'pip', 'install', '--disable-pip-version-check', '--no-cache-dir', 'uv']' returned non-zero exit status 1.
Last 50 lines of stdout:
    /private/tmp/ray/session_2025-06-26_09-43-12_595415_43417/runtime_resources/uv/28736e23cd90921c407f2f8d543c22f0cdc4c226/virtualenv/bin/python: No module named pip
```

However, this is a red herring. The actual problem is inside the call to `_check_uv_existence`

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#54134.title: [Core] bug in _install_uv_packages() method of uv runtime backend breaks installing packages in ray runtimes

### What happened + What you expected to happen

I would expect this function called with .map to succeed even if it returns variable shaped numpy arrays for the array key. The xarray.Dataset is sliced and a subset of it is converted to a numpy array with `.values`

```python
def create_random_data_from_shape(item: dict[str, dict[str, tuple[int, int]]]) -> dict[str, Any]:
    # Extract dimension ranges from the item
    time_range = item["time"]
    band_range = item["band"]
    y_range = item["y"]
    x_range = item["x"]

    # Calculate actual dimensions from ranges
    time_size = time_range[1] - time_range[0]
    band_size = band_range[1] - band_range[0]
    y_size = y_range[1] - y_range[0]
    x_size = x_range[1] - x_range[0]

    # Create the array shape (T, C, H, W)
    shape = (time_size, band_size, y_size, x_size)

    # Create random data with the calculated shape
    data = np.random.rand(*shape).astype(np.float32)
    return {
        "data": data,
        "shape": {"time": time_range, "band": band_range, "y": y_range, "x": x_range},
    }
```

ray-project/ray#54201.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The following code will hang indefinitely

```python
from ray import serve
import asyncio


@serve.deployment
class DeploymentA:
    async def __call__(self, number: int) -> int:
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
        return number * 2


@serve.deployment
class DeploymentB:
    async def __call__(self, number: int) -> int:
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
        return number * 3


@serve.deployment
class DeploymentC:
    async def __call__(self, number: int) -> int:
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
        return number * 4


@serve.deployment
class ComposedDeployment:
    def __init__(self, deployment_a, deployment_b, deployment_c):
        self.deployment_a = deployment_a
        self.deployment_b = deployment_b
        self.deployment_c = deployment_c

    async def __call__(self, number: int) -> int:
        a = self.deployment_a.remote(number)
        b = self.deployment_b.remote(a)
        c = self.deployment_c.remote(b)

        true_b = await b
        true_c = await c
        return true_b * true_c


composed_deployment = ComposedDeployment.bind(
    DeploymentA.bind(), DeploymentB.bind(), DeploymentC.bind()
)

handle = serve.run(composed_deployment)

print("Deployment done")
response = handle.remote(5)
print(response.result())
```

Changing the order of the await will solve the problem (await c before await b)
```python
async def __call__(self, number: int) -> int:
        a = self.deployment_a.remote(number)
        b = self.deployment_b.remote(a)
        c = self.deployment_c.remote(b)

        true_c = await c
        true_b = await b
        return true_b * true_c
```

### Versions / Dependencies

2.47.1

### Reproduction script

Run the above code

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#54201.title: [Serve] Deadlock when awaiting DeploymentResponse

ray-project/ray#54241.body:
### Problem Description

When the Dashboard starts, it retrieves the list of all nodes from the GCS (Global Control Service) and updates node information via the `_update_node` method. However, the logic for updating the `head_node_id` in Redis's KV storage within this method has specific constraints: the update operation is only executed if the currently processed node is a head node **and** the `head_node_register_time` (registration time of the head node) has not been initialized. If the node is not a head node or if `head_node_register_time` has already been initialized, the method skips the node without updating the `head_node_id`.

The root cause of this critical issue is that the node list retrieved from GCS is unordered. During the initial processing of node information, there is no guarantee that the first head node encountered is still alive. If this head node is already inactive (e.g., crashed), the `head_node_register_time` will still be initialized. Subsequently, the `_update_node` method will ignore all subsequent nodes. As a result, when submitting tasks, the invalid `HEAD_NODE_ID` stored in Redis continues to be used, leading to task submission failures.


Error Logs During Job Submission

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/33a39ffc-1db1-49d0-aca1-8416fd943e45)


Dashborad Logs

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/58053920-f8d0-4055-a624-6494e01d5ad6)

Cluster Node Status

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/22d7f1ca-77c5-41d3-849b-3ab5a4e15208)

From the Dashboard logs, the currently active node ID is **70c111443a292aa19fcdfee67d36386a078b38a7879e3a169dd2fa9e**, but the system attempts to submit tasks to the deceased head node **49f0bacf0a574f22fc386938999dcccd0b4117750d1778a701f9a8ad**. This occurs because the `head_node_id` retrieved from the GCS KV storage via the `gcs_client` is incorrect.


Redis Query Results

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9cdf3b0a-384c-4d92-bd5d-09291f9da441)

The head\_node\_id stored in GCS still refers to the old, deceased head node.

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.44.0 and older releases.

### Reproduction script

None

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#54241.title: [Core] Tasks cannot be submitted after unexpected restart of the head node with Redis enabled

ray-project/ray#54251.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Hello ray team,

as adviced by @ArturNiederfahrenhorst, I am opening this issue here. Basically, we are coming from [this post](https://discuss.ray.io/t/rllib-tune-air-checkpointing-as-per-custom-metric-minimum/20534) on ray discussion forum.

What I expected?
- Minimum expectation: I get a Tune checkpoint for PPO training at every time when a custom metric value reaches a new minimum value.
- Best expectation: I can use `num_to_keep` to steer how many checkpoints as per the scoring are kept on disk.

What happened?
- Good: The logs tell me that a checkpoint was created at the end of the trial.
- Bad: There have been no intermediate checkpoints created for iterations wherein custom metric value had been 0. Considering one trial, the minimum reached values 0,1,3. I would have expected checkpoint at the last iteration in which the custom metric value was 0.



### Versions / Dependencies

ray==2.20
torch==2.3.1+cu118
gymnasium==0.28.1

### Reproduction script

```
"""Reproduction of issue #XXX in Ray RLlib."""

import numpy as np
import ray
from ray import air
from ray import tune
from ray.rllib.algorithms.callbacks import DefaultCallbacks
from ray.rllib.algorithms.ppo import PPOConfig
from ray.tune.schedulers import ASHAScheduler
from ray.tune.stopper import MaximumIterationStopper


class MyCallback(DefaultCallbacks):
    def on_episode_end(self, worker, base_env, policies, episode, env_index: int):
        """Conduct tasks at end of each episode."""
        # For the reproduction case, we use a random scalar metric.
        episode.custom_metrics["scalarmetric"] = np.random.Generator.integers(
            low=0, high=100
        )


config = (
    PPOConfig()
    .experimental(_enable_new_api_stack=False)
    .environment("CartPole-v1")  # Use a simple environment for demonstration.
    .callbacks(MyCallback)  # Use the callback which records the custom scalar metric.
    .framework("torch")
    .rollouts(num_rollout_workers=2)
    .resources(
        num_cpus_per_worker=2,
        num_gpus_per_worker=0.2,
    )
)

scheduler = ASHAScheduler(
    metric="episode_reward_max",  # The metric to optimize
    mode="max",  # Maximize the reward
    max_t=20,  # Max number of training iterations
    grace_period=10,  # Minimum number of iterations before stopping
    reduction_factor=2,  # Scaling factor for trials
)

# Define the Tuner object.
tuner = tune.Tuner(
    "PPO",
    param_space=config,
    run_config=air.RunConfig(
        name="checkpoint-new-best-test",  # All data are available in ~/ray_results/$name$
        stop=MaximumIterationStopper(max_iter=20),  # Stop after 20 iterations.
        checkpoint_config=air.CheckpointConfig(
            checkpoint_score_attribute="custom_metrics/scalarmetric_min",
            checkpoint_score_order="min",
            checkpoint_at_end=True,  # Save the final checkpoint at the end of training, required for potential resuming.
        ),
        log_to_file=True,
    ),
    tune_config=tune.TuneConfig(
        num_samples=3,
        scheduler=scheduler,
        trial_dirname_creator=lambda trial: f"{trial.trainable_name}_{trial.trial_id}",
    ),
)

results = tuner.fit()

ray.shutdown()

```

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#54251.title: [RLlib,Tune,AIR] Checkpointing scoring per custom metric does not work

### Reproduction script

```python
import pathlib

import pandas as pd
import pyarrow as pa
import ray
import ray.data
from pyiceberg.catalog.sql import SqlCatalog
from pyiceberg.exceptions import NamespaceAlreadyExistsError, TableAlreadyExistsError
from ray.data import Dataset, read_iceberg

ray.init()


parent_path = pathlib.Path(__file__).parent


def pivot_batch(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    pivoted = df.pivot(index="index", columns="index_2", values="value")
    pivoted.reset_index(inplace=True)
    return pivoted


df = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "index": [1, 1, 2, 2],
        "index_2": ["a", "b", "a", "b"],
        "value": [10, 20, 30, 40],
    }
)

df.to_parquet("test_data.parquet")

schema = pa.schema(
    [
        pa.field("index", pa.int64()),
        pa.field("index_2", pa.string()),
        pa.field("value", pa.int64()),
    ]
)


catalog = SqlCatalog(
    name="local",
    uri="sqlite:///" + str(parent_path / "pyiceberg.db"),
    warehouse="file://" + str(parent_path / "warehouse"),
)

run_id = 1

namespace = "testing"
table_id = f"{namespace}.pivot_testing_{run_id}"

try:
    catalog.create_namespace(namespace)
except NamespaceAlreadyExistsError:
    pass

try:
    catalog.create_table(identifier=table_id, schema=schema)
except TableAlreadyExistsError:
    pass

table = catalog.load_table(table_id)

table.delete()

pa_table = pa.table(df, schema=schema)
table.append(df=pa_table)


# This Runs Fine
# From In-Memory Dataframe
ds = ray.data.from_pandas(df)
ds_pivoted = ds.groupby("index").map_groups(pivot_batch, batch_format="pandas")
print("Pivoted DataFrame from In-Memory DataFrame:")
print(ds_pivoted.to_pandas())
print("Successfully pivoted in-memory DataFrame!")

# test on parquet file
ds_parquet = ray.data.read_parquet("test_data.parquet")
ds_pivoted_parquet = ds_parquet.groupby("index").map_groups(pivot_batch, batch_format="pandas")
print("Pivoted DataFrame from Parquet File:")
print(ds_pivoted_parquet.to_pandas())
print("Successfully pivoted Parquet file!")


ds_catalog = read_iceberg(
    table_identifier=table_id,
    catalog_kwargs={"name": "local", **catalog.properties},
)


# Evaluate non-groupby UDF on Iceberg Catalog dataset
def add_constant_column(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df["constant"] = 42
    return df


ds_transformed_catalog = ds_catalog.map_batches(add_constant_column, batch_format="pandas")
print("Transformed DataFrame with non-groupby UDF from Iceberg Catalog:")
print(ds_transformed_catalog.to_pandas())
print("Successfully applied non-groupby UDF on Iceberg Catalog!")


# This Fails
ds_pivoted_catalog = ds_catalog.groupby("index").map_groups(pivot_batch, batch_format="pandas")
print("Pivoted DataFrame from Iceberg Catalog:")
print(ds_pivoted_catalog.to_pandas())
print("Successfully pivoted Iceberg Catalog!")
```

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#54280.title: [data -- read_iceberg] pickling error on UDF for dataset.groupby.map_batches

ray-project/ray#54281.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

In https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/53935, if the argument is not inlined, `tensor_transport` is hardcoded to `None`, so the default value `OBJECT_STORE` will be used.

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/5b47378acb28806e51a79cb2c4d7fd99ea59e325/python/ray/_raylet.pyx#L606

### Versions / Dependencies

master branch

### Reproduction script

```python
def test_mix_cpu_gpu_data(ray_start_regular):
    world_size = 2
    actors = [GPUTestActor.remote() for _ in range(world_size)]
    create_collective_group(actors, backend="torch_gloo")

    tensor = torch.randn((1,))
    cpu_data = b"1" * 1000 * 1000
    data = [tensor, cpu_data]

    sender, receiver = actors[0], actors[1]
    ref = sender.echo.remote(data)
    ref = receiver.double.remote(ref)
    result = ray.get(ref)

    assert result[0] == pytest.approx(tensor * 2)
    assert result[1] == cpu_data * 2
```

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#54281.title: [core][gpu-objects] tensor_transport doesn’t transfer correctly when the argument is not inlined

ray-project/ray#54284.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Hello Ray team,

I am opening this issue here as told by @Lars_Simon_Zehnder in one of the links below. And, I am are coming from [this post](https://discuss.ray.io/t/unexpected-keyerror-while-training-sac/22770/1) on ray discussion forum. Multiple posts report the similar issue, below are the links

**Ray Discussion Links:**
1. [Discussion-1](https://discuss.ray.io/t/per-buffer-throws-keyerror-during-training-of-sac/22240/6)
2. [Discussion-2](https://discuss.ray.io/t/unexpected-keyerror-while-training-sac/22770/1)
3. [Discussion-3](https://discuss.ray.io/t/error-occuring-repeatedly-during-training-at-random-time-steps/21144/3)


### Issue Details: 

**What I expected?**

I am running the below setup for 100k steps across 1000 iterations. It does successfully run for a few iterations every time. Below is the code snippet.

**What happened?**

But almost every time after reaching nearly 40k-50k steps it results in a key error. And the key error is always the same number: 2097151, even across all the posts/discussions linked above. Below is the entire error trace.


### Versions / Dependencies

- Ray version: 2.47.1
- Python version: 3.11.11
- OS: Mac Sequoia 15.5

### Reproduction script

**Config:**
``` 
SACConfig()
        .environment(
            env='custom_env',
            env_config=env_config
        )
        .training(
            actor_lr=7e-5,
            critic_lr=2e-5,
            alpha_lr=2e-4,
            initial_alpha=0.1,
            target_entropy="auto",
            n_step=1,
            tau=0.005,
            train_batch_size_per_learner=512,
            target_network_update_freq=1,
            num_steps_sampled_before_learning_starts=3000,
            grad_clip=1.0
        )
        .learners(
            num_learners=1,
            num_cpus_per_learner=7
        )
        .rl_module(
            model_config=DefaultModelConfig(
                fcnet_hiddens=[360, 250, 180],
                fcnet_activation="relu",
                fcnet_kernel_initializer=nn.init.xavier_uniform_,
                fcnet_kernel_initializer_kwargs={"gain": 1.0},
                head_fcnet_hiddens=[100, 30],
                head_fcnet_activation='relu',
                head_fcnet_kernel_initializer=nn.init.xavier_uniform_,
                head_fcnet_kernel_initializer_kwargs={"gain": 0.01}
                )
        )
        .evaluation(
            evaluation_interval=3,
            evaluation_num_env_runners=1,
            evaluation_duration=5,
            evaluation_parallel_to_training=True,
        )
        .reporting(
            metrics_num_episodes_for_smoothing=5,
            min_sample_timesteps_per_iteration=200
        )
        .api_stack(
            enable_rl_module_and_learner=True, 
            enable_env_runner_and_connector_v2=True
        )
```

**Trace:**

```
File "code/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/tune/trainable/trainable.py", line 330, in train
    raise skipped from exception_cause(skipped)
  File "/code/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/tune/trainable/trainable.py", line 327, in train
    result = self.step()
             ^^^^^^^^^^^
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/code/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/rllib/algorithms/algorithm.py", line 1035, in step
    train_results, train_iter_ctx = self._run_one_training_iteration()
                                    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/code/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/rllib/algorithms/algorithm.py", line 3309, in _run_one_training_iteration
    training_step_return_value = self.training_step()
                                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/code/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/rllib/algorithms/dqn/dqn.py", line 644, in training_step
    return self._training_step_new_api_stack()
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/code/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/rllib/algorithms/dqn/dqn.py", line 686, in _training_step_new_api_stack
    episodes = self.local_replay_buffer.sample(
               ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/code/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/rllib/utils/replay_buffers/prioritized_episode_buffer.py", line 477, in sample
    index_triple = self._indices[self._tree_idx_to_sample_idx[idx]]
                                 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^
KeyError: 2097151
```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.

ray-project/ray#54364.body: ### What happened + What you expected to happen The issue is when we have a stage with vLLM engine and oom killer kicks in, the process associated with the killed process is not killed. This is separate from this issue https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/53124 that was mitigated by using ray as the distributed executor backend. This issue is there regardless of whether we use distributed_exector_backend = ray or mp. In case of using ray, the respawning may request a new gpu in a multi gpu cluster but the old process still lingers on its gpu. ### Versions / Dependencies N/A ### Reproduction script ```python import ray from vllm import LLM class UDF: def __init__(self): self.memory = [] self.llm = LLM( model="unsloth/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct", enforce_eager=True, # If it's MP it the zombie process and the new process will collide on the same GPU. # If it's ray it can choose another gpu and not collide but eventually it can hit the same GPU. distributed_executor_backend="ray", ) def __call__(self, batch): # ~400x4 MB of data per batch GIANT_OBJECT = "🤗" * 400_000_000 self.memory.append(GIANT_OBJECT) return batch ds = ray.data.range(2000) ds = ds.map_batches(UDF, batch_size=2, concurrency=1, num_gpus=1) ds = ds.materialize() print(ds.take_all()) ``` In this repro, I have a UDF that instantiates an LLM engine inside. In its call function I am creating a 400MB object and append it to the memory state effectively increasing the heap of the udf to trigger cpu oom. When CPU OOM killer kicks in and restarts the actor the gpu that was occupied by the previous dead process remains occupied. I ran this on 4xL40S with 380GB of VRAM. ### Issue Severity Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#54364.title: [data] OOM killer kicks in but vLLM gpu processes are not cleaned up

### What happened + What you expected to happen

When querying a databricks UC table that returns 0 rows, the user will see an error message that looks like this:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):

    cfg, datasets = get_datasets(cfg)

  File "/tmp/ray/session_2025-07-07_15-15-03_966921_8/runtime_resources/working_dir_files/_ray_pkg_ba9175dfaa5d56d6/hinge_deeplike/preprocessing.py", line 180, in get_datasets
    user_ids, cfg = load_unique_user_ids(cfg)
                    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/tmp/ray/session_2025-07-07_15-15-03_966921_8/runtime_resources/working_dir_files/_ray_pkg_ba9175dfaa5d56d6/hinge_deeplike/preprocessing.py", line 34, in load_unique_user_ids
    user_ids = load_user_ids(**cfg.load_user_id_kwargs)

    databricks_source = DatabricksUCDatasource(**datasource_kwargs)

KeyError: 'chunks'
```

That points us [right here](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/ce102a03c574e721644773c28c82c6845aab20a5/python/ray/data/_internal/datasource/databricks_uc_datasource.py#L110) where we attempt to get the `"chunks"` key from `manifest`. 

Depending on the desired user experience for this scenario, we could simply wrap that line in a try/except and log a slightly more informative error if chunks doesn't exist, indicating that the query returned 0 rows. Alternatively we could return an empty datasource.

ray-project/ray#54372.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

We have come across a behavior on a Ray + KubeRay cluster where Ray schedules a remote function call, with a reference to a sizeable object as an argument, one task at a time while other tasks are waiting for scheduling even though worker nodes have already been allocated. The tasks are set up to consume most of the resources of each node, so that running multiple tasks in parallel would necessitate running across several nodes. We expect Ray to transfer the object to the other nodes and schedule the tasks there, but it seems data locality is taking precedence.

More specifically:
- Pulling a 25GB polars df from s3 and storing the ref
- Passing the ref to `[myfunc_w_ref.options(num_cpus=100).remote(ref) for _ in range(10)]` (purposely setting hints high to force 1 task per worker)
- Ray only schedules 1 `myfunc_w_ref task` at a time while the remaining 9 are waiting for scheduling even though worker nodes have already been allocated
If we do the same thing but call some `myfunc_simple` that doesn't take ref to the object then all 10 tasks get scheduled right away. 

Code to reproduce (further below) for the following steps:
1. Setting up
2. Generate and write a large dataframe to S3
3. Get reference to dataframe in objectstore
4. Attempt to process remote file in parallel with 10 tasks
Expected: All 10 tasks should be scheduled in parallel across available workers
Actual: Only 1 task runs at a time, with the remaining 9 waiting for scheduling



### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.43.0
KubeRay v0.6.0

### Reproduction script

1. Setting up
```
import os
import numpy as np
import polars as pl
import ray
import time
RAY_ADDRESS = "ray://******:80"
AUTH_TOKEN = "******"
ray_config = {
    'address'         : f"{RAY_ADDRESS}",
    '_metadata'       : [("authorization", f"Basic {AUTH_TOKEN}")],
    'runtime_env'     : {
        "env_vars": {
            "AWS_PROFILE": "s3_user",
            "BUCKET_NAME": "some-bucket-name",
            "AWS_CONFIG_FILE": "./aws_credentials.ini",
            "AWS_SHARED_CREDENTIALS_FILE": "./aws_credentials.ini",
        },
    }
}
# Initialize Ray.
ray_context = ray.init(**ray_config)
```
2. Generate and write a large dataframe to S3
```
@ray.remote
def gen_large_df():
    LENGTH = 35_000_000
    df_tmp = pl.DataFrame(np.random.randn(10_000, 100)).lazy()
    table = pl.concat((df_tmp,)* (LENGTH // 10_000)).collect()
    size = table.estimated_size("gb")
    print("Estimated size of the DataFrame:", size)
    return table.to_arrow()

@ray.remote
def polars_write_to_object_store(df, key):
    bucket = os.environ['BUCKET_NAME']
    s3_path = f"s3://{bucket}/{key}"
    df = pl.from_arrow(df)
    df.write_parquet(s3_path)
    print(f"DataFrame written to {s3_path}")
    return s3_path

# create large dummy df
table = gen_large_df.remote()
# upload to s3/object store as parquet file
my_file_path = ray.get(polars_write_to_object_store.remote(table,"foo_large.pq"))
```
3. Get reference to dataframe in objectstore
```
@ray.remote
def read_pl_df_from_object_store(s3_path):
    df = pl.read_parquet(s3_path)
    print(f"DataFrame read from {s3_path}, shape: {df.shape}")
    return df.to_arrow()
# download from s3/object store
reference = read_pl_df_from_object_store.remote(my_file_path)
```
4. Attempt to process remote file in parallel (only 1 task runs at a time)
```
@ray.remote
def remote_call(table):
    print("Starting remote call...Running long computation")
    print(type(table))
    print(table)
    time.sleep(2.5 * 60)  # Simulate a long computation
    np.random.randn(1_000_000, 100) # doing something to ensure some computation is done
    table = pl.from_arrow(table).head().with_columns(hello=pl.lit("hello"))
    size = table.estimated_size("gb")
    print("updated DataFrame size:", size)
    return table

# process the downloaded file in parallel
result = [remote_call.options(num_cpus= 100, memory=1024**3 * 100).remote(reference) for _ in range(10)]
result = [ray.get(r) for r in result]
```
We have the following autoscaler settings:
```
 enableInTreeAutoscaling: true
  autoscalerOptions:
    env:
    - name: TZ
      value: America/New_York
    # autoscale 1 container first, then autoscale the rest of the containers, per multi-tenancy
    upscalingMode: Conservative
    # idleTimeoutSeconds: 60
    # autoscaler sidecar image, that matches the ray head image ray version, and ray autoscaler
    image: "{{ artifactory }}/rayproject/ray:{{ ray_version }}-py39-gpu"
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
    resources:
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "512Mi"
      requests:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "512Mi"
```

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#54372.title: [Core|Autoscaler|Kuberay] Cannot parallelize tasks across multiple nodes that depend on a large input arrow table

### What happened + What you expected to happen

Ray Tune code raises an exception when handling results from runs using TorchTrainer and which have raised an exception themselves.

The issue is easily reproducible by running the reproduction script below while setting the environment variable `RAY_TRAIN_V2_ENABLED=1`. This causes the following exception:

```
    return tuner.fit()
           ^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/tune/tuner.py", line 345, in fit

           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/tune/impl/tuner_internal.py", line 512, in fit

           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/tune/result_grid.py", line 80, in __init__
    self._trial_to_result(trial) for trial in self._experiment_analysis.trials
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/tune/result_grid.py", line 272, in _trial_to_result
    error=self._populate_exception(trial),
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/tune/result_grid.py", line 254, in _populate_exception
    return trial.get_pickled_error() or trial.get_error()
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/tune/experiment/trial.py", line 773, in get_pickled_error
    return cloudpickle.loads(f.readall())
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
TypeError: RayTaskError._make_normal_dual_exception_instance.<locals>.cls.__init__() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given
```

The exception does not happen when setting `RAY_TRAIN_V2_ENABLED=0` (which causes the Train V1 code path to be taken in the reproduction script, passing the `TorchTrainer` directly to `ray.tune.Tuner` instead of a function.

This is a blocking issue for me for hyperparameter tuning with the train V2 API enabled, since it prevents me from inspecting the trial results.

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray: 2.47.1
Python: 3.12.11

### Reproduction script
```python
import os
import ray.train
import ray.tune
import ray.train.torch


def train_worker_fn(config):
    raise ValueError("Blip.")


def train_fn(config):
    print(f"Running trial {ray.tune.get_context().get_trial_id()}")

    trainer = ray.train.torch.TorchTrainer(
        train_worker_fn,
        train_loop_config=config,
    )
    trainer.fit()


def main_v2():
    tuner = ray.tune.Tuner(
        train_fn,
        param_space={'p': 1}
    )
    return tuner.fit()


def main_v1():
    trainer = ray.train.torch.TorchTrainer(
        train_worker_fn,
    )
    tuner = ray.tune.Tuner(
        trainer,
        param_space={'train_loop_config': {'p': 1}},
    )
    return tuner.fit()

ray.init(num_cpus=2)


if os.environ.get('RAY_TRAIN_V2_ENABLED') == '1':
    results = main_v2()
else:
    results = main_v1()

print(f"Done. Errors: {results.num_errors}, Terminated: {results.num_terminated}")
```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.

ray-project/ray#54385.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

this `get_max_chunk_size` function in can lead to an unhandled zero division error.

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/ray-2.47.1/python/ray/data/_internal/arrow_block.py#L187

```python
def _get_max_chunk_size(
    table: "pyarrow.Table", max_chunk_size_bytes: int
) -> Optional[int]:
    """
    Calculate the max chunk size in rows for Arrow to Batches conversion in
    ArrowBlockAccessor.iter_rows().
    Args:
        table: The pyarrow table to calculate the max chunk size for.
        max_chunk_size_bytes: The max chunk size in bytes.
    Returns:
        The max chunk size in rows, or None if the table is empty.
    """
    if table.nbytes == 0:
        return None
    else:
        avg_row_size = int(table.nbytes / table.num_rows)
        return max(1, int(max_chunk_size_bytes / avg_row_size))

```

I'm making a ray dataset using `from_items` where the items are small dictionaries containing tuples used to slice a very large array store. I think this means that `table.nbytes` can be very smaller than `table.num_rows`. So if for example `table.nbytes / table.nrows = .5` then `int(.5) = 0` leading to the zero division error.

I need to use `from_items` with these really small items that describe large array slices, so this bug is a blocker for my use case.


The traceback is below

```
Trace:

      File "/root/x/services/inference.py", line 104, in run_inference
        .take_all()
         ^^^^^^^^^^
      File "x/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/data/dataset.py", line 3096, in take_all
        for row in self.iter_rows():
      File "/x.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/data/iterator.py", line 247, in _wrapped_iterator
        for row in batch.iter_rows(public_row_format=True):
      File "/x/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/data/_internal/arrow_block.py", line 425, in iter_rows
        self._max_chunk_size = _get_max_chunk_size(
                               ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
      File "x/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/data/_internal/arrow_block.py", line 188, in _get_max_chunk_size
        return max(1, int(max_chunk_size_bytes / avg_row_size))
                          ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    ZeroDivisionError: division by zero

Message:

    ZeroDivisionError: division by zero
```

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray - 2.47.1
Python - 3.11
OS - Ubuntu 22.04

### Reproduction script

I don't have an MRE, but you can see from the above function how zero division error can occur.

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#54385.title: [Ray Data] Zero Division Error when pyarrow block table nbytes is small and table.num_rows is large, integer casting leads to zero division

ray-project/ray#54408.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

# What happened
A run with remote `SingleAgentEnvRunner`s crashed. Restoring it via `Algorithm.restore_from_path` led to discontinuities in returns. Before returns were monotonically rising. After restoration returns were continuiously falling. 

# What you expected to happen? 
That restoring leads to continuity in metrics.

Looking into restoring logics shows that remote envrunner states are not restored, i.e. if stateful connectors like `MeanStdFilter` (in my case) are present the states do not get restored and produce data with distributional shift which probably lead to very different metrics and a network that is not trained for this data. 
It appears as if it needs a synchronization of env-runner states, but without `merge` of states. Instead the state needs to be recovered from the local env-runner, if present or directly from the checkpoint.  

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.46
Python 3.12.4
Linux Fedora 40

### Reproduction script

Take `rllib/tuned_examples/ppo/cartpole_ppo.py` with remote env-runners and a `MeanStdFilter` connector.

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#54408.title: [RLlib] - Restoring run with stateful connectors leads to discontinuities.

ray-project/ray#54652.body:
### Description

**TL;DR**: This RFC proposes a new asynchronous API for Ray Serve to support long-running, computationally expensive tasks like batch inference and model fine-tuning. It introduces a producer-consumer architecture where tasks are enqueued and processed by Task Handler, integrating with Task Processor systems like Celery to decouple task submission from execution.

## 1. Motivation

Ray Serve excels at low-latency, synchronous inference but lacks native support for long-running, asynchronous jobs like batch processing, model fine-tuning, or large-scale ETL. Handling these tasks in standard deployments leads to critical issues such as request timeouts, blocked Actors, and a lack of fault tolerance (e.g., no persistence or retries on failure). This RFC proposes a robust, asynchronous execution model to address these limitations by decoupling long-running tasks from the synchronous request-response path.

## 2. Proposal Overview

We propose a producer-consumer architecture.

- **Task Producers** are standard deployments that offload long-running tasks to a message broker and immediately return a task ID.
- **Task Consumer Deployment** are new, dedicated background deployments that pull jobs from the message broker to execute them, complete with retry and failure handling. These deployments achieve this by using Task Processor systems like Celery(possibly others that users can choose from) under the hood.

This architecture will be enabled by a new set of decorators and a TaskProcessorAdapter abstraction, which allows plugging in different Task Processor systems (e.g., Celery).

## 3. Detailed Design and API

### 3.1. Task Consumer

To define a deployment that consumes tasks, users will use two new decorators: `@serve.task_consumer` and `@serve.task_handler`.

- `@serve.task_consumer(config)`: A class decorator that marks a deployment as a background task consumer. It takes a TaskProcessorConfig object which specifies the Broker details.
- `@serve.task_handler(name)`: A method decorator that marks a specific method within the consumer class as the entrypoint for executing a task. The name is used by the producer to specify which handler to invoke.

### Example: A Document Indexing Consumer

```python
# Configuration for the task queue adapter
task_processor_config = TaskProcessorConfig(
    queue_name='document_indexing_queue',
    adapter_config=CelerySettings(broker_url='redis://localhost:6379/0'), # Typed config for the adapter
    max_retry=3, # Max retries on application failure before moving to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)
    unprocessable_task_queue_name='dlq_document_indexing'
)

# Define the background consumer deployment
@serve.deployment
@serve.task_consumer(task_processor_config)
class DocumentIndexingConsumer:
    def __init__(self):
        # Initialize expensive resources once, e.g., models or clients.
        self.indexer = DocumentIndexingEngine()
        print("DocumentIndexingConsumer initialized.")

    @serve.task_handler(name='index_document')
    def index_document(self, document_id: str, document_url: str):
        """
        This method is triggered for each task from the queue.
        The arguments (document_id, document_url) are provided by the producer.
        """
        print(f"Indexing document {document_id} from {document_url}...")
        content = self.indexer.download(document_url)
        result = self.indexer.process(content)

        return {
            "document_id": document_id,
            "status": "indexed",
            "metadata": result
        }

```

### 3.2. The Task Producer

The producer is a regular Serve deployment that instantiates a adapter object that users can use to submit jobs. It uses a TaskProcessorAdapter instance to enqueue tasks.

### Example: A Web API for Submitting Indexing Tasks

```python
@serve.deployment
@serve.ingress(app)
class APIProducer:
    def __init__(self, task_processor_config: TaskProcessorConfig, consumer_app):
        # The adapter provides the interface to the message queue (e.g., Celery).
        self.adapter = create_adapter(task_processor_config)
        self.consumer_app = consumer_app

    @app.post("/index")
    async def submit_task(self, request: Request):
        """Submits a document indexing task to the queue."""
        data = await request.json()
        # Enqueue the task by referencing the handler's name.
        # The kwargs will be passed to the 'index_document' method.
        task = self.adapter.enqueue_task("index_document", kwargs=data)
        return {"task_id": task.id}

    @app.get("/status/{task_id}")
    async def get_task_status(self, task_id: str) -> TaskResult:
        """Retrieves the status or result of a task."""
        # This requires a result backend to be configured in CelerySettings.
        return self.adapter.get_task_status(task_id)

# The create_adapter function is a factory to instantiate the correct adapter.
def create_adapter(config: TaskProcessorConfig) -> TaskProcessorAdapter:
    if isinstance(config.adapter_config, CelerySettings):
        return CeleryAdapter(config.settings)
    # ... add other adapters here
    raise TypeError("Unsupported adapter config type.")
```

### 3.3. Binding the Application

Finally, the producer and consumer deployments are bound together into a single, deployable Serve application.

```python
# 1. Define the consumer application
consumer_app = DocumentIndexingConsumer.bind()

# 2. Bind the producer, passing the config and a handle to the consumer
producer_app = APIProducer.bind(task_processor_config, consumer_app)

# The final 'producer_app' can be deployed with `serve.run`.
# Ray Serve will ensure the consumer deployment is also started.
```

### 3.4. Core Abstractions and Class Definitions

The following classes form the foundation of this API.

`TaskProcessorConfig`
A Pydantic model for configuring the task queue behavior.

```python
class TaskProcessorConfig(BaseModel):
    queue_name: str
    adapter_config: Union[CelerySettings, TestSettings] # Extendable for more backends
    max_retry: Optional[int] = 3
    failed_task_queue_name: Optional[str] = None # Name for the Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)
    unprocessable_task_queue_name: Optional[str] = None # For tasks that fail deserialization
```

`TaskResult`
A standardized structure for returning task status and results.

```python
class TaskResult(BaseModel):
    id: str
    status: str # e.g., PENDING, STARTED, SUCCESS, FAILURE
    backend_task_id: str # The internal ID from the backend (e.g., Celery task ID)
    created_at: float
    result: Optional[Any] = None
```

`TaskProcessorAdapter`
An abstract base class defining the interface for interacting with a Task Processor systems. This allows for a pluggable architecture.

```python
class TaskProcessorAdapter(abc.ABC):
    @abc.abstractmethod
    def initialize(self, **kwargs):
        """Initialize the adapter connection."""
        ...

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def register_task_handler(self, func, name=None):
        """Registers a Python function as a handler for a task name."""
        ...

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def enqueue_task(self, task_name, args=None, kwargs=None, **options) -> TaskResult:
        """Adds a task to the queue."""
        ...

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def get_task_status(self, task_id) -> TaskResult:
        """Gets the status and result of a task."""
        ...

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def start_consumer(self, **kwargs):
        """Starts the worker process to consume tasks from the queue."""
        ...

    # --- Other lifecycle and utility methods ---
    def stop_consumer(self): ...
    def shutdown(self): ...
    def health_check(self) -> bool: ...
    def cancel_task(self, task_id) -> bool: ...
    def get_metrics(self) -> Dict[str, Any]: ...
```

### 4. Concrete Implementations

We will provide initial, production-grade support for common backends, beginning with the `CeleryAdapter` in the first release. Serve will also ship with a memory broker for local testing. 

```python
class CeleryAdapter(TaskProcessorAdapter):
    # Implements the adapter interface using the Celery library.
    ...

```

### 5. Handling safe rollouts and rollbacks

In scenarios where both the producer and consumer code remain unchanged, upgrades and rollbacks are inherently safe. However, issues arise when:

- Backwards incompatibility: New consumers cannot process old enqueued tasks.
- Forwards incompatibility: Old consumers cannot process newly enqueued tasks.

#### 5.1. **Sources of Incompatibility**

These typically stem from the following changes:

1. Task name changes: If the name of a task changes, previously enqueued tasks may not match any handler, causing them to be dropped or unprocessed.
2. Handler removal: Removing a task handler leads to the same outcome — any existing tasks relying on it will fail to be processed.
3. Parameter schema changes: Changing the arguments or structure of a task can cause deserialization failures, leading to runtime crashes in the consumer.

#### 5.2. **Ray Serve Runtime Behavior During Upgrades/Rollbacks**

Ray Serve handles upgrades and rollbacks by:

- Spinning up new deployment replicas for the updated version.
- Gracefully draining old replicas: ongoing requests are completed before shutdown.
- Ungraceful shutdowns (e.g., preemption or crash) interrupt task execution, causing tasks to be returned to the queue for reprocessing.

During this transitional phase, it's inevitable that:

- Old consumers may encounter new tasks they cannot parse.
- New consumers may encounter old tasks they are incompatible with.

Since Ray Serve does not own or control the producer, and producers may reside outside the Ray ecosystem, Serve cannot enforce protocol guarantees or task schema stability at the source.

#### 5.3. **What Ray Serve Can Do**

To minimize impact from incompatibilities, Serve should adopt the following design strategies:

1. Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)
    - Automatically move tasks that fail due to deserialization or missing handler into a DLQ identified by **TaskProcessorConfig.unprocessable_task_queue_name**.
    - Tasks that enter DLQ should include failure metadata (e.g., exception trace, payload, task name).
2. Operational Metrics + Alerting
    - Emit metrics for Failed task handler resolution and Task deserialization failures
    - Emit logs with task metadata for observability.
3. Manual Recovery Path
    - Users can inspect DLQ and decide whether to:
        - Re-enqueue tasks into a compatible consumer.
        - Transform and migrate them.
        - Drop them intentionally.
4. Stop polling new tasks when shutdown sequence starts.

#### 5.4. **What Ray Serve Cannot Do**

- Guarantee compatibility: Ray Serve cannot enforce or validate compatibility between task schemas across versions.
- Prevent breakage from external producers: It cannot ensure that the producer respects expected schemas or naming conventions.
- Migrate old tasks to new schema: Transformation or adaptation of task formats should remain a user responsibility.

#### 5.5. **Shifting traffic during rollouts**

As new deployment replicas spin up, both the old and new versions may concurrently consume tasks from the broker. The distribution of tasks between versions is roughly equal to distribution of number replicas between old and new version; and behavior is entirely governed by the broker’s routing logic—similar to Kafka’s consumer group semantics. This default behavior does not enforce a gradual rollout or traffic shift. Each Task Consumer Deployment exposes a health_check API that Ray Serve can use to assess replica health. If a replica fails the check, it can be marked as UNHEALTHY.

### 6. Glossary

1. **Task**: references a user defined function along with arguments to that function
2. **Task Processor**: e.g., Celery, ARQ, RQ.
3. **Broker**: eg: Redis Queue, SQS, kafka. Interactions with queues happen through Task Processors, Ray Serve will not talk to broker directly as far as this design is concerned.
4. **Task Processor Adapter**: A pluggable Ray Serve interface that abstracts over different task processors.
5. **Task Consumer Deployment**: A Ray Serve deployment responsible for consuming and executing background tasks from a queue.
6. **Task Handler**: A UDF within a Task Consumer Deployment annotated to handle specific task(s).
7. **Task Result**: A standardized response object containing metadata such as task ID, status, and optional result (if supported).
8. **Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)**: A failure-handling mechanism where tasks that can't be processed (due to deserialization or handler errors) are stored with failure metadata for later manual inspection by the end user.

### Use case

#### Video Inferencing
Many video inference jobs take more than the standard timeout for a given request. However they benefit greatly from the scaling capabilities of Ray Serve. Async Task Processing is a natural fit for these inference workloads
ray-project/ray#54652.title: [RFC] - [Serve] Support for Asynchronous inference

ray-project/ray#54659.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

If `SingleAgentEpisode.concat_episode` gets dict observations, it will fail with the following error:
```
...
File "/Users/<redacted>/miniconda3/envs/tax-metrics-exp/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/rllib/env/single_agent_episode.py", line 626, in concat_episode
    assert np.all(other.observations[0] == self.observations[-1])
                  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
ValueError: The truth value of an array with more than one element is ambiguous. Use a.any() or a.all()
```

This is, since np.all needs to be called for each numpy array of each key separately. Something like this could maybe work, but I'm not sure these are always numpy array. Otherwise overwriting the equality check of the dict might be possible?

```python3
import numpy as np

class SingleAgentEpisode:
    ...
    def concat_episode(self, other: "SingleAgentEpisode") -> None:
        ...
        np.testing.assert_equal(other.observations[0], self.observations[-1])
        ...
```

### Versions / Dependencies

ray[rllib]: 2.47.1
Python 3.11.13
MacOS Sequoia 15.5

### Reproduction script

```python3
import gymnasium as gym
from gymnasium import spaces
import numpy as np
from ray.rllib.env.single_agent_episode import SingleAgentEpisode

class DummyDictObsEnv(gym.Env):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_features = 5
        self.n_actions = 4

        self.observation_space = spaces.Dict({
            "features": spaces.Box(shape=(self.n_features,),
                                   low=-np.inf, high=np.inf, dtype=np.float32),
            "action_mask": spaces.Box(shape=(self.n_actions,),
                                      low=0.0, high=1.0, dtype=np.float32)
        })

        self.action_space = spaces.Discrete(self.n_actions)
        self._current_step = 0
        self._max_steps = 3

    def _get_obs(self):
        return {
            "features": np.zeros((self.n_features,), dtype=np.float32),
            "action_mask": np.ones((self.n_actions,), dtype=np.float32) # Mask always full for simplicity
        }

    def reset(self, seed=None, options=None):
        self._current_step = 0
        return self._get_obs(), {}

    def step(self, _):
        self._current_step += 1
        return self._get_obs(), 1.0, False, False, {}


print("Setting up DummyDictObsEnv (Simplified with Gymnasium)...")
env = DummyDictObsEnv()

print("\nCollecting data for Episode 1...")
obs_list_1 = []
reward_list_1 = []
action_list_1 = []
terminal_list_1 = []
info_list_1 = []

initial_obs_1, initial_info_1 = env.reset()
obs_list_1.append(initial_obs_1)
info_list_1.append(initial_info_1)

for i in range(env._max_steps):
    action = env.action_space.sample()
    observation, reward, terminated, truncated, info = env.step(action)
    obs_list_1.append(observation)
    reward_list_1.append(reward)
    action_list_1.append(action)
    terminal_list_1.append(terminated)
    info_list_1.append(info)

episode1 = SingleAgentEpisode(
    id_="test_episode",
    observations=obs_list_1,
    rewards=reward_list_1,
    actions=action_list_1,
    terminated=False,
    infos=info_list_1,
)
print(f"Episode 1 created with {len(episode1)} steps.")
print(f"Episode 1 last observation (t={len(episode1)-1}): {episode1.observations[-1]}")

print("\nCollecting data for Episode 2...")
obs_list_2 = []
reward_list_2 = []
action_list_2 = []
terminal_list_2 = []
info_list_2 = []

initial_obs_2, initial_info_2 = env.reset()
obs_list_2.append(initial_obs_2) # Add initial observation
info_list_2.append(initial_info_2)

for i in range(env._max_steps):
    action = env.action_space.sample()
    observation, reward, terminated, truncated, info = env.step(action)
    obs_list_2.append(observation)
    reward_list_2.append(reward)
    action_list_2.append(action)
    terminal_list_1.append(terminated)
    info_list_2.append(info)


episode2 = SingleAgentEpisode(
    id_="test_episode",
    observations=obs_list_2,
    rewards=reward_list_2,
    actions=action_list_2,
    terminated=False,
    infos=info_list_2,
)

concatenated_episode = episode1.concat_episode(episode2) # Fails
```


### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#54659.title: [RLlib] SingleAgentEpisode is not designed to handle dict observations

ray-project/ray#54790.body: We have various simple Ray Data pipelines consisting of a BigQuery datasource, an actor with a CPU or GPU workload, and a BigQuery datasink. We’re seeing a dropoff in throughput and actor utilization when the number of actors exceeds about 500. We crafted a simple repro example (attached) using a real BigQuery datasource, a dummy actor, and a dummy writer. The dummy actor does no compute - it just sleeps - so the whole dummy workload “fits” on a single-node Ray cluster. _Note: The example requires a BigQuery table with 1 million rows (schema doesn’t matter)._ Here are some observed execution times for the repro example with different numbers of actors (actor concurrency): We noticed that [`DataOpTask.on_data_ready()`](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/data/_internal/execution/interfaces/physical_operator.py#L121) deliberately busy-waits until more data is available, by repeatedly calling for more data using a timeout value of 0, i.e. check for more data and return immediately. Using 1,000 actors, we modified the `self._streaming_gen._next_sync` call [here](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/61d3f2f1aa33563faa398105f4abda88cb39440b/python/ray/data/_internal/execution/interfaces/physical_operator.py#L115) to specify a non-zero `timeout_s`, e.g. 0.01s or 0.1s. This seems to increase actor throughput by 2x or more, and the overall execution time is lower. We added some logging to `DataOpTask.on_data_ready` to see when it’s called and how many blocks it returns. With `self._streaming_gen._next_sync(timeout_s=0)`: (the default), the observed behavior pattern is: - A few (~5) calls to `DataOpTask.on_data_ready` - returns only a few blocks total - Actors process small # of blocks returned by above calls - throughput is low - Repeat With `timeout_s > 0`, the observed pattern is: - Many (50+) calls to `DataOpTask.on_data_ready` - returns many blocks - Actors process many blocks - Repeat In the first case, there is not enough data returned on each “pump” of the input to utilize all the actors. In the second case, there is much more data available on each “pump” of the input, and the actors are fully “utilized”. We suspect that specifying a non-zero (blocking) timeout gives an upstream object (e.g. BigQuery reader/splitter) a chance to run and provide more data, while busy-waiting with a zero timeout starves that same upstream object of CPU time, but this is just a hypothesis. We added some timing to the scheduler main loop, and we determined that it spends most of its time in the actor ranking step. Profiling showed the hottest functions are those involved in updating the state of all the actors: ``` Collecting samples from 'python reproducible_example.py' (python v3.10.14) Total Samples 13109 GIL: 59.00%, Active: 63.00%, Threads: 6 %Own %Total OwnTime TotalTime Function (filename) 14.00% 41.00% 18.34s 46.64s update_resource_usage (ray/data/_internal/execution/operators/actor_pool_map_operator.py) 11.00% 11.00% 11.73s 11.73s __getattr__ (protobuf/internal/enum_type_wrapper.py) 8.00% 8.00% 10.63s 10.63s (ray/data/_internal/execution/operators/actor_pool_map_operator.py) 8.00% 10.00% 6.93s 8.19s _get_local_state (ray/actor.py) 4.00% 4.00% 5.10s 5.10s _actor_id (ray/actor.py) 4.00% 8.00% 4.52s 9.62s __hash__ (ray/actor.py) ``` If we modify the repro example to use Tasks instead of Actors, utilization is higher, and the actor state bottleneck disappears: ``` Collecting samples from 'python reproducible_example.py' (python v3.10.14) Total Samples 9714 GIL: 12.00%, Active: 16.00%, Threads: 6 %Own %Total OwnTime TotalTime Function (filename) 6.00% 6.00% 2.38s 2.44s wait (ray/_private/worker.py) 0.00% 0.00% 0.530s 1.25s invocation (ray/remote_function.py) 0.00% 2.00% 0.420s 2.37s update_usages (ray/data/_internal/execution/resource_manager.py) 0.00% 0.00% 0.380s 0.390s _deserialize_pickle5_data (ray/_private/serialization.py) 1.00% 4.00% 0.310s 1.62s on_data_ready (ray/data/_internal/execution/interfaces/physical_operator.py) 1.00% 1.00% 0.300s 0.300s get_output_blocking (ray/data/_internal/execution/streaming_executor_state.py) ``` It seems like the combination of 1) the slow actor ranking step and 2) the zero timeout on the `self._streaming_gen._next_sync()` call cause the actors to be starved for data, because (1) causes the scheduler main loop to run at a low frequency, and (2) limits the amount of data made available to actors per loop iteration. _If our understanding is correct, what can we do about (1) or (2)? For example, can we expose timeout_s as a configurable parameter? Or is it possible to run the actor ranking less frequently, so that the scheduler main loop body takes less time (on average) and the input is read more frequently?_ _Lastly, what is a reasonable max number of actors?_ ### Versions / Dependencies Ray 2.47.1 Python 3.10 OS: Linux ### Reproduction script ``` # Note: Requires a BigQuery table with 1 million rows (schema doesn’t matter). import time import ray from ray.data import Datasink PROJECT_ID = "project" QUERY = "SELECT * FROM `project.dataset.table`" ACTOR_CONCURRENCY = 1000 class DummyModel: def __init__(self): pass def __call__(self, batch): # Sleep 0.1 sec to simulate some processing time for each row. k = list(batch.keys())[0] for _ in range(len(batch[k])): # Simulate processing time time.sleep(0.1) # No-op, just return the batch as is for demonstration purposes. return batch class DummyDatasink(Datasink): def write( self, blocks, ctx, ): # print("Mock writing") pass @property def min_rows_per_write(self) -> int: return 1000 ds = ray.data.read_bigquery( project_id=PROJECT_ID, # 1M rows query=QUERY, # 100 items per block override_num_blocks=10000, ) ds = ds.map_batches( # Defer the actor instantiation to mimic the real ML model loading and moving to CUDA. DummyModel, #DummyModel(), # Uncomment this to use Tasks instead of Actors # One block per batch batch_size=None, concurrency=ACTOR_CONCURRENCY, num_cpus=0.001, zero_copy_batch=True, ) ds.write_datasink( DummyDatasink(), ) ``` ### Issue Severity Medium: It is a significant difficulty but we can work around it.
ray-project/ray#54790.title: [Data] Datasource throughput bottlenecked by scheduler's actor update with large number of actors?

ray-project/ray#54823.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The one hot encoder fails with the following error when the dataframe contains unhashable objects such as list, dicts or np.arrays.
```
./.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/data/preprocessors/encoder.py:281: in _transform_pandas
    codes = df[column].apply(lambda v, m=stats: m.get(v, -1)).to_numpy()
../.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/series.py:4924: in apply
    ).apply()
../.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/apply.py:1427: in apply
    return self.apply_standard()
../.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/apply.py:1507: in apply_standard
    mapped = obj._map_values(
../.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/base.py:921: in _map_values
    return algorithms.map_array(arr, mapper, na_action=na_action, convert=convert)
../.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/algorithms.py:1743: in map_array
    return lib.map_infer(values, mapper, convert=convert)
lib.pyx:2972: in pandas._libs.lib.map_infer
    ???
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ 

v = [1], m = {'1': 0, 'True': 1, '[1]': 2, '[]': 3, ...}

>   codes = df[column].apply(lambda v, m=stats: m.get(v, -1)).to_numpy()
E   TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'
```

This regression was introduced in this commit https://github.com/ray-project/ray/commit/c5e45ed42c4d23a1da0cb8ae1ade891d37866e39

### Versions / Dependencies

ray 2.48.0

### Reproduction script

```
import ray
from ray.data.preprocessors.encoder import OneHotEncoder

test_inputs = {"category": ["1", [1]]}
test_pd_df = pd.DataFrame(test_inputs)
test_data_for_fitting = {"category": ["1", "[1]", "a", "[]", "True"]}
test_ray_dataset_for_fitting = ray.data.from_pandas(pd.DataFrame(test_data_for_fitting))

encoder = OneHotEncoder(columns=["category"])
encoder.fit(test_ray_dataset_for_fitting)

pandas_output = encoder.transform_batch(test_pd_df)
```

it also fails with these other inputs:
```
{"category": ["1", np.array([1])]}
```

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#54823.title: [Data/Preprocessors]: one hot encoder fails with error on mixed types

```python
        "The future of AI is",
    ]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

ray-project/ray#54869.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The DEFAULT_API_VERSION version being used at https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/55477f1a8d95bd0acb8f43d0b10bff93b3c5855a/python/ray/autoscaler/_private/_azure/config.py#L118-L124 is not supported by `Microsoft.Network/virtualNetwork`s.

The DEFAULT_API_VERSION being used is `2024-11-01` and the latest version for virtualNetwork resources is `2024-10-01`

```bash
az provider show --namespace Microsoft.Network --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='virtualNetworks'].apiVersions[]" --output tsv
2024-10-01

2024-05-01
2024-03-01
2024-01-01
```

Development environment using ray@main
