ray-project/ray#54916.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

A named actor `A`, named "test," has exhausted its restart attempts. After exhausting its restart attempts, we delete all references to it. Subsequently, we restart a new actor with the same name, actor `B`. However, during the lifetime of actor `B`, calling get_actor('test') will result in an error.

After reviewing gcs_actor_manager.cc, I found that the `RemoveActorNameFromRegistry` function is triggered both when the retry attempts are exhausted and when the reference count drops to zero. This results in the name "test" being deleted twice. During the interval between these two deletions, actor B was inserted with the name "test," leading to its unintended deletion.


### Versions / Dependencies

- python: 3.9
- ray: master eb76665e83fee7adfaf4c7c129e743eac403d6bf

### Reproduction script

- pytest case:
``` python
def test_get_actor_after_same_name_actor_dead(shutdown_only):
    ray.init(namespace="test")

    @ray.remote
    class Actor:
        def get_pid(self):
            return os.getpid()

    def get_pid(actor):
        while True:
            try:
                return ray.get(actor.get_pid.remote())
            except:
                pass
    
    a = Actor.options(name="test", max_restarts=1).remote()

    pid = get_pid(a)
    os.kill(pid, signal.SIGKILL)

    pid = get_pid(a)
    os.kill(pid, signal.SIGKILL)
    # Ensure that the GCS detects that the actor(a) has exhausted its 
    # restart attempts and completes the function 
    # `GcsActorManager::RemoveActorNameFromRegistry`.
    time.sleep(5)
    
    b = Actor.options(name="test", max_restarts=1).remote()
    ray.get(b.__ray_ready__.remote())
    
    # triger rpc reply: WaitForActorRefDeleted
    del a
    gc.collect()

    # Ensure that the GCS detects that the actor(a) reference count 
    # has dropped to zero, and once again completes the function 
    # GcsActorManager::RemoveActorNameFromRegistry.
    time.sleep(5)

    # Ensure actor(b) is still alive
    ray.get(b.__ray_ready__.remote())
    _ = ray.get_actor("test", namespace="test")
```

- got error:
```bash
>   raise ValueError(message)
E   ValueError: Failed to look up actor with name 'test'. This could because 1. You are trying to look up a named actor you didn't create. 2. The named actor died. 3. You did not use a namespace matching the namespace of the actor.

python/ray/includes/common.pxi:87: ValueError
```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#54916.title: [Core]get_actor failed, but the named-actor is alive

ray-project/ray#54948.body:
### Description

For context see: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/54925

Looking at the methods in raylet_client.cc some of these methods are ONLY for the local raylet client to use. If we generated a raylet connection from the raylet client pool and called these methods it would crash (since conn_ is a nullptr). Hence we could create a new class "LocalRayletClient", separate out all the methods that only the local raylet client should use, and leave the grpc stuff in the RayletClient.  Methods that only the local raylet client use are those that use conn_, since the regular remote raylet client just has that set to nullptr.
ray-project/ray#54948.title: [core] Create a separate LocalRayletClient

ray-project/ray#54965.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

**Bug**

Hi, I am running a single Ray node in my local Docker network. I am trying to set up the architecture explained [here](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/serve/advanced-guides/multi-app-container.html), where Ray nodes leverage Podman to create containerized runtimes.

When Ray creates the new runtime_env, it executes a command that causes Podman to pull the image, run a container and execute a particular command. That particular command has the aim of establishing the image's entrypoint, that will be used as `override_worker_entrypoint` of the runtime_env.

The issue I'm experiencing is that the `override_worker_entrypoint` contains the whole log of Podman instead of just the command's output, as you can see in the log below:

_runtime_env_agent.log_
``` shell
2025-07-27 23:07:59,288	INFO runtime_env_agent.py:423 -- Successfully created runtime env: {"image_uri": "env-base:latest"}, context: {"command_prefix": [], "env_vars": {}, "py_executable": "podman run -v /tmp/ray:/tmp/ray --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs --network=host --pid=host --ipc=host --userns=keep-id --env RAY_CLIENT_MODE='0' --env RAY_ENABLE_RECORD_ACTOR_TASK_LOGGING='true' --env RAY_GRAFANA_HOST='http://grafana:3000' --env RAY_GRAFANA_ORG_ID='1' --env RAY_LD_PRELOAD_ON_WORKERS='0' --env RAY_NODE_ID='[REDACTED_TOKEN]' --env RAY_PROMETHEUS_HOST='http://prometheus:9090' --env RAY_PROMETHEUS_NAME='Prometheus' --env RAY_RAYLET_PID='599' --env RAY_REDIS_ADDRESS='redis:6379' --env RAY_USAGE_STATS_ENABLED='0' --env RAY_enable_pipe_based_agent_to_parent_health_check='1' --env RAY_record_task_actor_creation_sites='true' --env RAY_worker_register_timeout_seconds='600' --env RAY_JOB_ID=$RAY_JOB_ID --entrypoint python env-base:latest", **"override_worker_entrypoint": "Resolving \"env-base\" using unqualified-search registries (/etc/containers/registries.conf)\nTrying to pull docker.io/library/env-base:latest...\nTrying to pull docker-registry:5000/env-base:latest...\nGetting image source signatures\nCopying blob sha256:97e3786d2ab4c5d1ea4d84ec14b90451f31dfd1cbeaa646f81f6ab5fca385c3b\n[...redacted for conciseness....]Writing manifest to image destination\nStoring signatures\n/usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/workers/default_worker.py", "java_jars": []}
```

Note: if I pull the image into the Ray nodes **before** ever running the reproduction script, then ray sets the correct `override_worker_entrypoint`. Thus, the issue seems related to the first time the image gets pulled when `podman run` is executed.

I suspect that the involved function is this one: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/f9190c0b7971fdf468f42cf8da53ec183908ee86/python/ray/_private/runtime_env/utils.py#L47C11-L47C27 .

**Expected behavior**

The runtime env agent log should look like this one:
``` sh
2025-07-27 23:09:44,504	INFO runtime_env_agent.py:423 -- Successfully created runtime env: {"image_uri": "env-base:latest"}, context: {"command_prefix": [], "env_vars": {}, "py_executable": "podman run -v /tmp/ray:/tmp/ray --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs --network=host --pid=host --ipc=host --userns=keep-id --env <env_vars> --entrypoint python env-base:latest", "override_worker_entrypoint": "/usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/_private/workers/default_worker.py", "java_jars": []}
```

### Possible solution

A possible solution could be to suppress the pull log from `podman run` command, using the `--quiet` flag documented [here](https://docs.podman.io/en/v5.0.1/markdown/podman-run.1.html#quiet-q).

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray image `rayproject/ray:2.48.0-py311`
Python 3.11 with ray[default]==2.48.0


### Reproduction script

```python
@ray.remote(
    num_cpus=1,
    runtime_env={
        "image_uri": "rayproject/ray:2.48.0-py311",
    },
)
def example_task():
    return "Executed!"

ray.init(log_to_driver=True)
# same happens with an external deployment, e.g.:
# ray.init(address="ray://localhost:10001", log_to_driver=True)

remote = example_task.remote()
print(ray.get(remote))
```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#54965.title: [Core] override_worker_entrypoint is wrongly populated

ray-project/ray#55008.body:
## Summary
Ray Data currently lacks built-in checkpointing functionality, which makes it challenging to recover from failures in long-running data processing pipelines. This feature request proposes adding checkpoint and resume capabilities to Ray Data to improve fault tolerance and reduce the cost of restarting large-scale data processing jobs.

## Motivation
Large Ray Data pipelines can take hours or days to complete. When failures occur due to unconfigured retryable exceptions or bug, the entire pipeline must restart from the beginning, resulting in:
   
1. High Costs
   - Significant GPU resource waste
   - Extended time-to-completion
2. Operational Complexity
   - Users currently need to manually segment large jobs (e.g., splitting a single large job into 10 parts)
   - No built-in mechanism to preserve progress when jobs are interrupted
   - Cross-cluster job migration is not supported Proposed Solution: jobs must be migrated to other clusters/data centers when high-priority workloads require the urgently resources

## Requirements Overview

1. Job State Persistence to External Storage
2. Cross-Cluster Resume

### Use case

_No response_
ray-project/ray#55008.title: [Data] Add Checkpoint/Resume Support for Ray Data Pipelines

ray-project/ray#55043.body:
# Parallel write tasks overwrite each other's files when using write_parquet with mode="overwrite"

## Bug Description

When using `write_parquet` with `mode="overwrite"` on a partitioned Ray Dataset, parallel write tasks overwrite each other's output files, resulting in significant data loss. Each write task appears to independently clear the output directory instead of coordinating to write unique files.

## Expected Behavior

- Output directory should be cleared once at the beginning
- Each of the 10 partitions should be written to a separate file (e.g., `part_00000.parquet`, `part_00001.parquet`, etc.)
- All 1000 rows should be preserved across the 10 files

## Actual Behavior

- Multiple write tasks run in parallel
- Each task appears to clear the directory independently
- Tasks overwrite each other's files
- Final output contains only a fraction of the expected data (typically data from the last task to complete)

## Additional Notes

- The bug is reproducible consistently
- The warning message suggests the system is aware files might collide but doesn't prevent it

## Environment

- **Ray version**: 2.48.0
- **Python version**: 3.11
- **OS**: macOS Darwin 24.5.0

## Minimal Reproducible Example

```python
import ray
import pandas as pd

ray.init(num_cpus=4)

# Create dataset with 1000 rows
df = pd.DataFrame({'id': range(1000), 'value': [f'value_{i}' for i in range(1000)]})
ds = ray.data.from_pandas(df)

# Repartition to ensure multiple write tasks
ds = ds.repartition(10)

# This should write 10 files in parallel
# Bug: parallel write tasks overwrite each other
ds.write_parquet('test_output', mode='overwrite')

result = ray.data.read_parquet('test_output')
print(f"Expected: 1000 rows, Actual: {result.count()} rows")

ray.shutdown()
```

## Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#55043.title: Parallel write tasks overwrite each other's files when using write_parquet with mode="overwrite"

ray-project/ray#55117.body:
We'd like to report our recent findings about `agent.py` process causing significant training throughput slowdown due to repeated calls to psutil.memory_full_info(). This call seemed to be removed in https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/6553 as it was identified as expensive, but reintroduced in https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/25418. 

The call will invoke a low level linux kernel call that collect memory stats while putting a lock on the memory page table, which completely stalls the target process. In our distributed training setup, since this call is invoked in a loop of worker processes, it caused each rank to stall in a round-robin fashion, while every other rank has to wait at synchronization points. Removing this call in a custom Ray build, we were able to improve our training throughput by 50%. 

We use a custom ray build based on 2.10, PyTorch 2.6, and Python 3.11. The slow down is less obvious if
- disable torch.compile
- use a Python version <= 3.10
- use a PyTorch version <= 2.2.2
ray-project/ray#55117.title: psutil.memory_full_info can cause significant training slowdown

ray-project/ray#55119.body:
### Description

## Context:

1. Ray requires you to have a different command for head vs worker, and then a separate invocation for the actual job submission  
2. This is in contrast to other “simpler” setups like torchrun and mpirun, where there is 1 command that is executed across all nodes (with parameters like rank / node specification)

### Ray style execution in vLLM

```py
# head node, terminal 1:
ray start --block

# worker node, terminal 1:
ray start --block --address='ip:6379'

# head node, terminal 2:
vllm serve -tp 16
```

### torchrun style execution in SGLang

```py
# On all nodes, run this (and set rank to 0 or 1)
# This will setup distributed comms and start the model server
python3 -m sglang.launch_server --tp 16 --dist-init-addr ip:20000 --nnodes 2 --rank ?
```

This style of execution fits really well with other parallel execution shell utilities, like xpanes:

```py
xpanes -c "NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=bond0 GLOO_SOCKET_IFNAME=bond0 python3 -m sglang.launch_server --tp 16 --dist-init-addr ip:20000 --nnodes 2 --node-rank {}" 0 1
```

This is also similar to torchrun which is used for most distributed training on SLURM, where rank/other variables are extracted from the SLURM runtime:

```shell
MASTER_ADDR=$(scontrol show hostnames $SLURM_JOB_NODELIST | head -n 1)
MASTER_PORT=6000
GPUS_PER_NODE=8

torchrun --nproc_per_node $GPUS_PER_NODE \
    --nnodes $SLURM_NNODES \
    --node_rank $SLURM_PROCID \
    --rdzv_endpoint $MASTER_ADDR:$MASTER_PORT \     $YOUR_SCRIPT
```

## Core requirements for a solution

1. On every node, only 1 command needed to be run in order to start the cluster AND program execution. This command can have variable input per node. In particular, you should be able to specify which cluster IP for all nodes to connect to.  
2. The cluster lifecycle should be tied to the program (similar to ray.init() on a single node) \-- starts with the command, and cleans up with the command.  
3. Environment variables should be passed to the executing program.  
4. P1: Cluster startup should be fast.

```shell
# Run this command on each node, but only run the driver script on the head node
python -m ray.scripts.symmetric_run --address=ip:port -- python script.py --args --args2
# KEY: DON'T GENERALLY DOCUMENT THIS - ONLY IN A CERTAIN CONTEXT

# Specify env vars 
NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=bond0 GLOO_SOCKET_IFNAME=bond0 python -m ray.scripts.symmetric_run --head-ip-address=... -- python script.py --script-arg1 --script-arg2

# Run xpanes
xpanes -c \
   "NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=bond0 GLOO_SOCKET_IFNAME=bond0 python -m ray.scripts.symmetric_run --head-ip-address=... \     
   'vllm serve --model ...'"
```


### Use case

_No response_
ray-project/ray#55119.title: [RFC] Symmetric Ray Run (torchrun-style) CLI

ray-project/ray#55131.body: ### What happened + What you expected to happen Currently, the decrease in object_ref.submitted_task_ref_count is caused by two scenarios: 1. when the task marked finishes/fails 2. when the task is in the ResolveDependencies phase and discovers that the object_ref is inline. Currently, our implementation of submitting tasks can ensure that marking a task as finished or failed always occurs after the `ResolveDependencies` phase. However, ray.cancel can advance the call to `FailOrRetryPendingTask`. Although we have implemented numerous safeguards during the cancellation process, but the implementation of the actor `SubmitTask` is asynchronous, specifically ray will calling `LocalDependencyResolver::ResolveDependencies` in `io_service_`, can lead to an unintended timing issue. In a particular timing scenario, `ResolveDependencies` may be called after `FailOrRetryPendingTask` has completed. This unexpected sequence ultimately triggers the RAY_CHECK failed error. ### Versions / Dependencies - python: 3.11.2 - ray master: bbbe9ef08a896d6512f750e8b6aaf489b0a77dd5 ### Reproduction script - The reproduction relies on the special timing caused by the blockage of `CoreWorker::io_service_`, which is hard to reproduce under normal circumstances. An artificial sleep needs to be added: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/compare/master...Catch-Bull:ray:rp_submitted_task_ref_count_check_failed - test.py: ``` python import ray ray.init() import time @ray.remote def data(): return 10 @ray.remote(max_task_retries=-1) class Actor: def run(self, x): return True a = Actor.remote() in_ref = data.remote() ref = a.run.remote(in_ref) time.sleep(1) ray.cancel(ref) # Wait for a while, and the process will exit due to RAY_CHECK failed. time.sleep(60) ``` - run: `python test.py` Wait for a while, and the process will exit due to RAY_CHECK failed. ### Issue Severity None
ray-project/ray#55131.title: [Core]`ray.cancel` actor task may cause RAY_CHECK failed: `it->second.submitted_task_ref_count > 0`

ray-project/ray#55162.body:
### Description

This issue will serve as an implementation tracker for a `JaxTrainer` to support [jax](https://github.com/jax-ml/jax) and SPMD workloads. The initial support for this framework will target SPMD with multi-host TPUs on Kubernetes.

Milestone 1: MVP of JaxTrainer with single-slice multi-host TPUs
- Add default TPU info to Ray node labels
  - https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/55064
- Add API change to `ScalingConfig` to specify `topology` and `accelerator` arguments
- Add JaxTrainer wrapper of [DataParallelTrainer](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/train/api/doc/ray.train.data_parallel_trainer.DataParallelTrainer.html) to RayTrain with SPMD scheduling support
  - https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/55207 
- Extensively test Jax training workload with multi-host TPUs and Anyscale and KubeRay operators
- Add documentation and guides to RayTrain docs

Milestone 2: Full support for TPU multi-slice
- Implemented in: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/58629

Milestone 3: Elastic training with multi-slice TPUs in Ray Train
- Detect Alive TPU slices: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/61300
- Update elastic policy to scale for TPU slices: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/61299

### Use case

Full support for SPMD workloads orchestrated with Ray and RayTrain.

### Description

Right now we require actors to specify `@ray.remote(enable_tensor_transport=True)` *and* `@ray.method(tensor_transport="xxx")` to use GPU objects. Only the latter one is needed. We should implicitly set enable_tensor_transport=True on the actor if any of its methods have a non-object store `tensor_transport`.

_No response_
ray-project/ray#55269.title: [core][gpu-objects] Enable GPU objects when either @ray.remote or @ray.method is specified

ray-project/ray#55275.body:
### Description

It seems that `ray.train.torch.enable_reproducibility()` should only be used inside a trainer function, not outside. This is because it sets some global seed, which affects other parts of the code that uses `np.random` or similar. We should improve the doc on this.

For example, when training with multiple epochs, it's not clear if `ray.data.read_parquet(..., shuffle="files")` will use the same seed during the training or if it will reshuffle files with the same seed at each epoch.

### Use case

In an ideal world, the model performance (which depends on data ingestion and training) is not affected by fixing the random seed.
ray-project/ray#55275.title: [Data/Train/Doc] Improve reproducibility

ray-project/ray#55354.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The map_batches function should respect the max_concurrency setting as specified in the actor options. However, when I set max_concurrency > 1, the map_batches on a sync actor still has no concurrency, all operations on the actor are executed serially. The map_batches on an async actor gives expected concurrency.

The following reproduction code prints:
```shell
Multithreaded actor with max_concurrency=2: ['enter', 'enter', 'exit', 'exit']
Async actor with max_concurrency=2: ['enter', 'enter', 'exit', 'exit']
map_batches on a sync Actor with max_concurrency=2: ['enter', 'exit', 'enter', 'exit']
map_batches on an async Actor with max_concurrency=2: ['enter', 'enter', 'exit', 'exit']
```

You can see map_batches on a sync Actor with max_concurrency=2 is executed serially.

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.48.0

### Reproduction script

```python
import asyncio
import time
import ray
import pyarrow as pa


@ray.remote
class Recorder:
    def __init__(self):
        self._seq = []

    def clear(self):
        self._seq.clear()

    def append(self, seq):
        self._seq.append(seq)

    def get_seq(self):
        return self._seq


class Actor:
    def __init__(self, recorder):
        recorder.clear.remote()
        self._recorder = recorder

    def __call__(self, batch: pa.Table) -> pa.Table:
        self._recorder.append.remote("enter")
        time.sleep(1)
        self._recorder.append.remote("exit")
        return batch


class AsyncActor:
    def __init__(self, recorder):
        recorder.clear.remote()
        self._recorder = recorder

    async def __call__(self, batch: pa.Table) -> pa.Table:
        self._recorder.append.remote("enter")
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
        self._recorder.append.remote("exit")
        return batch


if __name__ == "__main__":
    ray.init(address="local")
    recorder = Recorder.remote()

    # Test multithreaded actor
    threaded_actor = ray.remote(Actor).options(max_concurrency=2).remote(recorder)
    threaded_actor.__call__.remote(None)
    ray.get(threaded_actor.__call__.remote(None))
    print(f"Multithreaded actor with max_concurrency=2: {ray.get(recorder.get_seq.remote())}")

    # Test async actor
    async_actor = ray.remote(AsyncActor).options(max_concurrency=2).remote(recorder)
    async_actor.__call__.remote(None)
    ray.get(async_actor.__call__.remote(None))
    print(f"Async actor with max_concurrency=2: {ray.get(recorder.get_seq.remote())}")

    texts = list(range(10))
    dataset = ray.data.from_items(texts)

    # Test map_batches on a sync actor with max_concurrency=2
    dataset.map_batches(Actor, fn_constructor_args=(recorder,),
                        concurrency=1,
                        batch_format="pyarrow", batch_size=5,
                        max_concurrency=2).materialize()
    print(f"map_batches on a sync Actor with max_concurrency=2: {ray.get(recorder.get_seq.remote())}")

    # Test map_batches on an async actor with max_concurrency=2
    dataset.map_batches(AsyncActor, fn_constructor_args=(recorder,),
                        concurrency=1,
                        batch_format="pyarrow", batch_size=5,
                        max_concurrency=2).materialize()
    print(f"map_batches on an async Actor with max_concurrency=2: {ray.get(recorder.get_seq.remote())}")

```

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#55354.title: [Data] map_batches on a threaded actor shows incorrect concurrency

ray-project/ray#55392.body: ### What happened + What you expected to happen When cleaning up a ray cluster by running `ray down` with the Azure provider, some resources are not cleaned up. specifically, there are managed identity, virtual network, and network security group object left around ex: These objects have cluster-specific identifiers in them so I'm pretty sure they should get cleaned up along with the cluster Ex: from the cached cluster config I see this ```bash on/ray$ cat /tmp/ray-config-01cb007290805aeeac3694abb554fb46eae36aeb | jq { "_version": 1, "provider_log_info": null, "config": { "cluster_name": "nightly-cpu-minimal-1754603045", "max_workers": 2, "upscaling_speed": 1.0, "docker": {}, "idle_timeout_minutes": 5, "provider": { "cache_stopped_nodes": false, "location": "westus2", "resource_group": "ray-nightly-cpu-minimal", "type": "azure", "subscription_id": "xxx", "unique_id": "dff1", "msi": "/subscriptions/xxx/resourceGroups/ray-nightly-cpu-minimal/providers/Microsoft.ManagedIdentity/userAssignedIdentities/ray-nightly-cpu-minimal-1754603045-dff1-msi", "nsg": "/subscriptions/xxx/resourceGroups/ray-nightly-cpu-minimal/providers/Microsoft.Network/networkSecurityGroups/ray-nightly-cpu-minimal-1754603045-dff1-nsg", "subnet": "/subscriptions/xxx/resourceGroups/ray-nightly-cpu-minimal/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/ray-nightly-cpu-minimal-1754603045-dff1-vnet/subnets/ray-nightly-cpu-minimal-1754603045-dff1-subnet" }, "auth": { "ssh_private_key": "xxx", "ssh_public_key": "xxx", "ssh_user": "ubuntu" }, ``` ### Versions / Dependencies ray @ main ### Reproduction script run `ray up` with an azure cluster config then run `ray down` ### Issue Severity Low: It annoys or frustrates me.
ray-project/ray#55392.title: [Core] [Azure] - Some azure resources are not cleaned up during ray down

ray-project/ray#55406.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The `max_model_len` field in `engine_kwargs` is not respected in Ray Serve LLM nightly builds when serving a config via `serve run`. The same field works with identical configuration via Python `serve.run` API. It's clear that the field is not applying to the vLLM engine in the CLI case because the engine fails to start due to OOM.

# working.py
# To run: python working.py
from ray import serve
from ray.serve.llm import LLMConfig, build_openai_app

llm_config = LLMConfig(
    model_loading_config=dict(
        model_id="meta-llama/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct",
        model_source="meta-llama/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct",
    ),
    engine_kwargs=dict(
        max_model_len=8000,
    ),
    runtime_env=dict(env_vars={"VLLM_NO_USAGE_STATS": "1"})
)

app = build_openai_app({"llm_configs": [llm_config]})
serve.run(app, blocking=True)

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#55406.title: [LLM] vLLM `engine_kwargs` via `serve run config.yaml` is not respected in nightly

ray-project/ray#55419.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

In the case of using multiple learners with offline data, `OfflineData.sample(...)` will split the data using `ray.data.Dataset.streaming_split` and then attempt to take the next batch from the returned list of iterators with `return next(self.batch_iterators)` which causes a crash.

```
return next(self.batch_iterators)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
TypeError: 'list' object is not an iterator
```

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/ddec97636b0403cb59f3fb670b28955fac50049f/rllib/offline/offline_data.py#L266


### Versions / Dependencies

ray 2.48.0

### Reproduction script

```python
import os
import typing as tp
import uuid

import gymnasium as gym
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.parquet as pq
import ray
from ray.rllib.algorithms.bc import BCConfig
from ray.rllib.env.single_agent_episode import SingleAgentEpisode
from ray.rllib.offline.offline_prelearner import OfflinePreLearner, SCHEMA
from ray.rllib.utils.typing import EpisodeType
from gymnasium.spaces import Box, Discrete


ray.init()

algo_config = BCConfig()

algo_config.api_stack(
    enable_env_runner_and_connector_v2=True,
    enable_rl_module_and_learner=True,
)

algo_config.framework(
    framework="torch", torch_compile_learner=True, torch_compile_worker=True
)

algo_config.rl_module(
    model_config={
        "conv_filters": [[32, [3, 3], 1], [64, [3, 3], 1], [64, [3, 3], 1]],
        "conv_activation": "relu",
        "head_fcnet_hiddens": [256],
    }
)

algo_config.training(
    gamma=0.99,
    lr=0.001,
    grad_clip=20,
    train_batch_size_per_learner=128,
    minibatch_size=500,
    num_epochs=5,
)

algo_config.environment(
    "DummyEnv",
    env_config={
        "observation_space": Box(
            low=0, high=1.0, shape=(9, 9, 198), dtype=np.float32
        ),
        "action_space": Discrete(144),
        "normalize_actions": False,
    },
    observation_space=Box(low=0, high=1.0, shape=(9, 9, 198), dtype=np.float32),
    action_space=Discrete(144),
)

algo_config.learners(
    num_learners=4,
    num_cpus_per_learner=1,
    num_gpus_per_learner=0,
)

class CustomOfflinePreLearner(OfflinePreLearner):
    @staticmethod
    def _map_to_episodes(
        is_multi_agent: bool,
        batch: tp.Dict[str, tp.Union[list, np.ndarray]],
        schema: tp.Dict[str, str] = SCHEMA,
        to_numpy: bool = False,
        input_compress_columns: tp.Optional[tp.List[str]] = None,
        ignore_final_observation: tp.Optional[bool] = False,
        observation_space: gym.Space = None,
        action_space: gym.Space = None,
    ) -> tp.Dict[str, tp.List[EpisodeType]]:
        episodes = []
        for _ in range(100):
            observation = observation_space.sample()
            action = action_space.sample()
            episode = SingleAgentEpisode(
                id_=uuid.uuid4().hex,
                observations=[observation, observation],
                actions=[action],
                rewards=[0.0],  # dummy reward for BC
                len_lookback_buffer=0,
            )
            episodes.append(episode)
        
        return {"episodes": episodes}


# Create a dummy parquet file.
n_examples = 100
dummy_data = {
    "observations": [np.random.rand(9, 9, 198).astype(np.float32).tolist() for _ in range(n_examples)],
    "actions": [np.random.randint(0, 144) for _ in range(n_examples)],
    "rewards": [0.0 for _ in range(n_examples)],
}

dummy_df = pd.DataFrame(dummy_data)
dummy_table = pa.Table.from_pandas(dummy_df)
pq.write_table(dummy_table, "/tmp/dummy.parquet")

algo_config.offline_data(
    input_="/tmp/dummy.parquet",
    input_read_method="read_parquet",
    input_read_episodes=False,
    prelearner_class=CustomOfflinePreLearner,
    dataset_num_iters_per_learner=1,
    map_batches_kwargs={"concurrency": 1, "num_cpus": 1},
)

algo = algo_config.build_algo()

for _ in range(10):
    algo.train()

algo.stop()
```

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#55419.title: [RLlib] `OfflineData.sample(...)` broken for multiple learners

ray-project/ray#55437.body:
Profiling links (StackTrace/CPU Profiling/Memory Profiling) currently use IP as the identifier for nodes. This becomes problematic in environments like hostnetwork, where multiple nodes can share the same IP. This can cause confusion and incorrect identification of nodes, especially when monitoring and profiling performance.
To address this, we propose using Node ID as the consistent identifier for nodes instead of IP. The Node ID is unique per node and will avoid the issues introduced by multiple nodes sharing the same IP address.
Changes:
- Updated ProfilingLink to use Node ID for identifying nodes, instead of IP.
- Adjusted relevant parts of the codebase to ensure consistency.

Note: This change should not affect non-hostnetwork environments, as the use of Node ID and IP does not differ for those cases.
ray-project/ray#55437.title: [Core][Dashboard]Remove IP as Node Identifier in ProfilingLink and Use Ray Node ID Instead

ray-project/ray#55452.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

When running a MultiAgentEnvRunner in collect episodes-mode there's an early-out termination of a for loop in _sample:

```
                    # Also early-out if we reach the number of episodes within this
                    # for-loop.
                    if eps == num_episodes:
                        break
```

This causes the code to skip the creation of a new episode but it also causes done_episodes_to_run_env_to_module to be a subset of the episodes in episodes. Normally, they would be disjoint since episodes would have re-created episodes in those slots. This causes a problem because slightly later in the call, env-to-module connectors are called, first with done_episodes_to_run_env_to_module, then with episodes:
```
if done_episodes_to_run_env_to_module:
                    # Run the env-to-module connector pipeline for all done episodes.
                    # Note, this is needed to postprocess last-step data, e.g. if the
                    # user uses a connector that one-hot encodes observations.
                    # Note, this pipeline run is not timed as the number of episodes
                    # can differ from `num_envs_per_env_runner` and would bias time
                    # measurements.
                    self._env_to_module(
                        episodes=done_episodes_to_run_env_to_module,
                        explore=explore,
                        rl_module=self.module,
                        shared_data=shared_data,
                        metrics=None,
                    )
                self._cached_to_module = self._env_to_module(
                    episodes=episodes,
                    explore=explore,
                    rl_module=self.module,
                    shared_data=shared_data,
                    metrics=self.metrics,
                    metrics_prefix_key=(ENV_TO_MODULE_CONNECTOR,),
                )
```
This causes connectors to have to deal with information they have already processed, for the done episodes.



### Versions / Dependencies

We use ray[rllib]==2.44.1 but the issue seems present on Github.

### Reproduction script

See description above.

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#55452.title: [Rllib] MultiAgentEnvRunner in episodes mode calls connectors one time too many.

ray-project/ray#55511.body:
### Description

GPU object refs passed back to the same actor are passed as a mutable tensor, so if the user task writes to the tensor, it will also write Ray's "copy" of the tensor. If the tensor then gets passed to another actor, then that actor will receive the updated copy instead of the original. This is different from Ray's usual immutable semantics, so we should warn the user if it happens.

### Use case

```python
class Actor:
  @ray.method(tensor_transport="gloo")
  def tensor(self):
    return torch.Tensor(...)

  def increment(self, t):
    t += 1

a, b = Actor.remote(), Actor.remote()
create_collective_group([a, b], backend="torch_gloo")
t = a.tensor.remote()
t1 = a.increment.remote(t)
t2 = b.increment.remote(t)
assert torch.allclose(ray.get([t1, t2]))
```
ray-project/ray#55511.title: [core][gpu-objects] Warn if GPU object ref is passed both intra- and inter-actor

ray-project/ray#55574.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Based on documentation, runai_streamer should be supported as a load format in order to stream the model weights from s3 into memory directly. However, when we launch vLLM engines from Ray's documentation here with runai_streamer (https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/working-with-llms.html), the model is still being downloaded to disk, which leads to significant startup overhead. 

The code that forces model downloading without taking in consideration of whether or not we specified the streaming load format is here:
https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/5f624859ab51c5d6759d8d4f3ab503ecfd0bc07c/python/ray/llm/_internal/batch/stages/vllm_engine_stage.py#L465-L470

I propose that we should check in engine_kwargs whether or not load_format is specified, and if it is specified as "runai_streamer", we should skip the download of the safetensor files (we probably still need to download the config.json, other metadata files). 

### Versions / Dependencies

ray version 2.48.0
python 3.11
Using the official docker image from dockerhub. 

### Reproduction script

```python
import ray
from ray.data.llm import vLLMEngineProcessorConfig, build_llm_processor
import numpy as np

config = vLLMEngineProcessorConfig(
    model_source="s3://path/to/model/",
    engine_kwargs={
        "enable_chunked_prefill": True,
        "max_num_batched_tokens": 4096,
        "max_model_len": 16384,
        "load_format": "runai_streamer"

    },
    concurrency=1,
    batch_size=64,
)
processor = build_llm_processor(
    config,
    preprocess=lambda row: dict(
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "You are a bot that responds with haikus."},
            {"role": "user", "content": row["item"]}
        ],
        sampling_params=dict(
            temperature=0.3,
            max_tokens=250,
        )
    ),
    postprocess=lambda row: dict(
        answer=row["generated_text"],
        **row  # This will return all the original columns in the dataset.
    ),
)

ds = ray.data.from_items(["Start of the haiku is: Complete this for me..."])

ds = processor(ds)
ds.show(limit=1)
```

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#55574.title: [Data/LLM] vLLM model files are downloaded to disk even when "load_format": "runai_streamer" is specified in engine_kwargs

│ 2025-08-19 05:27:20,181 - WARNING - Instance 37eab9d2-6dc2-4492-bbf8-afffad536b98(QUEUED) is stuck in QUEUED for 14148.0 seconds.                                                                                                                                           │

```
concurrency –
The semantics of this argument depend on the type of fn:
If fn is a function and concurrency isn’t set (default), the actual concurrency is implicitly determined by the available resources and number of input blocks.
If fn is a function and concurrency is an int n, Ray Data launches at most n concurrent tasks.
If fn is a class and concurrency is an int n, Ray Data uses an actor pool with exactly n workers.
If fn is a class and concurrency is a tuple (m, n), Ray Data uses an autoscaling actor pool from m to n workers.
If fn is a class and concurrency isn’t set (default), this method raises an error.

ray-project/ray#55786.body:
## Summary

Currently, Ray Serve deployments have hardcoded retry limits for constructor failures, controlled by environment variables `MAX_DEPLOYMENT_CONSTRUCTOR_RETRY_COUNT` (default: 20) and `MAX_PER_REPLICA_RETRY_COUNT` (default: 3). This creates operational challenges, especially in environments like Anyscale workspaces where changing environment variables requires rebuilding images and restarting clusters.

## Problem

When a deployment constructor fails, Ray Serve automatically retries up to 20 times (or `min(num_replicas * 3, 20)`). This behavior:

1. **Hides root causes**: Multiple retries can mask the actual deployment failure reason
2. **Causes operational overhead**: In stateful programs, excessive retries can complicate debugging
3. **Lacks configurability**: Users cannot easily adjust retry behavior per deployment
4. **Requires cluster restarts**: Environment variable changes need image rebuilds and cluster restarts

## Proposed Solution

Add `max_constructor_retries` as a configurable parameter in the deployment configuration schema, similar to other deployment options like `max_ongoing_requests` or `health_check_timeout_s`.

### Implementation Details

**1. Schema Changes** (`python/ray/serve/schema.py`)
Add to `DeploymentSchema`:
```python
max_constructor_retries: int = Field(
    default=DEFAULT.VALUE,
    description=(
        "Maximum number of times to retry deployment constructor failures. "
        "Defaults to min(num_replicas * 3, 20) if not specified."
    ),
    ge=0,
)
```

**2. Deployment State Logic** (`python/ray/serve/_private/deployment_state.py`)
Modify `_failed_to_start_threshold` property to use deployment config:
```python
@property
def _failed_to_start_threshold(self) -> int:
    if self._target_state.deployment_config.max_constructor_retries is not DEFAULT.VALUE:
        return self._target_state.deployment_config.max_constructor_retries
    return min(
        MAX_DEPLOYMENT_CONSTRUCTOR_RETRY_COUNT,
        self._target_state.target_num_replicas * MAX_PER_REPLICA_RETRY_COUNT,
    )
```

**3. Deployment Config Updates**
Update `DeploymentConfig` class to include the new field and ensure it's properly serialized/deserialized.

## Benefits

- **Per-deployment control**: Each deployment can have its own retry configuration
- **Operational simplicity**: No need to restart clusters to change retry behavior
- **Better debugging**: Reduce retries for stateful deployments to surface issues faster
- **Backward compatibility**: Maintains current behavior when not specified

## Related Code

- Current retry logic: `python/ray/serve/_private/deployment_state.py:1545-1549`
- Environment variables: `python/ray/serve/_private/constants.py:57-63`
- Deployment schema: `python/ray/serve/schema.py:282-450`

## Use Cases

1. **Development/Testing**: Set `max_constructor_retries=0` to fail fast and see issues immediately
2. **Stateful Applications**: Limit retries to avoid state corruption or resource leaks
3. **Production Deployments**: Fine-tune retry behavior per service requirements

This enhancement would align with Ray Serve's goal of providing fine-grained deployment control while maintaining operational simplicity.

## Context

This issue was requested in the Ray community forum: https://discuss.ray.io/t/retries-for-deployments/20805

The current hardcoded retry mechanism causes particular pain points in managed environments where environment variable changes require significant operational overhead.

ray-project/ray#55837.body: ### Background Ray proactively triggers gc.collect() on idle workers to release Python objects that may still hold Plasma shared memory (shm) references. In the current implementation in (`_raylet.pyx gc_collect()`), Ray calls `gc.collect()` from Cython under a `with gil` block periodically. If the Python object graph is complex (e.g., cyclic references with finalizers), `gc.collect()` may take a long time. During this period, since the GIL is held for the entire collection, user code is completely frozen if `gc.collect()` time is longer than the periodic interval (e.g., 10s). Code to reproduce the problem, we can see `[ping] timeout` due to gc.collect() blocked by `HeavyFinalizer.__del__()`. ``` import gc import time import ray @ray.remote class GarbageMaker: def __init__(self): gc.disable() def produce(self): class HeavyFinalizer: def __del__(self): time.sleep(1.0) junk = [] for _ in range(100): a, b = HeavyFinalizer(), HeavyFinalizer() a.peer = b b.peer = a junk.append((a, b)) junk = None print("[produce] created objects with heavy finalizers") return 1 def ping(self) -> int: return 1 if __name__ == "__main__": ray.init( num_cpus=1, _system_config={ "local_gc_interval_s": 1, "local_gc_min_interval_s": 0, "global_gc_min_interval_s": 0, }, ) actor = GarbageMaker.remote() ray.get(actor.produce.remote()) deadline = time.time() + 10 while time.time() < deadline: try: ray.get(actor.ping.remote(), timeout=1) print("[ping] success") except ray.exceptions.GetTimeoutError: print("[ping] timeout <-- indicates local GC blocked the worker") time.sleep(1) ray.shutdown() ``` ### Analysis - `CoreWorker::HandleLocalGC` → calls into Python binding `gc_collect` in `_raylet.pyx`. - `gc_collect` directly executes `gc.collect()` while holding the GIL. - If finalizers (del/dealloc) or complex cycles exist, this can take arbitrarily long. - Since RPC IOService keeps pushing new LocalGC requests, the worker stays trapped in GC calls, starving user code. It is worth noting that long `gc.collect()` times are often due to user code issues (e.g., expensive logic in `__del__` or complicated object cycles). However, because Ray currently invokes gc.collect() from its own RPC handler, the freeze appears inside Ray’s code path. To users, this looks like “Ray is stuck,” even though the root cause is user objects. ### Proposed Solution We propose decoupling GC execution from the RPC call: 1. `gc_collect` in Cython should not directly run `gc.collect()`. 2. Instead, it should "signal an event" with minimum execution time (e.g., using a threading.Event or similar). 3. A dedicated Python GC thread consumes this event and executes `gc.collect()` asynchronously, with a configurable GC interval. This design has several benefits: - Prevents gc.collect() from blocking user tasks, since GIL is not held by CoreWorker RPC. - Allows rate-limiting and smarter scheduling of GC (e.g., skip if too frequent). - More robust in production where finalizers can take unbounded time.
ray-project/ray#55837.title: [Core] gc_collect() in _raylet.pyx blocks user program due to GIL hold

ray-project/ray#55858.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

#### Background

In the `JobManager`, the role of `_monitor_job` (and `_monitor_job_internal`) is to monitor the status of every non-terminal job in the cluster until it reaches a terminal state. It is triggered in the following scenarios:

- When the `job_manager` is initialized (e.g., when a new raylet is created), it is triggered by `_recover_running_jobs`.
- After a job is submitted, it is triggered to monitor the status of that specific job.

The [_monitor_job_interna](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/4bfee4b062d8b1ac3a873592f1b298a0dd0b4558/python/ray/dashboard/modules/job/job_manager.py#L349) method includes cleanup logic to prevent orphaned job supervisor actors: 

```python
if job_supervisor is not None:
    ray.kill(job_supervisor, no_restart=True)
```

The cleanup logic is intended to prevent actor leaks by ensuring the supervisor is terminated even in unexpected scenarios. 

#### Issue Description

However, we've identified a case where this cleanup fails to execute:

1. A job is submitted but remains in `PENDING` state.
2. The node that submitted the job has died. At the same time, a new node is created and the raylet on new node (new raylet) is created.
3. During the new raylet's startup process, the job reaches its `DEFAULT_JOB_START_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` threshold
4. The job manager on the new raylet detects the timeout and updates the job status to `FAILED`
5. **Leak occurs:** In the loop `_monitor_job_internal` for determining job Pending status, during the first iteration of the loop, the loop exits prematurely due to job timeout. 
6. **Leak consequences:** This causes the `job_supervisor` handle in the new raylet to be `None`. So the cleanup logic skips termination, potentially leaving residual job_supervisor actor artifacts and preventing proper resource release.



### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.46


### Issue Severity

Normal, but I'll fix it.
ray-project/ray#55858.title: [Dashboard] Job supervisor actor leak when job times out during new raylet startup

### What happened + What you expected to happen

This error message was added in recent Ray releases (https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/52380). When working with large datasets it completely overwhelms your terminal. As a secondary issue, it emits data contents, which hasn't been done elsewhere in Ray before and presents a mild risk of PII exposure depending on how you ingest and consume logs internally.

We need a flag to turn this off or smarter truncation. 

### Versions / Dependencies

See original PR.

n/a

None

ray-project/ray#56125.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

When using multimodal/vision models in batch processing, image inputs via `url` or `data:` do not work if the message doesn't include a prompt with direct content. (i.e "content": "You are an assistant" vs "content": [...] )

https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/working-with-llms.html#batch-inference-with-vision-language-model-vlm

This input with and without the system prompt leads to inconsistent image preparation behaviour:

```python
messages=[
    # {"role": "system", "content": "You are an assistant"},
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": [
            {
                "type": "image",
                "image": "https://i.scdn.co/image/ab67616d0000b27331efa547b5c271f00bcde9b9",
            },
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Can you describe this image in {row['id']} words?",
            },
        ],
    },
],
```

With a system prompt direct content (i.e 'content': 'You are an assistant' rather than an content array) → Model can see image, responses are reasonable, and row `messages` is preserved and include image_sizes
```
{'resp': ' In the image we can see there are three people and a dog and there is a text on it.',
 'messages': [{'role': 'system', 'content': 'You are an assistant'},
  {'role': 'user',
   'content': [{'type': 'image',
     'image': 'https://i.scdn.co/image/ab67616d0000b27331efa547b5c271f00bcde9b9'},
    {'type': 'text', 'text': 'Can you describe this image in 1 words?'}]}],
 'image_sizes': [[640, 640]],
 'prompt': '<|im_start|>System: <end_of_utterance>\nUser:<image>Can you describe this image in 1 words?<end_of_utterance>\nAssistant:',
 ...

```

Without a system prompt → Model outputs nonsensical responses (Larger models like `google/gemma-3-4b-it` will output that it can't see an image.), `image_sizes` is missing, and messages content is mutated (adding  `'text': None` and `'image': None`). You can add bad data as the image content (i.e inconsistent types or invalid url) and it doesn't fail.

```
 {'resp': ' The image depicts a scene from a historical or fictional narrative, likely involving a battle or conflict between two groups of people. The background is blurred, focusing attention on the foreground, which is a group of people standing in a group. The people are dressed',
'messages': [{'content': [{'image': 'https://i.scdn.co/image/ab67616d0000b27331efa547b5c271f00bcde9b9',
     'text': None,
     'type': 'image'},
    {'image': None,
     'text': 'Can you describe this image in 1 words?',
     'type': 'text'}],
   'role': 'user'}],
 'prompt': '<|im_start|>User:<image>Can you describe this image in 1 words?<end_of_utterance>\nAssistant:',
...
```

### Versions / Dependencies

python 3.11
ray==2.49.0
vllm==0.10.0


### Reproduction script

```python
!pip install "ray==2.49.0"
!pip install "vllm==0.10.0"

import ray
from packaging.version import Version
from ray.data.llm import build_llm_processor, vLLMEngineProcessorConfig

config = vLLMEngineProcessorConfig(
    model_source="HuggingFaceTB/SmolVLM-256M-Instruct",
    task_type="generate",
    engine_kwargs=dict(
        # Skip CUDA graph capturing to reduce startup time.
        enforce_eager=True,
        # CI uses T4 GPU which does not support bfloat16.
        dtype="half",
    ),
    # CI uses T4 GPU which is not supported by vLLM v1 FlashAttn.
    runtime_env=dict(
        env_vars=dict(
            VLLM_USE_V1="0",
        ),
    ),
    apply_chat_template=True,
    has_image=True,
    tokenize=True,
    detokenize=True,
    batch_size=16,
    accelerator_type="T4",
    concurrency=1,
)

processor = build_llm_processor(
    config,
    preprocess=lambda row: dict(
        messages=[
            # {"role": "system", "content": "You are an assistant"},
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": [
                    {
                        "type": "image",
                        "image": "https://i.scdn.co/image/ab67616d0000b27331efa547b5c271f00bcde9b9",
                    },
                    {
                        "type": "text",
                        "text": f"Can you describe this image in {row['id']} words?",
                    },
                ],
            },
        ],
        sampling_params=dict(
            temperature=0.3,
            max_tokens=50,
        ),
    ),
    postprocess=lambda row: {
        "resp": row["generated_text"],
        **row,
    },
)

ds = ray.data.range(3)
ds = ds.map(lambda x: {"id": x["id"], "val": x["id"] + 5})
ds = processor(ds)
ds = ds.materialize()
outs = ds.take_all()
```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#56125.title: [Data] multimodal vision batch inference skips the image preparation step

ray-project/ray#56271.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The 'evaluation_parallel_to_training' feature does not work properly when the 'step' call of an environment is taking a long time. This leads to sequential stepping of train -> eval -> train -> eval rather than paralell steps [train,eval] -> [train,eval]. 

This method still works properly on the old-api-stack. 

The below reproduction script shows this behavior. The script is based on the evaluation_parallel_to_training.py example script, but the environment is replaced to a simple example that sleeps for 5 seconds in the step call. When running this for 4 steps we get the following outputs:

**New API Stack**
```
+---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------+-------------+
| Trial name          | status     | loc                  |   iter |   total time (s) |   ts |   combined return |   return p0 |
|---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------+-------------|
| PPO_env_1a881_00000 | TERMINATED | 172.21.137.227:13070 |      4 |          40.0991 |    4 |                 1 |           1 |
+---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------+-------------+
```

**Old API Stack**
```
+---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------+
| Trial name          | status     | loc                  |   iter |   total time (s) |   ts |   combined return |
|---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------|
| PPO_env_537d5_00000 | TERMINATED | 172.21.137.227:19015 |      4 |          25.0681 |    4 |                 1 |
+---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------+
```

We can see that:
* New API: 40 seconds (matches: 5 seconds * 4 iterations * 2 (train/eval sequential)
* old API : 25 seconds (matches: 5 seconds * 4 iterations * 1 (train/eval parallel) + something.

Doing some digging through the stack leads to the following function that hangs until the 'train' step is complete before the 'eval' step gets executed.  https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/ray-2.49.1/rllib/env/env_runner_group.py#L646


The below script reproduces the problem, it can be run as-is against the latest release.

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.49.1  (and earlier).
Tested on Linux / Python 3.12

### Reproduction script

```python
"""Example showing how one can set up evaluation running in parallel to training.

Reproduction example of evaluation in parallel not working on the new api stack

When running with the new API stack:

RAY_DEDUP_LOGS=0 python evaluation_parallel_to_training.py --num-agents 1 --stop-iters=4  \
    --evaluation-parallel-to-training --evaluation-num-env-runners=1 --evaluation-duration=1 \
    --num-env-runners 1 --evaluation-duration-unit "timesteps"  --evaluation-interval 1 \
    --num-gpus-per-learner 0

+---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------+-------------+
| Trial name          | status     | loc                  |   iter |   total time (s) |   ts |   combined return |   return p0 |
|---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------+-------------|
| PPO_env_1a881_00000 | TERMINATED | 172.21.137.227:13070 |      4 |          40.0991 |    4 |                 1 |           1 |
+---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------+-------------+

When running with the old API stack

RAY_DEDUP_LOGS=0 python evaluation_parallel_to_training.py --num-agents 1 --stop-iters=4  \
    --evaluation-parallel-to-training --evaluation-num-env-runners=1 --evaluation-duration=1 \
    --num-env-runners 1 --evaluation-duration-unit "timesteps"  --evaluation-interval 1 \
    --num-gpus-per-learner 0 --old-api-stack

Number of trials: 1/1 (1 TERMINATED)
+---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------+
| Trial name          | status     | loc                  |   iter |   total time (s) |   ts |   combined return |
|---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------|
| PPO_env_537d5_00000 | TERMINATED | 172.21.137.227:19015 |      4 |          25.0681 |    4 |                 1 |
+---------------------+------------+----------------------+--------+------------------+------+-------------------+

Code appears to hang in this ray.get call:
https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/ray-2.49.1/rllib/env/env_runner_group.py#L646
"""

import time
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional, Union

import gymnasium as gym
import numpy as np
from ray.rllib.env.multi_agent_env import make_multi_agent
from ray.rllib.utils.test_utils import (
    add_rllib_example_script_args,
    run_rllib_example_script_experiment,
)
from ray.tune.registry import get_trainable_cls, register_env
from ray.tune.result import TRAINING_ITERATION

parser = add_rllib_example_script_args(
    default_timesteps=20000000,
    default_reward=500000.0,
)
parser.set_defaults(
    evaluation_num_env_runners=2,
    evaluation_interval=1,
)


class MyTestEnv(gym.Env[np.ndarray, Union[int, np.ndarray]]):
    def __init__(self, config):
        print("INIT called: ", config)
        high = np.array([np.inf, np.inf, np.inf, np.inf], dtype=np.float32)
        self.action_space = gym.spaces.Discrete(2)
        self.observation_space = gym.spaces.Box(-high, high, dtype=np.float32)
        self.step_count = 0
        self.mode = config.get("mode", "TRAIN")

    def step(self, action):
        print(f"STEP - {self.step_count} - {self.mode} ! {datetime.now()}")
        time.sleep(5)
        self.step_count += 1
        return self.observation_space.sample(), 1, True, False, {}

    def reset(self, *, seed: Optional[int] = None, options: Optional[dict] = None):
        super().reset(seed=seed)
        print(f"RESET - {self.step_count} - {self.mode} ! {datetime.now()}")
        return self.observation_space.sample(), {}


MultiAgentMyTestEnv = make_multi_agent(lambda c: MyTestEnv(c))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Register our environment with tune.
    if args.num_agents > 0:
        register_env("env", lambda c: MultiAgentMyTestEnv(c))
    else:
        raise ValueError("Test run requires at least 1 agent.")

    base_config = (
        get_trainable_cls(args.algo)
        .get_default_config()
        .environment("env" if args.num_agents > 0 else "CartPole-v1")
        .env_runners(create_env_on_local_worker=True)
        .evaluation(
            evaluation_parallel_to_training=args.evaluation_parallel_to_training,
            evaluation_num_env_runners=args.evaluation_num_env_runners,
            evaluation_interval=args.evaluation_interval,
            evaluation_duration=args.evaluation_duration,
            evaluation_duration_unit=args.evaluation_duration_unit,
            evaluation_config={
                "explore": False,
                "metrics_num_episodes_for_smoothing": 1,
                "evaluation_duration": 1,
                "env_config": {"mode": "eval"},
            },
        )
    )

    # Add a simple multi-agent setup.
    if args.num_agents > 0:
        base_config.multi_agent(
            policies={f"p{i}" for i in range(args.num_agents)},
            policy_mapping_fn=lambda aid, *a, **kw: f"p{aid}",
        )
    # Set some PPO-specific tuning settings to learn better in the env (assumed to be
    # CartPole-v1).
    if args.algo == "PPO":
        base_config.training(
            lr=0.0003, num_epochs=1, vf_loss_coeff=0.01, minibatch_size=1, train_batch_size=1
        )

    run_rllib_example_script_experiment(
        base_config,
        args,
        stop={TRAINING_ITERATION: args.stop_iters}
    )
```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#56271.title: [RLLib] 'evaluation_parallel_to_training' does not work properly in new API stack

ray-project/ray#56304.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

In `ray/rllib/algorithms/algorithm_config.py` (around line 4546), the construction of a `MultiRLModuleSpec` fails to pass along the `model_config`, which leads to missing configuration during runtime. The current snippet is:

```python
# Now construct the proper MultiRLModuleSpec. 
# We need to infer the multi-agent class from `current_rl_module_spec`
# and fill in the module_specs dict.
multi_rl_module_spec = current_rl_module_spec.__class__(
    multi_rl_module_class=current_rl_module_spec.multi_rl_module_class,
    rl_module_specs=module_specs,
    modules_to_load=current_rl_module_spec.modules_to_load,
    load_state_path=current_rl_module_spec.load_state_path,
)
```

However, it should probably be:

```python
# Now construct the proper MultiRLModuleSpec. 
# We need to infer the multi-agent class from `current_rl_module_spec`
# and fill in the module_specs dict.
multi_rl_module_spec = current_rl_module_spec.__class__(
    multi_rl_module_class=current_rl_module_spec.multi_rl_module_class,
    rl_module_specs=module_specs,
    modules_to_load=current_rl_module_spec.modules_to_load,
    load_state_path=current_rl_module_spec.load_state_path,
    model_config=current_rl_module_spec.model_config
)
```

I was trying to use a custom `MultiRLModule` that needed some global configurations and I was unable to pass it to the env_runners by using the model_config argument, causing the `Algorithm.build_algo` method to fail. Adding the line fixed the problem.


### Versions / Dependencies

- **Ray version**: 2.48.0
- **Python version**: 3.10

### Reproduction script

```python
from ray.rllib.core.rl_module.torch import TorchRLModule
from ray.rllib.core.rl_module import MultiRLModule, MultiRLModuleSpec, RLModuleSpec
from ray.rllib.algorithms import AlgorithmConfig, DQNConfig
from ray.rllib.env.multi_agent_env_runner import MultiAgentEnvRunner
from ray.rllib.examples.multi_agent.multi_agent_cartpole import MultiAgentCartPole
import gymnasium as gym

class CustomMultiRLModule(MultiRLModule):
    def setup(self):
        super().setup()
        assert self.model_config is not None

spec = MultiRLModuleSpec(
    multi_rl_module_class=CustomMultiRLModule,
    rl_module_specs={
        'agent_1': RLModuleSpec(
            TorchRLModule,
            observation_space=gym.spaces.Box(0, 1),
            action_space=gym.spaces.Box(0, 1)
        )
    },
    model_config={'some_config': 1}
)

# This works fine.
module = spec.build()

# This doesn't, because of the missing parameter in `AlgorithmConfig` class.
algo_config = (
    DQNConfig()
    .environment(MultiAgentCartPole)
    .rl_module(rl_module_spec=spec)
    .multi_agent(
        policies={'agent_1'},
        policy_mapping_fn=lambda agent_id, episode, worker, **kwargs: agent_id
    )
)
MultiAgentEnvRunner(algo_config)
```

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#56304.title: [RLlib] Missing model_config parameter when constructing MultiRLModuleSpec in algorithm_config.py

ray-project/ray#56424.body:
### Description

Currently, Ray’s multimodal/vision model support for batch processing is limited to images only.

🔗 [Documentation reference](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/working-with-llms.html#batch-inference-with-vision-language-model-vlm)

However, libraries like vLLM have multimodal support for video:
- https://github.com/QwenLM/Qwen2.5-VL?tab=readme-ov-file#inference-locally
- https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/features/multimodal_inputs.html#video-inputs
- https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/main/vllm/multimodal/utils.py#L277-L297

###  Problem
Today, if a user wants to batch process videos in Ray they would need to manually download and preprocess videos into frames and feed them as individual images. This preparation and preprocessing should be handled by Ray.

### Proposal
Extend the existing [PrepareImageStage](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/llm/_internal/batch/stages/prepare_image_stage.py) to handle additional media types, making it a more generalised PrepareMediaStage. (Alternative, could be adding a PrepareVideoStage)
ray-project/ray#56424.title: [Data][LLM] Extend Ray’s Batch Processing to Support Video Inputs for Multimodal Models

ray-project/ray#56529.body:
### Description

Daft's with_column API support an actor as a UDF

``` Python
# Define the return type for embeddings
embedding_type = daft.DataType.embedding(daft.DataType.float32(), ENCODING_DIM)

@daft.udf(
    return_dtype=embedding_type,
    concurrency=NUM_GPU_NODES,
    num_gpus=1,
    batch_size=BATCH_SIZE
)
class EncodingUDF:
    def __init__(self):
        from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

        device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
        self.model = SentenceTransformer(EMBEDDING_MODEL_NAME, device=device)
        self.model.compile()

    def __call__(self, text_col):
        embeddings = self.model.encode(
            text_col.to_pylist(),
            batch_size=SENTENCE_TRANSFORMER_BATCH_SIZE,
            convert_to_tensor=True,
            torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
        )
        return embeddings.cpu().numpy()


daft.read_parquet("s3://desmond-demo/text-embedding-dataset.parquet")
    .with_column("sentences", EncodingUDF(col("text")))
    .explode("sentences")
```

### Use case

For simple inference, I can currently use map_batches instead, but it would be good if Ray Data's with_column API could support Actors.
ray-project/ray#56529.title: [Ray Data] with_column API support Actor

ray-project/ray#56531.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

I observed an issue where log streaming becomes corrupted after log file rotation when viewed through the driver/kubectl (actual files on disk are showing logs correctly). 

The logs start showing truncated content until they eventually stop being streamed altogether, like in the following example:


```
(Counter pid=44404) Counter-8: count = 44
(Counter pid=44409) Counter-7: count = 44
(Counter pid=44408) Counter-6: count = 44
(Counter pid=44415) Counter-9: count = 44
(Counter pid=44405) Counter-5: count = 44
(Counter pid=44403) Counter-2: count = 45
(Counter pid=44407) Counter-1: count = 45
(Counter pid=44400) Counter-3: count = 45
(Counter pid=44412) Counter-4: count = 45
(Counter pid=44404) Counter-8: count = 45
(Counter pid=44409) Counter-7: count = 45
(Counter pid=44408) Counter-6: count = 45
(Counter pid=44415) Counter-9: count = 45
(Counter pid=44405) Counter-5: count = 45
(Counter pid=44411) 30
(Counter pid=44406) 30
(Counter pid=44414) 30
(Counter pid=44402) 30
(Counter pid=44413) 30
(Counter pid=44401) 30
(Counter pid=44410) 30
(Counter pid=44434) 30
(Counter pid=44436) 30
(Counter pid=44435) 30
(Counter pid=44433) 30
(Counter pid=44411) 
(Counter pid=44406) 
(Counter pid=44414) 
(Counter pid=44402) 
(Counter pid=44413) 
(Counter pid=44401) 
(Counter pid=44410) 
```

The log files keep showing the correct logs and rotate, as expected. 

From my investigation, in [log_monitor.py:85](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/f896b686f52fa46ec2ab2d75d4660b99b3f54c3f/python/ray/_private/log_monitor.py#L85), when reopen_if_necessary() detects an inode change (rotation), it reopens the file but seeks to the previous
file position. This was explicitly implemented in commit #27508.

Resetting the file position/not seeking to the last position seems to solve the issue for us and displays the logs correctly, even after rotation.

In the example, I set the RAY_ROTATION_MAX_BYTES to 1kb for faster reproduction.

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray: 2.49.1 (Also reproduced with 2.47.x and 2.48.x)
Python: 3.10.11
OS: Reproduced on MacOS 15.6.1 and Kubernetes 1.32.6 with KubeRay v1.1.1

### Reproduction script

`export RAY_ROTATION_MAX_BYTES=1024 && export RAY_ROTATION_BACKUP_COUNT=1 && export RAY_DEDUP_LOGS=0 && ray start --head`

```
import ray
import time

@ray.remote
class Counter:
    def __init__(self, name: str):
        self.name = name
        self.count = 0

    def increment(self):
        self.count += 1
        print(f"{self.name}: count = {self.count}")
        return self.count

def main():
    print("Starting Ray actors counting example")

    num_actors = 20

    counters = []
    for i in range(num_actors):
        counter = Counter.remote(f"Counter-{i+1}")
        counters.append(counter)

    print(f"Created {num_actors} counter actors")

    while True:
        for counter in counters:
            ray.get(counter.increment.remote())

        time.sleep(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    ray.init()
    try:
        main()
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print("Stopping counters...")
    finally:
        ray.shutdown()
```

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#56531.title: [Core] Log file rotation breaks log streaming

ray-project/ray#56578.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

 I'm using Autoscaler v2 for automatic scaling. Due to heavy load on my Kubernetes API server, AuthScaler v2 is stuck when executing the self._k8s_api_client.patch operation to modify the CRD. The Autoscaler v2 Reconciler times out:
"queue again to launch after timeout=600s".

View the Autoscaler v2 patch call code:
/ray-project/ray/python/ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/cloud_providers/kuberay/cloud_provider.py:

def _patch(self, remote_path: str, payload: List[Dict[str, Any]]) -> Dict[str, Any]: 
"""Patch a resource on the Kubernetes API server.""" 
return self._k8s_api_client.patch(remote_path, payload)

/ray-project/ray/python/ray/autoscaler/_private/kuberay/node_provider.py:
def patch(self, path: str, payload: List[Dict[str, Any]]) -> Dict[str, Any]: 
"""Wrapper for REST PATCH of resource with proper headers 

Args: 
path: The part of the resource path that starts with the resource type. 
payload: The JSON patch payload. 

Returns: 
The JSON response of the PATCH request. 

Raises: 
HTTPError: If the PATCH request fails. 
""" 
url = url_from_resource( 
namespace=self._namespace, 
path=path, kuberay_crd_version = self._kuberay_crd_version,
)
headers, verify = self._get_refreshed_headers_and_verify()
result = requests.patch(
url,
json.dumps(payload),
headers = {**headers, "Content-type": "application/json-patch+json"},
verify = verify,
)
if not result.status_code == 200:
result.raise_for_status()
return result.json()

I've found that requests.patch will hang around waiting for a response from Kubernetes until TCP or the autoscaler v2 times out (10 minutes).
I think this is unreasonable. Autoscaler v2 uses a patch to modify the crd file, which normally completes quickly. Therefore, requests.patch should have a timeout set. After the timeout expires, this function should return quickly. If the failure persists after three retries, the instance state will be changed to ALLOCATION_FAILED.
  

### Versions / Dependencies

ray v2.47

### Reproduction script

Under heavy load, the Kubernetes API server may experience slow request execution, which may lead to API server timeouts.

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#56578.title: [Core] A timeout should be set when submitting patch requests for autoscaler v2.

      request_routing_stats_period_s: 10
      request_routing_stats_timeout_s: 30
```

a lot of the newlines can be omitted. 

### Versions / Dependencies

2.49.1

### Reproduction script

```
from ray import serve
from ray.serve.request_router import ReplicaID
import time
from collections import defaultdict
from ray.serve.context import _get_internal_replica_context
from typing import Any, Dict
from ray.serve.config import RequestRouterConfig
# from custom_request_router import UniformRequestRouter
import random
from ray.serve.request_router import (
    PendingRequest,
    RequestRouter,
    ReplicaID,
    ReplicaResult,
    RunningReplica,
)
from typing import (
    List,
    Optional,
)


class UniformRequestRouter(RequestRouter):
    async def choose_replicas(
        self,
        candidate_replicas: List[RunningReplica],
        pending_request: Optional[PendingRequest] = None,
    ) -> List[List[RunningReplica]]:
        print("UniformRequestRouter routing request")
        index = random.randint(0, len(candidate_replicas) - 1)
        return [[candidate_replicas[index]]]

    def on_request_routed(
        self,
        pending_request: PendingRequest,
        replica_id: ReplicaID,
        result: ReplicaResult,
    ):
        print("on_request_routed callback is called!!")



@serve.deployment(
    request_router_config=RequestRouterConfig(
        request_router_class=UniformRequestRouter,
    ),
    num_replicas=10,
    ray_actor_options={"num_cpus": 0},
)
class UniformRequestRouterApp:
    def __init__(self):
        context = _get_internal_replica_context()
        self.replica_id: ReplicaID = context.replica_id

    async def __call__(self):
        return self.replica_id


app = UniformRequestRouterApp.bind()
```

`serve build app:app`

### Issue Severity

Low: It annoys or frustrates me.

I am not a Ray expert, but in this output the RequestContext is the same for both requests, including request_id and internal_request_id, which seems odd to me

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.48.0
Python 3.11.6


### Reproduction script

```python
from ray import serve
from fastapi import FastAPI
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger("ray.serve")

app = FastAPI()

@serve.deployment
class MultiplexedModel:
    @serve.multiplexed(max_num_models_per_replica=2)
    async def get_model(self, model_id: str):
        logger.info(f"Loading model: {model_id}")
        return f"model_obj_for_{model_id}"

    @serve.batch(max_batch_size=2, batch_wait_timeout_s=0.05, max_concurrent_batches=1)
    async def batched(self, lst):
        model_id = serve.get_multiplexed_model_id()
        logger.info(f"Request for model_id: {model_id}\n{serve.context._get_serve_request_context()}")
        model = await self.get_model(model_id)

        result = []
        for item in lst:
            result.append(f"Response from {model} {item}")
        return result

    async def non_batched(self, item):
        model_id = serve.get_multiplexed_model_id()
        logger.info(f"Request for model_id: {model_id}\n{serve.context._get_serve_request_context()}")
        model = await self.get_model(model_id)

        return f"Response from {model} {item}"

@serve.deployment
@serve.ingress(app)
class APIIngress:
    def __init__(self, model_handle) -> None:
        self.model_handle = model_handle

    @app.post("/predict")
    async def predict(self, model_id: str, arg: str, kind: str):
        logger.info(f"model_id {model_id}")
        if kind == "batched":
            return await self.model_handle.options(multiplexed_model_id=model_id).batched.remote(arg)
        else:
            return await self.model_handle.options(multiplexed_model_id=model_id).non_batched.remote(arg)

app = APIIngress.bind(MultiplexedModel.bind())
```

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#56633.title: [Serve] model multiplexing and batching does not work together

ray-project/ray#56731.body:
## What happened + What you expected to happen
### Description

Ray worker pod fails to start when configuring MemRay environment variables for memory profiling.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Set MemRay-related environment variables:
  
    ```shell
    export RAY_INTERNAL_MEM_PROFILE_COMPONENTS=dashboard_agent
    export RAY_INTERNAL_MEM_PROFILE_OPTIONS="--live,--live-port,12345,-q,--native"
    ```
2. Attempt to start the Ray process (worker pod):

    ```shell
    bash -c "$KUBERAY_GEN_RAY_START_CMD"
    ```
3. Observe startup failure and check logs at `/tmp/ray/session_latest/logs/raylet.err`

### Error Logs

```log
usage: memray run [-m module | -c cmd | file] [args]
memray run: error: argument --live: not allowed with argument -o/--output
[2025-09-18 09:46:22,564 E 72559 72617] (raylet) agent_manager.cc:87: The raylet exited immediately because one Ray agent failed, agent_name = dashboard_agent.
The raylet fate shares with the agent. This can happen because
- The version of `grpcio` doesn't follow Ray's requirement. Agent can segfault with the incorrect `grpcio` version. Check the grpcio version `pip freeze | grep grpcio`.
- The agent failed to start because of unexpected error or port conflict. Read the log `cat /tmp/ray/session_latest/logs/{dashboard_agent|runtime_env_agent}.log`. You can find the log file structure here https://docs.ray.io/en/master/ray-observability/user-guides/configure-logging.html#logging-directory-structure.
- The agent is killed by the OS (e.g., out of memory).
```

**Root Cause**: `memray run: error: argument --live: not allowed with argument -o/--output`

### Code Analysis

Relevant Ray source code for MemRay configuration and command construction:

```python
# Comma separated name of components that will run memory profiler.  
# Ray uses `memray` to memory profile internal components.  
# The name of the component must be one of ray_constants.PROCESS_TYPE*.  
RAY_MEMRAY_PROFILE_COMPONENT_ENV = "RAY_INTERNAL_MEM_PROFILE_COMPONENTS"  
# Options to specify for `memray run` command. See  
# `memray run --help` for more details.  
# Example:  
# RAY_INTERNAL_MEM_PROFILE_OPTIONS="--live,--live-port,3456,-q,"  
# -> `memray run --live --live-port 3456 -q`  
RAY_MEMRAY_PROFILE_OPTIONS_ENV = "RAY_INTERNAL_MEM_PROFILE_OPTIONS"

def _build_python_executable_command_memory_profileable(
    component: str, session_dir: str, unbuffered: bool = True
):
    """Build the Python executable command.
    It runs a memory profiler if env var is configured.
    Args:
        component: Name of the component. It must be one of
            ray_constants.PROCESS_TYPE*.
        session_dir: The directory name of the Ray session.
        unbuffered: If true, Python executable is started with unbuffered option.
            e.g., `-u`.
            It means the logs are flushed immediately (good when there's a failure),
            but writing to a log file can be slower.
    """
    command = [
        sys.executable,
    ]
    if unbuffered:
        command.append("-u")
    components_to_memory_profile = os.getenv(RAY_MEMRAY_PROFILE_COMPONENT_ENV, "")
    if not components_to_memory_profile:
        return command
    components_to_memory_profile = set(components_to_memory_profile.split(","))
    try:
        import memray  # noqa: F401
    except ImportError:
        raise ImportError(
            "Memray is required to memory profiler on components "
            f"{components_to_memory_profile}. Run `pip install memray`."
        )
    if component in components_to_memory_profile:
        session_dir = Path(session_dir)
        session_name = session_dir.name
        profile_dir = session_dir / "profile"
        profile_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
        output_file_path = profile_dir / f"{session_name}_memory_{component}.bin"
        options = os.getenv(RAY_MEMRAY_PROFILE_OPTIONS_ENV, None)
        options = options.split(",") if options else []
        # Ray appends `-o {output_file_path}` by default here
        command.extend(["-m", "memray", "run", "-o", str(output_file_path), *options])
    return command
```

### Key Finding

Ray automatically appends the `-o {output_file_path}` parameter when starting MemRay for profiling. However, MemRay's run command explicitly prohibits using the `--live` argument together with `-o`/`--output`, which causes the startup failure.

## Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.46

## Issue Severity

Medium. I can fix it.
ray-project/ray#56731.title: [Core] Ray Fails to Start After Configuring RAY_MEMRAY_PROFILE_OPTIONS_ENV for MemRay
