```
  File "python/ray/_raylet.pyx", line 2164, in ray._raylet.task_execution_handler
  File "python/ray/_raylet.pyx", line 2023, in ray._raylet.execute_task_with_cancellation_handler
  File "python/ray/_raylet.pyx", line 1606, in ray._raylet.execute_task
AttributeError: 'dict' object has no attribute 'function_name'
An unexpected internal error occurred while the worker was executing a task.
```

This appears related to the missing or incorrectly initialized execution_info / dict_info inside _raylet, similar to issue #36869, but the reproduction path is different and fully deterministic.

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray version: 2.52.1

Python version: 3.10

OS: Linux (Docker / Ubuntu)

Environment: standalone (no KubeRay, fully local execution)

### Reproduction script

```python
import ray
from ray.job_config import JobConfig
from ray.dag import InputNode

class Counter:
    def __init__(self):
        self.value = 0

    def increment(self, i) -> int:
        self.value += i
        return self.value

if __name__ == "__main__":
    ray.init(
        job_config=JobConfig(code_search_path=['/usr'])
    )
    counter = ray.remote(Counter)
    counter_actor = counter.remote()

    with InputNode() as dag_inputs:
        dag = counter_actor.increment.options(num_returns=1).bind(
            dag_inputs['i']
        )

    compiled_dag = dag.experimental_compile()
    output = compiled_dag.execute(**{
        'i': 10
    })
    print(ray.get(output, timeout=100))
```
The script runs correctly if any of the following changes are applied:

- Remove code_search_path from ray.init()

- Use @ray.remote instead of ray.remote(Class)

- Do not call experimental_compile()

Therefore the issue only occurs when all three components interact.

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.

ray-project/ray#59274.body:
There are a few inconsistencies in the `ray.remote` and `ray.method` public APIs that need to be fixed and will improve user experience.

- [ ] @ray.remote and @ray.method decorators should fail fast and return an error if `num_returns='streaming'` or `num_returns='dynamic'`, but the python function is not a generator function. 
- [ ] @ray.method decorator should fail fast and return an error if `num_returns` is < 0.

ray-project/ray#59301.body:
The ["Input/output"](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/api/input_output.html) API reference is long, unorganized, and inconsistent with the user guides (the user guides split up the APIs into ["Loading data"](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/loading-data.html) and ["Saving data"](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/saving-data.html)).

To improve organization, we should split the "Input/output" API reference into two separate pages with sections that mirror the respective user guides.

**Current state of the world**

- Input/output (all APIs in unorganized list)

**Proposed state of the world**

- Loading Data API 
  - Reading files (this is a section, similar to [Basic transformations](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/api/dataset.html#basic-transformations))
  - Loading data from other libraries
  - Reading databases
  - ...
- Saving Data API
  - Writing files
  - ...

ray-project/ray#59303.body:
### Description

I'm new to Ray, but while reading the docs and source code I noticed that the typing story for actor methods could be improved.  
The [official doc](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/ray-core/actors.html#type-hints-and-static-typing-for-actors) recommends decorating actor methods with `@ray.method` so that static checkers give better auto-completion / type checking for `.remote()` calls. This works for the simplest cases, yet breaks as soon as the method has default arguments.

**Minimal reproducer**  
```python
import ray

@ray.method
def f2(self, b: int = 1) -> bool:
    return False

fn = f2          # pyright / mypy infer the type here
```

Pyright infers `fn` as `_RemoteMethodNoArgs`, i.e. a remote method that accepts **no arguments** (see [actor.py#L93](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/631684a13067b15d95117ec097b6f3c6c1a44554/python/ray/actor.py#L93)).  
Consequently `fn.remote(5)` is flagged by the type checker although it is perfectly valid at runtime.

**Root cause**  
The current stubs use overloads on plain `Callable` types:

```python
@overload
def method(__method: Callable[[Any, T0], Ret]) -> bool: ...
@overload
def method(__method: Callable[[Any], Ret]) -> float: ...
```

A function with a default argument (`self, b: int = 1`) matches **both** overloads; which one is chosen depends only on declaration order, so the no-argument version wins and the information about the optional parameter is lost.

**Possible improvement for modern Python (≥3.10)**  
We can keep the same runtime behaviour while giving exact static types:

```python
from typing import TypeVar, Generic, Concatenate, ParamSpec, Callable

P = ParamSpec("P")
T = TypeVar("T")
C = TypeVar("C")          # the actor class

class RemoteMethod(Generic[P, T]):
    def remote(self, *args: P.args, **kwargs: P.kwargs) -> T: ...
    def bind(self,  *args: P.args, **kwargs: P.kwargs) -> Any: ...

def ray_method(f: Callable[Concatenate[C, P], T]) -> RemoteMethod[P, T]:
    import ray
    return ray.method(f)          # runtime delegate
```

This preserves the signature of the original method (including defaults) inside the `RemoteMethod` instance, so type checkers know exactly which arguments `.remote()` accepts.

**Suggestion**  
Would the team accept a PR that adds ParamSpec-based overloads for `@ray.method` when Python version is satisfied?, while keeping the current overloads for older versions?

### Use case

_No response_
ray-project/ray#59303.title: [Type Enhance] The improvement of static check of `ray.method`

ray-project/ray#59325.body:
### Description

Follow-up to #59212. The current `continue_on_error` implementation only handles the case where the vLLM engine runs in the same lifecycle as the Ray Data pipeline (`vLLMEngineStage`). When using Ray Serve handles (`ServeDeploymentStage`), error handling requires a separate implementation due to differences in error propagation.

### Background

`ServeDeploymentStage` accesses the LLM engine via `DeploymentHandle` (RPC calls) rather than in-process. This changes how errors show up (from the POV of a Ray Data user):

| Error Type | vLLMEngineStage (in-process) | ServeDeploymentStage (RPC) |
|------------|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Prompt too long | `ValueError` | Wrapped in `RayTaskError` |
| Engine OOM | `EngineDeadError` | `RayActorError` (replica died) |
| Network issue | N/A | `RayActorError`, timeout |
| Replica crashed | N/A | `RayActorError` |

### Current State

`serve_deployment_stage.py` has the same vulnerability as the original `vllm_engine_stage.py`:

```python
tasks = [asyncio.create_task(self.generate_async(row)) for row in batch]

for resp in asyncio.as_completed(tasks):
    request, output, time_taken = await resp  # Exception propagates, kills batch
```

### Proposed Implementation

1. Add `should_continue_on_error` parameter to `ServeDeploymentStageUDF.__init__`

2. Create `_generate_with_error_handling` wrapper for `generate_async`

3. Define fatal vs non-fatal errors for serve handle case:
   ```python
   _SERVE_FATAL_ERRORS = (
       ray.exceptions.RayActorError,  # Replica crashed
       # Connection/timeout errors TBD
   )
   ```

4. Handle error unwrapping - `RayTaskError` wraps the original exception, need to inspect `cause` to determine if underlying error was fatal

5. Wire `continue_on_error` through `ServeDeploymentProcessorConfig`

### Challenges

- **Error serialization**: vLLM exception types (`EngineDeadError`) are wrapped/serialized over RPC, not directly catchable
- **Fatal error detection**: Need to distinguish "replica died" (fatal, don't continue) from "request validation failed" (non-fatal, safe to continue)
- **Error unwrapping**: May need to parse error messages or inspect `RayTaskError.cause` to determine root cause
- **Serve middleware**: Errors might be converted to HTTP responses rather than exceptions depending on deployment configuration

### Files to Modify

- `python/ray/llm/_internal/batch/stages/serve_deployment_stage.py` - Add error handling wrapper
- `python/ray/llm/_internal/batch/processor/serve_deployment_proc.py` - Wire config through
- `python/ray/llm/tests/batch/gpu/stages/test_serve_deployment_stage.py` - Add tests

### Related

- Parent PR: #59212
- Issue: #52449

### Use case

_No response_
ray-project/ray#59325.title: [Data][LLM] Add `should_continue_on_error` support for ServeDeploymentStage (Data <> Serve)

ray-project/ray#59418.body:
Ray Data implements PyArrow and pandas data type extensions to natively support Python objects and tensors.

Even though these extensions are only used by Ray Data, they live in the ray.air subpackage (this is due to historical reasons).

To make the package structure easier to understand, we should move these files to the ray.data subpackage and update imports accordingly:

- ray.air.util.objection_extensions -> ray.data._internal.object_extensions
- ray.air.util.tensor_extensions -> ray.data._internal.tensor_extensions
ray-project/ray#59418.title: [Data] Move type extensions from ray.air to ray.data

ray-project/ray#59420.body:
## Description

Relocate extension types from `ray.air.util` to `ray.data._internal` to reduce module dependencies. This includes moving `object_extensions` and `tensor_extensions`, updating imports codebase-wide, fixing import errors and test failures, and planning further cleanups to fully decouple `ray.data` from `ray.air`

The changes mainly included: 

`ray.air.util.tensor_extensions` -> `ray.data._internal.tensor_extensions`
`ray.air.util.object_extensions` -> `ray.data._internal.object_extensions`
`ray.air._internal.torch_utils`  -> `ray.data.util.torch_utils`
`python/ray/air/util/transform_pyarrow.py`  ->  `ray/data/_internal/utils/transform_pyarrow.py`
`ray.air.util -> ray.data.util`

## Related issues
Closes #59418
ray-project/ray#59420.title: [Data] Move extension types to `ray.data`

ray-project/ray#59437.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Some exception types may not allow `args` to be set, which we do [here](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/a0ef66fbd7567910609411b8454bf253291d36f9/python/ray/exceptions.py#L177) in order to make an error that inherits from both `RayTaskError` and the user exception type.

Currently this fails with a confusing error message. We should either find a way to avoid needing to set `self.args` or else skip inheriting from the user-defined exception type in this case with a warning (only inherit from `RayTaskError`).

### Versions / Dependencies

Master

### Reproduction script

```bash
pip install -U rasterio
```

```python
import ray
from rasterio._err import CPLE_AppDefinedError

@ray.remote
def f():
    raise CPLE_AppDefinedError("Error", 200, "Something went wrong.")

ray.get(f.remote())
```

ray-project/ray#59437.title: [core] Exception types that don't allow `args` to be set cannot be deserialized

ray-project/ray#59445.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Context
---

 - Pandas uses custom extension types (like Int64Dtype, etc) to support nullable values. 
 - Ray Data's `TensorArray` represents tensors as Numpy tensors and therefore can't support nullable numeric values
 - When converting from Pandas `Series` into Numpy tensor, it becomes a tensor with dtype="object"
 - When we try to convert it into Arrow's tensor calling `pyarrow.from_numpy_dtype` fails with `Unsupported numpy type 17`


### Versions / Dependencies

2.52

### Reproduction script

NA

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#59445.title: [Data] Failure when converting to Arrow from `TensorArray`

ray-project/ray#59482.body:
Currently, `repr(dataset)` looks like this:

```python
>>> import ray
>>> ray.data.read_parquet("example://iris.parquet")
Dataset(
   num_rows=?,
   schema={
      sepal.length: double,
      sepal.width: double,
      petal.length: double,
      petal.width: double,
      variety: string
   }
)
>>>  ray.data.read_parquet("example://iris.parquet").materialize()
MaterializedDataset(
   num_blocks=20,
   num_rows=150,
   schema={
      sepal.length: double,
      sepal.width: double,
      petal.length: double,
      petal.width: double,
      variety: string
   }
)
```

ray-project/ray#59522.body:
### Description

Hi team! I'd like to list a few of the things I'm seeing when scaling up Ray Data LLM to batch sizes of 10 million + prompts (each prompt itself is also quite long, often times over 10k tokens). Thought this would serve as a helpful place for discussion on the resiliency of Ray Data LLM at a large scale.

1) When using Ray Data LLM with the vLLM processor, there seems to be no actor level resiliency during vllm engine failures. When running at a high concurrency (for example 4 replicas of Qwen3-32B-FP8), if one of the vllm engine dies, I expect that the job will continue on the 3 remaining replicas while the 4th one tries to restart itself. 
As an example, I'm processing millions of prompts in a single rayjob via kuberay, this rayjob asks for 4 GPU instances (p5.48xlarge). Sometimes the engine dies randomly (see logs below) out of my control and shuts the whole job down. It would be very cool if it was possible to add resiliency at the engine failure level to restart a failed engine while continuing the batch on the existent vllm engines.

2) Another prevalent case here is especially for spot instances, it seems that if the spot node goes down, we lose a lot of objects in the object store memory and the job then also fails (will also attach logs). Is there a recommended way to deal with the storage of objects? I'm playing around with a very powerful CPU headnode and trying to allocate storage there but sometimes the spread of objects just distributes across all the nodes in the cluster. Also why would this kill a job?


Logs:
1) Random vllm engine failure logs:

ray-project/ray#59562.body:
`ObjectLostError` currently contains a generic error message:

```
All copies of [REDACTED_TOKEN] have been lost due to node failure. Check cluster logs (`/tmp/ray/session_latest/logs`) for more information about the failure."
```

This error message is confusing because often users expect that the object would be recovered via lineage reconstruction. We should include more context information in this error message, for example:

- `The object could not be reconstructed because it was created with ray.put`
- `The object could not be reconstructed because it was returned from a task that ran out of retries`
- `The object could not be reconstructed because it was returned from a task with no retries configured`

We would need to track this metadata in the reference counter: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/d3a9d8ced031b05e7d4668d1768441be5a9c201b/src/ray/core_worker/reference_counter.cc#L352

We could track it as a proto enum, like `NotReconstructableState` or `ReconstructableState` (which includes a state indicating that it _can_ be reconstructed).
ray-project/ray#59562.title: [core] Add more context information to `ObjectLostError`

ray-project/ray#59573.body:
### Description

When using Ray actors, especially **detached actors** (lifetime="detached"), they can outlive the driver process that created them. In production or long-running workflows, sometimes these actors get stuck, leak resources (e.g., GPU memory), or hang due to exceptions/bugs.

Currently, the only ways to terminate such actors are:
- From Python code: `ray.kill(ray.get_actor(name))` (requires knowing the name and having a live driver connected).
- Killing the underlying OS process (kill -9 <pid>) – risky and not Ray-native.

There is **no official CLI command** to kill an actor by its ActorID (or name) from the command line. This forces users to either:
- Restart the entire Ray cluster (disruptive and expensive), or
- Write custom scripts using `ray.state` APIs or `ray.get_actor()`.



### Use case

Cleaning up stuck or hanging actors without restarting the cluster。
ray-project/ray#59573.title: [Ray CLI] Add a CLI command ray kill --actor-id <actor_id> to forcefully terminate actors (including detached ones)

ray-project/ray#59615.body:
We observed that raylet frequently emits log messages of the form “Dropping sync message with stale version”, which can become quite noisy in practice.

This behavior occurs because raylet does not update the message version for certain sync messages, and stale-version broadcast messages are expected to be skipped by default. As a result, these log entries are generated repeatedly even though this is normal and non-actionable behavior.

Given that this does not indicate an error or unexpected state, logging it at the INFO level significantly increases log noise and makes it harder to identify genuinely important events.

We propose demoting this log from INFO to DEBUG in RaySyncerBidiReactorBase to keep raylet logs cleaner while still preserving the information for debugging purposes when needed.

![img_v3_02t7_be5071d6-99d2-4b3c-b189-66aa77476d3g](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ed91c317-3a86-441c-a2bf-b317ac0af618)
ray-project/ray#59615.title: [Core] Demote "stale sync message drop" log to DEBUG in RaySyncer

ray-project/ray#59633.body:
## Description
This PR adds an option to use `ActorPoolStrategy` as `compute` for `read_datasource`. This is a simple extension to expose the option to set `Read` op's compute strategy (see #59422 ).

For the time being, there is no change to the `concurrency` parameter. When both `concurrency` and `compute` are set, a warning is given and we will simply use `TaskPoolStrategy(concurrency)`.

## Additional information
Unlike `map_batches`, there is no "class-based" implementation of a `ReadTask` yet. So there is no change to how user should implement a `Datasource`, i.e., they should still provide a list of `ReadTask` that calls some read _functions_ (`get_read_tasks`).

Ideally, in the future, we can allow user to define their "class-based" datasource where they can handle how each ReadTask should run on a pool of actors.

### Changes in this PR
* added a new parameter `compute` to `read_datasource`
* no change to other read_* APIs
* added a bunch of tests on the concurrency/compute options, fusion rules, etc.
* [minor] fixed some type hints in parquet datasource
ray-project/ray#59633.title: [Data] Add compute parameter and allow ActorPoolStrategy for read_datasource()

ray-project/ray#59639.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

I think there’s a bug / design issue in the uv run runtime_env integration (enabled by default in Ray 2.53).

When the driver is launched with uv run, Ray’s uv_runtime_env_hook sets runtime_env["py_executable"] to the detected uv run ... command line. In a worker/job runtime_env, that can cause uv to pick a different Python version than the driver/cluster (e.g. driver is on 3.11 but uv selects 3.12 for the worker). Ray then immediately fails with:

```
  RuntimeError: Version mismatch: The cluster was started with:
      Ray: 2.53.0
      Python: 3.11.x
  This process on node 127.0.0.1 was started with:
      Ray: 2.53.0
      Python: 3.12.x
```


This is easy to hit if:

- You have both Python 3.11 and 3.12 available to uv,
- The project has requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.13",
- The driver is run in a 3.11 venv, and
- A worker runtime env is created under a different working dir (Ray’s runtime_env packaging dir), where uv re-resolves Python.


### Workaround

- Pin the Python for uv run explicitly so the hook also pins workers:
    - uv run --python 3.11 python repro.py
- Or disable the feature:
    - RAY_ENABLE_UV_RUN_RUNTIME_ENV=0


### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.53, mac os, system python 3.12.

### Reproduction script


pyproject.toml:

```toml
  [project]
  name = "ray-uv-bug-repro"
  version = "0.0.0"
  requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.13"
  dependencies = ["ray==2.53.0"]
```

```python
import os, sys, ray
@ray.remote
def worker_python_version():
   import sys as _sys
   return _sys.version

def main():
   os.environ.setdefault("RAY_ENABLE_UV_RUN_RUNTIME_ENV", "1")
   print("driver sys.executable:", sys.executable)
   print("driver sys.version:", sys.version)
   ray.init(address="local", num_cpus=1, log_to_driver=True)
   ref = worker_python_version.options(runtime_env={}).remote()
   print("worker sys.version:", ray.get(ref))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

### Issue Severity

Low
ray-project/ray#59639.title: [Core] `uv run` `runtime_env` hook can start workers on a different Python than the driver, causing “Version mismatch” failures

On Ray version `2.48` and above, on m3 mac, the provided script runs and the system hangs almost immediately for about 40 seconds. 

Script logs:

```py
....
This cluster consists of
    nodes: 1 
    resources: {'CPU': 11.0, 'node:127.0.0.1': 1.0, 'memory': 8610283520.0, 'object_store_memory': 2147483648.0, 'node:__internal_head__': 1.0}
```

```py
from collections import Counter
import socket
import time

import ray

ray.init()

print(
    """This cluster consists of

""".format(

    time.sleep(0.001)

start = time.time()
```

ray-project/ray#59682.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The new cluster autoscaler works by checking if the global logical utilization is high, and if so, adding one node of each type.

To determine the different types of nodes, the autoscaler calls `ray.nodes()`: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/6dc66d4b7da62e93093b98190c064d50d5c1905f/python/ray/data/_internal/cluster_autoscaler/default_cluster_autoscaler_v2.py#L124-L140.

The problem with this approach is that it can only detect alive nodes. If you start from zero worker nodes, the autoscaler won't be aware of that node type. To address this issue, we can use the Ray Core API introduced in https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/49568.

**Outcome**:

`DefaultAutoscalerV2` should try to scale up node types even if there are currently zero of them.

**Constraints**:

* Use `ray._private.state.state.get_cluster_config()` (you might also need to still use `ray.nodes()` since `get_cluster_config()` doesn't give you counts)


### Versions / Dependencies

6dc66d4b7da62e93093b98190c064d50d5c1905f

### Reproduction script

TODO

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#59682.title: [Data] `DefaultAutoscalerV2` doesn't scale nodes from zero

ray-project/ray#59683.body:
The `DefaultAutoscaler2` implementation needs an `AutoscalingCoordinator` and a way to get all of the `_NodeResourceSpec`. 

Currently, we can't explicitly inject fake implementations of either dependency. This is problematic because the tests need to assume what the implementation of each dependency looks like and use brittle mocks. 

For example, `patch_autoscaling_coordinator` works by patching `get_or_create_autoscaling_coordinator` and `ray.get` -- both of which are implementation details of the autoscaling coordinator: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/6dc66d4b7da62e93093b98190c064d50d5c1905f/python/ray/data/tests/test_default_cluster_autoscaler_v2.py#L28-L41.

To make the dependencies explicit and easier to fake, we should introduce seams.

**Outcome**

* Add the `FakeAutoscalingCoordinator` implementation to a new `fake_autoscaling_coordinator.py` module (you can use the code below)
* `DefaultClusterAutoscalerV2` has two new parameters `autoscaling_coordinator: Optional[AutoscalingCoordinator] = None` and `get_node_counts: Callable[[], Dict[_NodeResourceSpec, int]] = get_node_resource_spec_and_count`. If `autoscaling_coordinator` is None, you can use the default implementation.
* Update `test_try_scale_up_cluster` to use the explicit seams rather than mocks. Where possible, assert against the public interface rather than implementation details

```
class FakeAutoscalingCoordinator(AutoscalingCoordinator):
    """A lightweight implementation for testing.

    This implementation always allocates the requested resources to the requester.
    It doesn't support the `priority` parameter.
    """

    @dataclass
    class Allocation:
        resources: List[ResourceDict]
        expiration_time_s: float
        request_remaining: bool

    def __init__(
        self,
        get_time: Callable[[], float] = time.time,
        remaining: Optional[List[ResourceDict]] = None,
    ):
        if remaining is None:
            remaining = []

        self._get_time = get_time
        self._remaining = remaining

        self._allocations: Dict[str, self.Allocation] = {}

    def request_resources(
        self,
        requester_id: str,
        resources: List[ResourceDict],
        expire_after_s: float,
        request_remaining: bool = False,
        priority: ResourceRequestPriority = ResourceRequestPriority.MEDIUM,
    ) -> None:
        if priority != ResourceRequestPriority.MEDIUM:
            raise NotImplementedError(
                "This fake implementation doesn't support the `priority` parameter."
            )

        self._allocations[requester_id] = self.Allocation(
            resources=resources,
            expiration_time_s=self._get_time() + expire_after_s,
            request_remaining=request_remaining,
        )

    def cancel_request(self, requester_id: str):
        if requester_id in self._allocations:
            del self._allocations[requester_id]

    def get_allocated_resources(self, requester_id: str) -> List[ResourceDict]:
        allocation = self._allocations.get(requester_id)

        # Case 1: The requester hasn't been allocated any resources.
        if allocation is None:
            return []

        # Case 2: The requester's allocation has expired.
        elif allocation.expiration_time_s < self._get_time():
            del self._allocations[requester_id]
            return []

        # Case 3: The requester has been allocated resources, and they haven't expired.
        else:
            allocated_resources = allocation.resources

            if allocation.request_remaining:
                allocated_resources.extend(self._remaining)

            return allocated_resources
```
ray-project/ray#59683.title: [Data] Introduce seams to `DefaultAutoscaler2` to make it more testable

ray-project/ray#59684.body:
The `ClusterAutoscaler` base class implements a default constructor implementation: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/6dc66d4b7da62e93093b98190c064d50d5c1905f/python/ray/data/_internal/cluster_autoscaler/base_cluster_autoscaler.py#L18-L27.

This default constructor doesn't add value because the base class doesn't use the dependencies anywhere, and it adds complexity because it requires all `ClusterAutoscaling` implementations to accept the same dependencies, even if they don't need them.

**Outcome**:
* `__init__` removed from `ClusterAutoscaler`
* We don't call `super()` for  subclasses
ray-project/ray#59684.title: [Data] Remove constructor from `ClusterAutoscaler` base class

ray-project/ray#59726.body:
## Background: How Ray CI Testing Works

Ray uses [Buildkite](https://buildkite.com/) for CI, with tests running inside Docker containers. The system has two layers of parallelism:

### Layer 1: Buildkite Parallelism (Jobs)

Buildkite can spawn multiple **parallel jobs** for a single pipeline step. Each job runs on a separate machine.

```yaml
# .buildkite/data.rayci.yml:63-74
- label: ":database: data: arrow v9 tests"
  parallelism: 8  # ← Spawns 8 parallel Buildkite jobs
  commands:
    - bazel run //ci/ray_ci:test_in_docker -- //python/ray/data/... //python/ray/air/... data
      --workers "$${BUILDKITE_PARALLEL_JOB_COUNT}"   # ← Total jobs (8)
      --worker-id "$${BUILDKITE_PARALLEL_JOB}"       # ← This job's index (0-7)
      --parallelism-per-worker 3                      # ← Docker containers per job
```

### Layer 2: Docker Containers (Shards)

Each Buildkite job (worker) spawns multiple Docker containers to run tests in parallel. This is controlled by `--parallelism-per-worker`. Each container runs exactly **one shard** of tests (1:1 mapping).

```
total_shards = workers × parallelism_per_worker
```

With 8 workers and 3 parallelism-per-worker, you get **24 shards** (24 Docker containers total).

## Code Flow

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Buildkite Pipeline (.buildkite/data.rayci.yml)                         │
│  - Spawns N parallel jobs (workers)                                     │
│  - Each job runs: bazel run //ci/ray_ci:test_in_docker                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    │
                                    ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  test_in_docker.py → tester.py                                          │
│  - Receives --workers, --worker-id, --parallelism-per-worker            │
│  - Calculates which shards this worker handles                          │
│  - Creates TesterContainer with shard assignments                       │
│                                                                         │
│  ci/ray_ci/tester.py:322-324                                            │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ shard_count = workers * parallelism_per_worker                  │    │
│  │ shard_start = worker_id * parallelism_per_worker                │    │
│  │ shard_end = (worker_id + 1) * parallelism_per_worker            │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    │
                                    ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  tester_container.py - TesterContainer.run_tests()                      │
│  - For each shard_id assigned to this worker:                           │
│    - Calls shard_tests() to get tests for that shard                    │
│    - Spawns a Docker container to run those tests                       │
│                                                                         │
│  ci/ray_ci/tester_container.py:84-91                                    │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ chunks = [                                                      │    │
│  │     shard_tests(test_targets, self.shard_count, i)              │    │
│  │     for i in self.shard_ids                                     │    │
│  │ ]                                                               │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    │
                                    ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  bazel_sharding.py - The Sharding Algorithm                             │
│  - Queries Bazel for test metadata (size, timeout)                      │
│  - Groups tests by expected runtime                                     │
│  - Distributes tests to shards using bin-packing algorithm              │
│                                                                         │
│  ci/ray_ci/bazel_sharding.py:263-274 (get_rules_for_shard_optimal)      │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ 1. Calculate optimum = total_time / shard_count                 │    │
│  │ 2. For each test (longest first):                               │    │
│  │    - Find first shard below optimum time                        │    │
│  │    - Add test to that shard                                     │    │
│  │ 3. Return tests assigned to requested shard index               │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## The Problem

### Current Worker-to-Shard Assignment (Contiguous)

Workers receive **contiguous blocks** of shard IDs:

```
shard_ids = list(range(shard_start, shard_end))
```

| Worker | shard_start | shard_end | Assigned Shards |
|--------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|
| 0      | 0           | 3         | [0, 1, 2]       |
| 1      | 3           | 6         | [3, 4, 5]       |
| ...    | ...         | ...       | ...             |
| 7      | 21          | 24        | [21, 22, 23]    |

### How the Sharding Algorithm Fills Shards

The optimal sharding algorithm (`bazel_sharding.py:225-260`) assigns tests to minimize total runtime variance. It iterates shards 0→N and picks the **first shard below optimum**:

```python
# ci/ray_ci/bazel_sharding.py:244-247
if shard_time_with_item < optimum:
    first_shard_index_below_optimum = i
    break  # ← Always picks FIRST shard below optimum
```

This means earlier shards fill up first. With many long-running "eternal" tests (3600s each), the algorithm:

1. Assigns each eternal test to its own shard (shards 0-7)
2. Fills remaining shards (8-20) with shorter tests
3. Leaves shards 21-23 empty (no tests left)

### Observed Behavior

```
Shard 0:  1 test,  3600s  ─┐
Shard 1:  1 test,  3600s   │ Worker 0-2: Overloaded with
...                        │ long-running eternal tests
Shard 7:  1 test,  3600s  ─┘
Shard 8:  5 tests, 2460s  ─┐
...                        │ Workers 3-6: Normal workload
Shard 20: 26 tests, 1560s ─┘
Shard 21: 0 tests, 0s     ─┐
Shard 22: 0 tests, 0s      │ Worker 7: COMPLETELY IDLE
Shard 23: 0 tests, 0s     ─┘
```

## Desired Outcome

All workers should receive a balanced workload. No worker should be completely idle when there are tests to run.

## Constraints

- The sharding algorithm's time-balancing behavior is intentional and should be preserved
- The fix should not require changing CI pipeline configurations (worker count, parallelism)
- Tests must still be deterministically assigned (same inputs → same assignment)

## Relevant Code Links

| File | Lines | Description |
|------|-------|-------------|
| `ci/ray_ci/tester.py` | 322-324 | Shard calculation and contiguous assignment |
| `ci/ray_ci/tester.py` | 335, 352 | Where `shard_ids` is passed to containers |
| `ci/ray_ci/tester_container.py` | 84-91 | Where shards are executed |
| `ci/ray_ci/bazel_sharding.py` | 225-260 | `add_rule_to_best_shard` - the greedy algorithm |
| `ci/ray_ci/bazel_sharding.py` | 263-340 | `get_rules_for_shard_optimal` - main sharding logic |
| `.buildkite/data.rayci.yml` | 63-74 | Example CI config using test_in_docker |
ray-project/ray#59726.title: [Data][CI] Empty workers in `test_in_docker` sharding

ray-project/ray#59727.body:
## Description

The `base_test` Docker image retains ~838MB of unnecessary files after Miniforge installation:
- `/root/Miniforge3-25.3.0-1-Linux-x86_64.sh` (78MB) - the installer script
- `/opt/miniforge/pkgs/cache/` (760MB) - conda package cache

These files serve no purpose after installation completes and slows down CI by increasing image pull/push times. The cleanup should be added to the base image so all downstream images (like `data.build`) automatically benefit from the smaller size.

## Background

The Ray CI infrastructure uses Docker images for reproducible builds. The `base_test` image is the foundational image that installs system dependencies, Miniforge (a minimal conda distribution), and Bazel.

The Miniforge installation process:
1. Downloads the installer to `${HOME}/${filename}` (line 44-45 of `install-miniforge.sh`)
2. Runs the installer to populate `/opt/miniforge`
3. Does NOT clean up the installer or package cache afterward

## Implementation Boundaries & Constraints

There are two valid approaches to implement this fix:

### Option A: Update `install-miniforge.sh`

- **Target File**: `ci/env/install-miniforge.sh`

  Add cleanup at the end of the `install_miniforge` function (before the closing brace), after the conda environment is verified:
  ```bash
  # Clean up installer and cache
  rm -f "${miniforge_target}"
  rm -rf "${miniforge_dir}/pkgs/cache/"
  ```

  This approach ensures cleanup happens automatically wherever the script is used.

### Option B: Update the Dockerfile

- **Target File**: `ci/docker/base.test.Dockerfile`

  Add cleanup commands immediately after the `./ci/env/install-miniforge.sh` line:
  ```dockerfile
  RUN ./ci/env/install-miniforge.sh && \
      rm -rf /opt/miniforge/pkgs/cache/ /root/Miniforge3-*.sh
  ```

  Note: Using a glob pattern (`Miniforge3-*.sh`) makes this more maintainable if the version changes.

## Contributing expectations

Please review the [Ray Data Contributing Guide](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/contributing/contributing-guide.html) before submitting your PR.
ray-project/ray#59727.title: [Data][CI] Clean up Miniforge installer and conda cache in `base_test` Docker image

ray-project/ray#59729.body:
## Description

Split the monolithic `test_sort.py` test module (~777 lines, ~1139s runtime) into smaller, logically-grouped test modules. This enables better parallelization in CI, where tests are distributed across workers by module rather than by individual test function.

## Background

Ray Data's CI distributes test execution across workers at the **module level**, not at the individual test level. When a single test module takes ~1139 seconds (nearly 19 minutes), it becomes a bottleneck that prevents even workload distribution across CI workers.

The current `test_sort.py` contains 23 test functions covering diverse functionality:
- Basic sort operations (single key, multiple keys, different data formats)
- Push-based shuffle scheduling algorithms
- Multi-node cluster shuffle tests
- Memory usage and warning tests
- Debug configuration tests

These represent distinct categories that can be cleanly separated.

## Implementation Boundaries & Constraints

### Target Files
- `python/ray/data/tests/test_sort.py` (777 lines, 23 tests)

### Proposed Split

Create **3 new test modules** based on natural functional groupings:

#### 1. `test_sort.py` (Keep - Core Sort Operations) ~250 lines
Keep the core sort functionality tests:
- `test_sort_with_specified_boundaries` (lines 24-52)
- `test_sort_multiple_keys_produces_equally_sized_blocks` (lines 55-73)
- `test_sort_simple` (lines 75-98)
- `test_sort_partition_same_key_to_same_block` (lines 100-116)
- `test_sort_arrow` (lines 118-163)
- `test_sort` (lines 166-184)
- `test_sort_arrow_with_empty_blocks` (lines 187-235)
- `test_sort_with_multiple_keys` (lines 238-269)
- `test_sort_pandas` (lines 272-301)
- `test_sort_pandas_with_empty_blocks` (lines 304-342)
- `test_sort_with_one_block` (lines 345-355)

#### 2. `test_push_based_shuffle.py` (New) ~300 lines
Move all push-based shuffle tests:
- `test_push_based_shuffle_schedule` (lines 357-475) - scheduler algorithm test (no cluster needed)
- `test_push_based_shuffle_stats` (lines 477-515) - stats collection with multi-node cluster
- `test_sort_multinode` (lines 517-537) - multi-node sort end-to-end
- `test_push_based_shuffle_reduce_stage_scheduling` (lines 573-657) - reduce task scheduling

#### 3. `test_shuffle_diagnostics.py` (New) ~130 lines
Move memory/warning/debug tests:
- `test_debug_limit_shuffle_execution_to_num_blocks` (lines 667-688)
- `test_memory_usage` (lines 691-702)
- `test_sort_object_ref_warnings` (lines 705-735)
- `test_sort_inlined_objects_warnings` (lines 738-770)

### Do Not Touch
- `python/ray/data/tests/conftest.py` - Contains shared fixtures
- Other shuffle-related test files (`test_hash_shuffle.py`, `test_random_e2e.py`)
- The `configure_shuffle_method` fixture and parameterization patterns

### Testing Requirements
- All tests must pass after the split
- Make sure you still call `pytest.main` at the bottom all of new test modules!
- Ensure no duplicate or dropped tests across modules

## Contributing expectations

See the [Ray Data contributing guide](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/contributing/contributing-guide.html) for development setup and contribution workflow.
ray-project/ray#59729.title: [Data] Split `test_sort.py` into smaller test modules to improve CI parallelization

ray-project/ray#59780.body:
## Description

Reduce CI time for Data-only PRs by not running unrelated ML tests. Currently, when files in `python/ray/data/` are modified, the CI triggers all ML/train tests. 

## Background

Ray's CI uses a tag-based system to determine which tests run for different code changes:

1. **test_rules.txt** (`ci/pipeline/test_rules.txt`) maps file paths to CI tags. Currently, changes to `python/ray/data/` trigger both `data`, and `ml` and `train` tags

2. **CI YAML files** define test steps that run based on these tags. For example, the `ml.rayci.yml` file contains a `:train: ml: train v1 tests` step that's run whenever the `train` tag is triggered.

3. **Bazel BUILD files** define individual test targets with tags like `team:ml`, `train_v2`, etc. Bazel uses `--only-tags` and `--except-tags` flags to filter which tests execute.

The problem is that Data PRs currently trigger ALL train tests, when we only actually care about these four tests:
- test_data_integration
- test_data_resource_cleanup
- test_data_config
- test_iter_torch_batches_gpu

## Implementation Boundaries & Constraints

### Target Files

1. **`ci/pipeline/test_rules.txt`** - Remove the `train`  and `ml` tag from Data file changes
   - Current (line 35-43):
     ```
     python/ray/data/
     .buildkite/data.rayci.yml
     ci/docker/data.build.Dockerfile
     ...
     @ data ml train
     ;
     ```
   - Change to: `@ data` (remove `train` and `ml`)

2. **`.buildkite/ml.rayci.yml`** - Add a new test step to run only the 4 Data-related ML tests
   - Pattern match against `:train: ml: train v1 gpu tests` and add a new step that runs with `--only-tags data_integration` (new tag to be added)

3.  In the `BUILD.bazel` files - Add a new tag `data_integration` to the 4 relevant test targets:
   - `test_data_integration`
   - `test_data_resource_cleanup`
   - `test_data_config`
   - `test_iter_torch_batches_gpu`

4. **`.buildkite/ml.rayci.yml`** - Add `--except-tags data_integration` to the other train test steps to avoid duplicate test runs

### Prototype implementation

See PR #59690 for a similar approach.

### Do Not Touch

- Individual test file implementations (`test_data_integration.py`, etc.)
- Core CI infrastructure (`ci/ray_ci/tester.py`)
- Docker build configurations

## Contributing Expectations

Please review the Ray Data contributing guide before starting: https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/contributing/contributing-guide.html
ray-project/ray#59780.title: [Data][CI] Stop running all ML tests on Data premerge

ray-project/ray#59795.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

When running `ray symmetric-run` on a multi-node cluster, worker nodes always fail with "Timed out waiting for head node to start", even when the head node's GCS is fully accessible.

**Head node logs:**

```bash
On head node. Starting Ray cluster head...
Head node started.
=======================
2026-01-01 02:15:40,468 INFO worker.py:1821 -- Connecting to existing Ray cluster at address: 10.34.5.161:6379...
2026-01-01 02:15:40,482 INFO worker.py:1998 -- Connected to Ray cluster. View the dashboard at http://127.0.0.1:8265 
/root/seven/ray-venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/_private/worker.py:2046: FutureWarning: Tip: In future versions of Ray, Ray will no longer override accelerator visible devices env var if num_gpus=0 or num_gpus=None (default). To enable this behavior and turn off this error message, set RAY_ACCEL_ENV_VAR_OVERRIDE_ON_ZERO=0
  warnings.warn(
Waiting for nodes to start... 1/2 nodes started
Waiting for nodes to start... 1/2 nodes started
Waiting for nodes to start... 1/2 nodes started
Waiting for nodes to start... 1/2 nodes started
Waiting for nodes to start... 1/2 nodes started
Waiting for nodes to start... 1/2 nodes started
(raylet) The autoscaler failed with the following error:
Terminated with signal 15
  File "/root/seven/ray-venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/autoscaler/_private/monitor.py", line 736, in <module>
    monitor.run()
  File "/root/seven/ray-venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/autoscaler/_private/monitor.py", line 593, in run
    self._run()
  File "/root/seven/ray-venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/ray/autoscaler/_private/monitor.py", line 448, in _run
    time.sleep(AUTOSCALER_UPDATE_INTERVAL_S)

Stopped all 6 Ray processes.
Error: Timed out waiting for other nodes to start.
```

**Worker node logs:**

```bash
On worker node. Connecting to Ray cluster at 10.34.5.161:6379...
Did not find any active Ray processes.
Error: Timed out waiting for head node to start.
```

Expected: Worker nodes should connect to the head node and execute the command.
Actual: Worker nodes time out waiting for the head node, even though the network is reachable.

```bash
# on worker node
(ray-venv) root@se7en:~# nc -zv 10.34.5.161 6379
Connection to 10.34.5.161 6379 port [tcp/redis] succeeded!
```

### Versions / Dependencies

2.53.0

### Reproduction script

On head node (10.34.5.161) and worker node, execute the same command:

```bash
ray symmetric-run --address 10.34.5.161:6379 --min-nodes 2 -- python test.py
```

```python
# test.py
import ray

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def hello():
    return "Hello from Ray!"

result = ray.get(hello.remote())
print(result)
```

### Issue Severity

None

ray-project/ray#59849.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

When using a nested tuple or dict observation space in a multi-agent setup, it is sometimes necessary to apply `FlattenObservations` to only some agents. The observation space for the other agents should just be passed through. However, [`recompute_observation_space`](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/7cf58eface6e24d420665e439dc5862b0af3bc13/rllib/connectors/common/flatten_observations.py#L222) fails due to the following problem:

Assume a nested observation space is used, for example (this is an extension of the test code in [flatten_observations.py](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/rllib/connectors/common/flatten_observations.py#L256))

```python
import gymnasium as gym
import numpy as np

# Some arbitrarily nested, complex observation space.
obs_space = gym.spaces.Dict(
    {
        "a": gym.spaces.Box(-10.0, 10.0, (), np.float32),
        "b": gym.spaces.Tuple(  # nested tuple space
            [
                gym.spaces.Discrete(2),
                gym.spaces.Box(-1.0, 1.0, (2, 1), np.float32),
            ]
        ),
        "c": gym.spaces.MultiDiscrete([2, 3]),
        "d": gym.spaces.Dict(  # nested dict space
            {
                "e": gym.spaces.Box(-10.0, 10.0, (), np.float32),
            }
        ),
    }
)
```
When the connector is used in the `multi_agent is True` setting but not for a specific agent with id `agent_id`, then the structure of the input observation space is copied https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/7cf58eface6e24d420665e439dc5862b0af3bc13/rllib/connectors/common/flatten_observations.py#L243
For all other agents, the input observation space is flattened to a `gym.spaces.Box`.
At the end, a new dictionary space is created
https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/7cf58eface6e24d420665e439dc5862b0af3bc13/rllib/connectors/common/flatten_observations.py#L256
This will however result in an error when used with nested observation spaces such as the one above.

The reason is that the observation space structure was earlier obtained as such
https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/7cf58eface6e24d420665e439dc5862b0af3bc13/rllib/connectors/common/flatten_observations.py#L227
where nested tuple and dict spaces get converted to Python tuples and dictionaries. This is what `tree.map_structure` expects but not what should be copied over when the connector is not applied to a observation space. Instead,
```python
if self._agent_ids and agent_id not in self._agent_ids:
    spaces[agent_id] = deepcopy(self.input_observation_space[agent_id])
```
should be used instead. This ensures that the observation space for agents unaffected by this connector stays intact.

### Versions / Dependencies

ray version 2.53.0 but same result in earlier version

### Reproduction script


```python
import gymnasium as gym
import numpy as np

from ray.rllib.connectors.env_to_module import FlattenObservations
from ray.rllib.env.single_agent_episode import SingleAgentEpisode
from ray.rllib.utils.test_utils import check

# Some arbitrarily nested, complex observation space.
obs_space = gym.spaces.Dict(
    {
        "a": gym.spaces.Box(-10.0, 10.0, (), np.float32),
        "b": gym.spaces.Tuple(  # nested tuple space
            [
                gym.spaces.Discrete(2),
                gym.spaces.Box(-1.0, 1.0, (2, 1), np.float32),
            ]
        ),
        "c": gym.spaces.MultiDiscrete([2, 3]),
        "d": gym.spaces.Dict(  # nested dict space
            {
                "e": gym.spaces.Box(-10.0, 10.0, (), np.float32),
            }
        ),
    }
)
act_space = gym.spaces.Discrete(2)

# Two example episodes, both with initial (reset) observations coming from the
# above defined observation space.
episode_1 = SingleAgentEpisode(
    observations=[
        {
            "a": np.array(-10.0, np.float32),
            "b": (1, np.array([[-1.0], [-1.0]], np.float32)),
            "c": np.array([0, 2]),
            "d": {"e": np.array(-10.0, np.float32)},
        },
    ],
    agent_id="a1",
)
episode_2 = SingleAgentEpisode(
    observations=[
        {
            "a": np.array(10.0, np.float32),
            "b": (0, np.array([[1.0], [1.0]], np.float32)),
            "c": np.array([1, 1]),
            "d": {"e": np.array(10.0, np.float32)},
        },
    ],
    agent_id="a2",
)

# Construct our connector piece.
connector = FlattenObservations(
    obs_space, act_space, multi_agent=True, agent_ids=["a1"]
)
```

Results in
```
AssertionError: Dict space element is not an instance of Space: key='b', space=(Discrete(2), Box(-1.0, 1.0, (2, 1), float32))
```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#59849.title: [RLlib] Nested spaces (tuple or dict) break FlattenObservations in partially active multi-agent setups

ray-project/ray#59860.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

When using the new API stack and when using a multi agent environment where all agents map to the same shared policy/module the reported metrics for module_episode_return_mean is incorrect. 

I believe the metric is only reporting one agent's episode return (then averaged over episodes) instead of the sum of all agents mapping to the same policy/module (the averaged over episodes) which I believe is the intended behavior. 

I think the bug is originating from these lines: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/79567db76fa20469dad70c7c3cdf3cbf0046dd48/rllib/env/multi_agent_env_runner.py#L637C1-L643C14
```python
  module_episode_returns = defaultdict(
      float,
      {
          sa_eps.module_id: sa_eps.get_return()
          for sa_eps in eps.agent_episodes.values()
      },
  )
```
When multiple agents share the same module_id, this dictionary comprehension will overwrite previous values instead of accumulating them. 
A simple suggested fix would be as follows:
```python
module_episode_returns = defaultdict(float)
for sa_eps in eps.agent_episodes.values():
    module_episode_returns[sa_eps.module_id] += sa_eps.get_return()
```
I have tested this fix with the simple repro script below. In this repro script, I create a simple example with three agents each getting the same reward at each time step (1, 5, & 10) with a fixed horizon on 10 steps. 
The expected module episode returns should be 160 (1 x 10 + 5 x 10 + 10 x 10) but the reported metric is 100 (only the agent_2 returns) 

I don't think this affected the learning process only metric reporting but this should be confirmed. 

Happy to submit PR if needed. 

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray version: 2.53.0
Python version: 3.12.12
OS: MacOS M3 Pro

### Reproduction script

```python
import gymnasium as gym
import numpy as np
import ray
from ray.rllib.algorithms.ppo import PPOConfig
from ray.rllib.env.multi_agent_env import MultiAgentEnv
from ray.tune.registry import register_env

class SimpleMultiAgentEnv(MultiAgentEnv):   
    def __init__(self, config=None):
        super().__init__()
        self.episode_length = 10
        self.current_step = 0
        self.agents = ["agent_0", "agent_1", "agent_2"]        
        self.agent_rewards = {"agent_0": 1.0, "agent_1": 5.0, "agent_2": 10.0}
        self._obs_space = gym.spaces.Box(low=0, high=1, shape=(4,), dtype=np.float32)
        self._action_space = gym.spaces.Discrete(2)
        self.observation_space = gym.spaces.Dict({agent_id: self._obs_space for agent_id in self.agents})
        self.action_space = gym.spaces.Dict({agent_id: self._action_space for agent_id in self.agents})
    
    def reset(self, *, seed=None, options=None):
        self.current_step = 0
        obs = {agent_id: self._obs_space.sample() for agent_id in self.agents}
        infos = {agent_id: {} for agent_id in self.agents}
        return obs, infos
    
    def step(self, action_dict):
        self.current_step += 1        
        obs = {agent_id: self._obs_space.sample() for agent_id in self.agents}        
        rewards = {agent_id: self.agent_rewards[agent_id] for agent_id in self.agents}        
        done = self.current_step >= self.episode_length
        terminateds = {agent_id: done for agent_id in self.agents}
        terminateds["__all__"] = done        
        truncateds = {agent_id: False for agent_id in self.agents}
        truncateds["__all__"] = False        
        infos = {agent_id: {} for agent_id in self.agents}        
        return obs, rewards, terminateds, truncateds, infos    
    
def main():   
    register_env("simple_multi_agent", lambda config: SimpleMultiAgentEnv(config))    
    algo = (
        PPOConfig()
        .api_stack(enable_rl_module_and_learner=True,enable_env_runner_and_connector_v2=True)
        .environment("simple_multi_agent")
        .multi_agent(policies={"shared_policy"},policy_mapping_fn=(lambda aid, eps, **kw: "shared_policy"))
        .build_algo()    
    )

    for i in range(3):
        result = algo.train()
        env_runners = result.get('env_runners', {})        
        agent_returns = env_runners.get('agent_episode_returns_mean', {})
        module_returns = env_runners.get('module_episode_returns_mean', {})
        episode_return = env_runners.get('episode_return_mean', 'N/A')
        print(f"Iteration {i+1}:")
        print(f"episode_return_mean: {episode_return}")
        print("agent_episode_returns_mean:")
        for agent_id, ret in sorted(agent_returns.items()):
            print(f"\t {agent_id}: {ret:.2f}")
        print("module_episode_returns_mean:")
        for module_id, ret in module_returns.items():
            print(f"\t {module_id}: {ret:.2f}")         
    
if __name__ == "__main__":
    ray.init()
    main()
```

this should print
```
...
Iteration 3:
episode_return_mean: 160.0
agent_episode_returns_mean:
         agent_0: 10.00
         agent_1: 50.00
         agent_2: 100.00
module_episode_returns_mean:
         shared_policy: 160.00
```
but instead prints
```
...
Iteration 3:
episode_return_mean: 160.0
agent_episode_returns_mean:
         agent_0: 10.00
         agent_1: 50.00
         agent_2: 100.00
module_episode_returns_mean:
         shared_policy: 100.00
```


### Issue Severity

Low: It annoys or frustrates me.

ray-project/ray#59881.body:
**Description**

The `test_operators.py` file (~1,670 lines, 28 test functions) occasionally times out during CI runs. We should split it into smaller, logically grouped test modules to improve test reliability and allow better parallel execution.

**Background**

The current [`test_operators.py`](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/f85c5255669d8a682673401b54a86612a79058e3/python/ray/data/tests/test_operators.py) tests multiple physical operator types used in Ray Data's streaming execution engine:

- **MapOperator** / **TaskPoolMapOperator** / **ActorPoolMapOperator**: Transform data blocks using tasks or actors
- **LimitOperator**: Truncates output to a row limit
- **OutputSplitter**: Distributes outputs across multiple consumers
- **BlockRefBundler**: Bundles block references to support `batch_size`
- **AllToAllOperator** / **InputDataBuffer**: Base operators for shuffles and input buffering

Each operator type has independent test coverage that can be isolated into separate files.

**Implementation Boundaries & Constraints**

- **Target File**: [`python/ray/data/tests/test_operators.py`](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/f85c5255669d8a682673401b54a86612a79058e3/python/ray/data/tests/test_operators.py)

- **Proposed New Test Files**:

| New File | Tests to Move | Line Range (approx) |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| `test_map_operator.py` | `test_map_operator_streamed`, `test_map_operator_actor_locality_stats`, `test_map_operator_min_rows_per_bundle`, `test_map_operator_output_unbundling`, `test_map_operator_output_block_size_options`, `test_map_operator_disable_block_shaping_with_batches`, `test_map_operator_ray_args`, `test_map_operator_shutdown`, `test_map_operator_pool_delegation`, `test_map_kwargs`, `test_map_estimated_num_output_bundles`, `test_map_estimated_blocks_split`, `test_operator_metrics` | L237-L274, L540-L577, L581-L613, L616-L811, L1012-L1032, L1316-L1527 |
| `test_actor_pool_map_operator.py` | `test_actor_pool_map_operator_init`, `test_actor_pool_map_operator_should_add_input`, `test_actor_pool_map_operator_num_active_tasks_and_completed` | L853-L1009 |
| `test_limit_operator.py` | `test_limit_operator`, `test_limit_operator_memory_leak_fix`, `test_limit_estimated_num_output_bundles`, `test_per_block_limit_fn` | L1035-L1131, L1530-L1561, L1624-L1662 |
| `test_output_splitter.py` | `test_split_operator`, `test_split_operator_random`, `test_split_operator_locality_hints`, `test_split_operator_with_locality` | L279-L537 |
| `test_block_ref_bundler.py` | `test_block_ref_bundler_basic`, `test_block_ref_bundler_uniform` | L1134-L1313 |
| `test_operators.py` (keep) | `test_name_and_repr`, `test_input_data_buffer`, `test_all_to_all_operator`, `test_num_outputs_total`, `test_all_to_all_estimated_num_output_bundles`, `test_input_data_buffer_does_not_free_inputs` | L107-L233, L1564-L1621 |

**Do Not Touch**:
  - Operator implementations in `python/ray/data/_internal/execution/operators/`
  - Test behavior or assertions (this is purely a file reorganization)

**Verification Steps**:
  1. Run all new test files individually: `pytest python/ray/data/tests/test_<new_file>.py -v`
  2. Run original test file to ensure remaining tests pass
  3. Ensure no duplicate or missing test names across files
  4. Verify imports are correct in each new file

**Contributing expectations**

Please review the [Ray Data Contributing Guide](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/data/contributing/contributing-guide.html) before submitting a PR.
ray-project/ray#59881.title: [Data] Split `test_operators.py` into smaller, focused test modules

ray-project/ray#59943.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The Python package aiohttp has a number of new CVEs, some of high priority:
- CVE-2025-69223
- CVE-2025-69224
- CVE-2025-69225
- CVE-2025-69226
- CVE-2025-69227
- CVE-2025-69228
- CVE-2025-69229
- CVE-2025-69230

These are all fixed by version 3.13.3 of aiohttp.  The latest release of Ray (2.53.0) includes aiohttp 3.13.2, which is vulnerable.  I'm assuming all that's needed is a new release of Ray with the newer aiohttp.  Given the high severity, this seems urgent.

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.53.0
aiohttp 3.13.2

### Reproduction script

poetry run pip install ray
ls -d .venv/lib/python3.*/site-packages/ray/_private/runtime_env/agent/thirdparty_files/aiohttp-*

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#59943.title: New release required with new version of aiohttp Python package

ray-project/ray#59964.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen


It looks like Ray has an incorrect validation regex for TPU 7x: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/05dfac3d003304a1aad2aa04c752b9d9a9038893/python/ray/_private/accelerators/tpu.py#L342

```
    @staticmethod
    def is_valid_tpu_accelerator_type(tpu_accelerator_type: str) -> bool:
        """Check whether the tpu accelerator_type is formatted correctly.

        The accelerator_type field follows a form of v{generation}-{cores/chips}.

        See the following for more information:
        https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/compute/tpus/tpu-vm/accelerator-types/describe

        Args:
            tpu_accelerator_type: The string representation of the accelerator type
                to be checked for validity.

        Returns:
            True if it's valid, false otherwise.
        """
        expected_pattern = re.compile(r"^v\d+[a-zA-Z]*-\d+$")
        if not expected_pattern.match(tpu_accelerator_type):
            return False
        return True
```

For 7x, this is the returned tpu accelerator type tpu7x-16.

### Versions / Dependencies

N/A

```
# from raylet

I0000 00:00:1767851741.605813 1609 ssl_transport_security.cc:1884] Handshake failed with error SSL_ERROR_SSL: error:100000f7:SSLroutines:OPENSSL_internal:WRONG_VERSION_NUMBER: Invalid certificate verification context
```
So I suspect the above issues are coming from OTEL client missing setup SSL context when calling metrics agent? 

I asked claude to trace code and generated a fix with below diff, it does look like we are simply missing SSL context, but I haven't got a chance to test it locally.
```
diff --git a/src/ray/observability/open_telemetry_metric_recorder.cc b/src/ray/observability/open_telemetry_metric_recorder.cc
index 961c9d0c4b..ca28ce53c0 100644
--- a/src/ray/observability/open_telemetry_metric_recorder.cc
+++ b/src/ray/observability/open_telemetry_metric_recorder.cc
@@ -27,6 +27,8 @@

 #include <cassert>
+#include <fstream>
+#include <sstream>
 #include <utility>
 
 #include "ray/common/constants.h"
@@ -96,6 +98,47 @@ void OpenTelemetryMetricRecorder::Start(const std::string &endpoint,
   // counting.
   exporter_options.aggregation_temporality =
       opentelemetry::exporter::otlp::PreferredAggregationTemporality::kDelta;
+
+  // Configure TLS/SSL credentials to match how Ray's gRPC servers are configured.
+  // When USE_TLS is enabled, the dashboard agent's gRPC server uses SSL, so the
+  // OpenTelemetry exporter must also use SSL to connect successfully.
+  if (RayConfig::instance().USE_TLS()) {
+    exporter_options.use_ssl_credentials = true;
+
+    // Helper lambda to read certificate file contents
+    auto read_cert_file = [](const std::string &filepath) -> std::string {
+      std::ifstream file(filepath);
+      std::stringstream buffer;
+      buffer << file.rdbuf();
+      return buffer.str();
+    };
+
+    // Load CA certificate for server verification
+    std::string ca_cert_file = std::string(RayConfig::instance().TLS_CA_CERT());
+    if (!ca_cert_file.empty()) {
+      exporter_options.ssl_credentials_cacert_as_string = read_cert_file(ca_cert_file);
+    }
+
+#ifdef ENABLE_OTLP_GRPC_SSL_MTLS_PREVIEW
+    // Load client certificate and key for mutual TLS (mTLS).
+    // Ray's gRPC server requires client authentication when CA cert is configured.
+    // Note: mTLS support requires the OpenTelemetry SDK to be built with
+    // ENABLE_OTLP_GRPC_SSL_MTLS_PREVIEW defined.
+    std::string client_cert_file = std::string(RayConfig::instance().TLS_SERVER_CERT());
+    std::string client_key_file = std::string(RayConfig::instance().TLS_SERVER_KEY());
+    if (!client_cert_file.empty()) {
+      exporter_options.ssl_client_cert_string = read_cert_file(client_cert_file);
+    }
+    if (!client_key_file.empty()) {
+      exporter_options.ssl_client_key_string = read_cert_file(client_key_file);
+    }
+    RAY_LOG(INFO) << "OpenTelemetry metric exporter configured with TLS and mTLS enabled";
+#else
+    RAY_LOG(INFO) << "OpenTelemetry metric exporter configured with TLS enabled "
+                  << "(mTLS not available - SDK built without ENABLE_OTLP_GRPC_SSL_MTLS_PREVIEW)";
+#endif
+  }
+
   // Add authentication token to metadata if auth is enabled
   if (rpc::RequiresTokenAuthentication()) {
     auto token = rpc::AuthenticationTokenLoader::instance().GetToken();

```


Columns flattened via map_batches: False
Block shapes after first join: [(0, 12), (0, 12), (0, 12), (0, 12), (30000, 12), (0, 12), (30000, 12), (30000, 12), ...

A workaround to this is to repartition or materialize the first join to remove the empty blocks before doing the second join, but this can cause memory & spilling issues with large datasets. A better solution would be to remove the empty blocks, or to ensure that the second join can handle the empty blocks.

Additionally in testing i noted that if any operations are done to the empty blocks (map_batches, drop columns, etc.), they lose their shape (e.g. they go from having shape (0,n_cols) to being (0,0) in shape. The downstream join fails in both cases regardless.

### Versions / Dependencies

linux, ray 2.53.0

### Reproduction script

note, i had difficult reproducing the empty blocks with simpler datasets, it seems to be sensitive to the sorting of the data before the joins.

```python
import os
import logging
from typing import List, Any

import pandas as pd
import ray
from ray.data import from_pandas, DataContext, read_parquet

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

ctx = DataContext.get_current()
ctx.enable_progress_bars = False


def create_table_b_data(
    table_a_ids: List[str],
    table_b_ids: List[str],
    scenarios: List[str],
    num_time_steps: int = 3,
    base_value: float = 1.0,
) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Create table B data with time series values."""
    rows = []
    for table_a_id in table_a_ids:
        for scenario in scenarios:
            for table_b_id in table_b_ids:
                for time_step in range(num_time_steps):
                    rows.append(
                        {
                            "table_a_id": table_a_id,
                            "table_b_id": table_b_id,
                            "scenario": scenario,
                            "time_step": time_step,
                            "value": base_value * (time_step + 1),
                            "label": f"Test item {table_b_id}",
                        }
                    )

    return pd.DataFrame(rows).astype(
        {
            "table_a_id": "string",
            "table_b_id": "string",
            "scenario": "string",
            "time_step": "int64",
            "value": "float64",
            "label": "string",
        }
    )


def create_table_a_data(
    table_a_ids: List[str],
    scenario_ids: List[str],
    location_ids: List[str],
    table_b_ids: List[str],
    iterations: List[int],
    count_values: List[float],
) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Create table A data with assignments."""
    rows = []
    for table_a_id in table_a_ids:
        for scenario_id in scenario_ids:
            for location_id in location_ids:
                for iteration in iterations:
                    for i, table_b_id in enumerate(table_b_ids):
                        count = count_values[i % len(count_values)]
                        rows.append(
                            {
                                "table_a_id": table_a_id,
                                "scenario_id": scenario_id,
                                "location_id": location_id,
                                "iteration": iteration,
                                "table_b_id": table_b_id,
                                "count": count,
                                "multiplier": 1.0,
                                "id": f"{table_a_id}_{scenario_id}_{location_id}_{iteration}_{table_b_id}",
                            }
                        )

    return pd.DataFrame(rows).astype(
        {
            "table_a_id": "string",
            "scenario_id": "string",
            "location_id": "string",
            "iteration": "int64",
            "table_b_id": "string",
            "count": "float64",
            "multiplier": "float64",
            "id": "string",
        }
    )


def run_double_join_case(
    flatten_columns: bool,
    num_locations: int = 10,
    num_mappings: int = 5,
    num_groups: int = 3,
    num_table_a: int = 5,
    num_scenarios: int = 3,
    num_iterations: int = 20,
    num_table_b_per_a: int = 5,
    num_time_steps: int = 50,
    base_count: float = 5.0,
) -> None:
    """Test case demonstrating double join operation with Ray Data."""

    # Calculate expected dataset sizes
    expected_table_a = num_table_a * num_scenarios * num_locations * num_table_b_per_a * num_iterations
    expected_table_b = num_table_a * num_table_b_per_a * num_time_steps
    expected_joined = expected_table_a * num_time_steps
    max_mapping_rows = num_mappings * num_table_a * num_scenarios * num_iterations * num_time_steps
    max_group_rows = num_groups * num_table_a * num_scenarios * num_iterations * num_time_steps

    logger.info("========== Expected Dataset Sizes ==========")
    logger.info(f"Table A:               {expected_table_a:>12,} rows")
    logger.info(f"Table B:               {expected_table_b:>12,} rows")
    logger.info(f"After first join:      {expected_joined:>12,} rows")
    logger.info(f"Mapping table (max):   {max_mapping_rows:>12,} rows")
    logger.info(f"Group table (max):     {max_group_rows:>12,} rows")
    logger.info("============================================")

    suppress_progress: bool = True

    if suppress_progress:
        ctx = DataContext.get_current()
        ctx.enable_progress_bars = False

    # Generate test data at scale
    table_a_ids = [f"a_{i}" for i in range(num_table_a)]
    scenario_ids = [f"scenario_{i}" for i in range(num_scenarios)]
    location_ids = [f"loc_{i:06d}" for i in range(num_locations)]
    iterations = list(range(1, num_iterations + 1))

    # Create table A data
    all_table_a = []
    for a_id in table_a_ids:
        table_b_ids = [f"{a_id}_b_{j}" for j in range(num_table_b_per_a)]
        table_a = create_table_a_data([a_id], scenario_ids, location_ids, table_b_ids, iterations, [base_count])
        all_table_a.append(table_a)
    table_a_df = pd.concat(all_table_a, ignore_index=True)

    # Create table B data
    all_table_b = []
    for a_id in table_a_ids:
        table_b_ids = [f"{a_id}_b_{j}" for j in range(num_table_b_per_a)]
        table_b = create_table_b_data([a_id], table_b_ids, ["base"], num_time_steps=num_time_steps)
        all_table_b.append(table_b)
    table_b_df = pd.concat(all_table_b, ignore_index=True)

    # Create temporary directory for parquet files
    os.makedirs("./temp_data", exist_ok=True)

    # Write dataframes to parquet
    table_a_path = "./temp_data/table_a.parquet"
    table_b_path = "./temp_data/table_b.parquet"

    table_a_df.to_parquet(table_a_path, index=False)
    table_b_df.to_parquet(table_b_path, index=False)

    # Read from parquet files
    table_a_ds = read_parquet(table_a_path)
    table_b_ds = read_parquet(table_b_path)

    rows_per_part = 40_000_000
    n_parts = 64
    n_table_a = table_a_ds.count()
    n_table_b = table_b_ds.count()
    n_parts = max(n_parts, (n_table_a * n_table_b) // rows_per_part)
    print("Using Partitons: ", n_parts)

    # First join: table A with table B on shared keys
    ds_joined = table_a_ds.join(
        table_b_ds,
        join_type="left_outer",
        num_partitions=n_parts,
        on=("table_a_id", "table_b_id"),  # type: ignore
    )

    if flatten_columns:
        ds_joined = ds_joined.map_batches(lambda row: row)

    # Print block shapes after first join
    shapes: List[Any] = [ray.get(block).shape for block in ds_joined.get_internal_block_refs()]  # type: ignore
    print(f"Columns flattened via map_batches: {flatten_columns}")
    print("Block shapes after first join:", shapes)

    # Create mapping table
    # Use some of the location_ids for the mapping
    shared_location_ids = location_ids[: max(1, len(location_ids) // 3)]
    unshared_location_ids = [f"loc_{i:06d}" for i in range(num_locations, num_locations + len(location_ids) // 3)]
    mapping_location_ids = shared_location_ids + unshared_location_ids

    # Create mapping
    mapping_ids = [f"mapping_{i:04d}" for i in range(num_mappings)]
    mapping_data: dict[str, Any] = {
        "location_id": mapping_location_ids,
        "mapping_id": [mapping_ids[i % len(mapping_ids)] for i in range(len(mapping_location_ids))],
    }
    mapping_df: pd.DataFrame = pd.DataFrame(mapping_data)
    ds_mapping = from_pandas(mapping_df)

    # Second join: add mapping data
    ds_double_joined = ds_joined.join(
        ds_mapping,
        on=("location_id",),
        join_type="left_outer",
        num_partitions=n_parts,
    )

    result: pd.DataFrame = ds_double_joined.to_pandas()
    print(f"Final result shape: {result.shape}")
    print(f"Columns: {list(result.columns)}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    ray.init(ignore_reinit_error=True)
    run_double_join_case(False)
    # run_double_join_case(True)

```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#60013.title: [Data] Dataset Left-Outer join produces empty blocks, causing errors in downstream joins

ray-project/ray#60026.body:
Currently, StreamingRepartition operator is essentially strict=True. We want to relax this to allow non-strict mode with following guarantees:

Strict mode: is guaranteeing that all output blocks, but maybe last, will be of size target_num_rows

Non-strict mode: will provide more relaxed guarantee – it can produce 1 block that is < `target_num_rows` blocks per each input block (ie it wouldn’t do any stitching)

This mode will be the default mode and would allow StreamingRepartition to be fused into previous operator
ray-project/ray#60026.title: [Data] Support strict=False mode for StreamingRepartition

ray-project/ray#60027.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

* `includes_paths=True` does not add `paths` to the schema
* it would fail some checks in the `groupby`

For example, `ds = ray.data.read_parquet("data/", include_paths=True)`, gives
```python
In [24]: ds
Out[24]: Dataset(num_rows=?, schema={id: int64, data: double, uuid: string})
```
without the expected path column.

Then if we want to do
```python
ds.groupby("path").count().take_all()
```
It fails in `SortKey.validate_schema(self, schema)`:
```python
     81 for column in self._columns:
     82     if column not in schema_names_set:
---> 83         raise ValueError(
     84             f"You specified the column '{column}', but there's no such "
     85             "column in the dataset. The dataset has columns: "
     86             f"{schema.names}"
     87         )

ValueError: You specified the column 'path', but there's no such column in the dataset. The dataset has columns: ['id', 'data', 'uuid']
```

For debugging purpose, it would work if:
* disable that line of check
* or use materialize()

master


ray-project/ray#60041.body:
### Description

The documentation for [Placement Groups](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/ray-core/scheduling/placement-group.html#advanced-child-tasks-and-actors) contains the following warning:

> **Warning**
> The value of ``placement_group_capture_child_tasks`` for a given actor isn't inherited from its parent. If you're creating nested actors of depth greater than 1 and should all use the same placement group, you should explicitly set ``placement_group_capture_child_tasks`` explicitly set for each actor.

However, in the Python SDK, this behavior **is inherited** by default. When an Actor (or Task) is created with `placement_group_capture_child_tasks=True`, its children automatically inherit this setting and are scheduled in the same placement group, unless explicitly overridden.

In `ray/actor.py` (and similarly in `ray/remote_function.py`), the client explicitly queries the worker context to inherit the setting if it is not provided by the user:

```python
# ray/actor.py around line 1732
if placement_group_capture_child_tasks is None:
    placement_group_capture_child_tasks = (
        worker.should_capture_child_tasks_in_placement_group
    )
placement_group = _configure_placement_group_based_on_context(
    placement_group_capture_child_tasks,
    placement_group_bundle_index,
    resources,
    actor_placement_resources,
    meta.class_name,
    placement_group=placement_group,
)
```
Or am I misinterpreting the meaning of this warning?

### Link

https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/ray-core/scheduling/placement-group.html#advanced-child-tasks-and-actors
ray-project/ray#60041.title: [Core][Doc] `placement_group_capture_child_tasks` is inherited in Python SDK, contradicting documentation
