ray-project/ray#61354.body:
## Summary

When running a TPCH Q5–style query implemented with Ray Data (multiple chained joins: region → nation → customer / supplier → orders → lineitem), the job intermittently fails during a hash shuffle join with:

> `pyarrow.lib.ArrowInvalid: No match or multiple matches for key field reference FieldRef.Name(c_nationkey) on right side of the join`

The failure is raised from PyArrow's `Table.join` inside Ray Data's `JoiningAggregation.finalize` and is reported as a `ray.data.exceptions.SystemException`.

From the user-facing Ray Data schema perspective, all intermediate datasets have **clean, non-duplicated join key columns**; the error appears to come from the way Ray Data constructs internal PyArrow tables and schemas for the final join, where the **right side can end up with multiple columns named like the join key** (e.g. `c_nationkey`), triggering this PyArrow restriction.

## Repro steps
python release/nightly_tests/dataset/tpch/tpch_q5.py --sf 1

https://github.com/daiping8/ray/blob/feea4bc07e5853f1084f8f28ffccf8dd853829f4/release/nightly_tests/dataset/tpch/tpch_q5.py

## Expected behavior

The long chain of joins (region/nation/customer/supplier/orders/lineitem) should successfully execute and return the TPCH Q5 result, as long as:

- the user-facing Ray Data schemas have no duplicate join key column names on each side of a join, and
- all join keys are of joinable types.

## Actual behavior

The job fails internally during the join finalization:

- The Ray Data physical plan shows a `Join(num_partitions=16)` followed by a `HashAggregate` and `Sort`.
- No user code is executing at the point of failure; the exception comes from the Ray Data executor.

```text
ray.exceptions.RayTaskError(ArrowInvalid): ray::HashShuffleAggregator.finalize()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/ray/data/_internal/execution/operators/join.py", line 140, in finalize
    supported = preprocess_result_l.supported_projection.join(
  File "pyarrow/table.pxi", line 5766, in pyarrow.lib.Table.join
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/pyarrow/acero.py", line 253, in _perform_join
    result_table = decl.to_table(use_threads=use_threads)
  File "pyarrow/_acero.pyx", line 591, in pyarrow._acero.Declaration.to_table
  File "pyarrow/error.pxi", line 155, in pyarrow.lib.pyarrow_internal_check_status
  File "pyarrow/error.pxi", line 92, in pyarrow.lib.check_status
pyarrow.lib.ArrowInvalid: No match or multiple matches for key field reference FieldRef.Name(c_nationkey) on right side of the join

ray.data.exceptions.SystemException
```

The same pattern has also been observed for other key names in similar pipelines (e.g. `n_nationkey`), always on the "right side of the join".

## Analysis / suspected root cause

From the Ray Data and PyArrow internals:

- Ray Data's `JoinOperator` uses `HashShufflingOperatorBase` with a `JoiningAggregation`:

  ```python
  # python/ray/data/_internal/execution/operators/join.py
  class JoiningAggregation(ShuffleAggregation):
      def finalize(self, partition_shards_map: Dict[int, List[Block]]) -> Iterator[Block]:
          left_table = _combine(left_partition_shards)
          right_table = _combine(right_partition_shards)

          left_on = list(self._left_key_col_names)
          right_on = list(self._right_key_col_names)

          # Preprocess: split unsupported columns and add index columns if needed
          preprocess_result_l, preprocess_result_r = self._preprocess(
              left_table, right_table, left_on, right_on
          )

          # Perform the join on supported columns
          arrow_join_type = _JOIN_TYPE_TO_ARROW_JOIN_VERB_MAP[self._join_type]

          supported = preprocess_result_l.supported_projection.join(
              preprocess_result_r.supported_projection,
              join_type=arrow_join_type,
              keys=left_on,
              right_keys=right_on,
              left_suffix=self._left_columns_suffix,
              right_suffix=self._right_columns_suffix,
          )
  ```
- `_preprocess` currently:

  - splits each side into `supported_projection` and `unsupported_projection` based on column types,
  - may add index columns if unsupported columns are present,
  - but **does not guarantee that join key names are unique within one side's** **`supported_projection`** **schema**.
- In long join chains (as in TPCH Q5), even if the logical Ray Data schemas avoid duplicate key names via `select_columns` and `rename_columns` at the Python level, the combination of: multiple joins, suffix handling, and repartition/shuffle, can still produce a right-hand table where **multiple columns share the same name as a join key** (e.g. multiple `c_nationkey` columns), from PyArrow's perspective.

PyArrow's `Table.join` is strict here: for each key name on the right side, there must be **exactly one** field with that name. If there is 0 or >1, it raises `ArrowInvalid: No match or multiple matches for key field reference FieldRef.Name(...)`.

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.53

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#61354.title: [Data] Join fails on TPCH Q5 multi-join pipelines

ray-project/ray#61359.body:
### Streaming path skips all tracing attribute setup **Medium Severity** When a client-streaming or bidi-streaming request is handled, `send_request_to_replica` returns early at line 878, completely skipping the tracing setup at lines 881–919. The `_send_streaming_request_to_replica` method has no equivalent tracing logic either. While the `@tracing_decorator_factory` decorator still creates a span, that span will lack all useful attributes (`request_id`, `deployment`, `app`, `request_type`), the descriptive span name, RPC span attributes, exception tracking via `set_span_exception`, and trace status. This creates a significant observability gap for streaming requests in production.
Additional Locations (1) - [`python/ray/serve/_private/proxy.py#L924-L981`](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/d16e7bb76d9fc02b6ab718ec0783aab109eac985/python/ray/serve/_private/proxy.py#L924-L981)

 

_Originally posted by @cursor[bot] in https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/60770#discussion_r2851013912_
ray-project/ray#61359.title: [Serve] Grpc client Streaming path skips all tracing attribute setup

`read_parquet` applies `file_extensions=["parquet"]`.

ray-project/ray#61384.body:
### Description

Ray compiled graph requires cupy-cuda12, which causes issues on CUDA 13. See: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/7b2b33a9ce68c207e0ed2c672797aac816d1e8c1/python/setup.py#L227-L230


This is related to https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/35315 and https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/172663 
Both PyTorch and vLLM are planning to move to CUDA 13, and vLLM requires `ray[cgraph]`

Note that a workaround is to install `ray` and `cupy-cuda13` instead of `ray[cgraph]`

### Use case

_No response_

ray-project/ray#61405.body:
Larger Issue
Refactored the execution callback system from dynamic, per-job injection to static, eager initialization. DataContext now serves as a stateless registry of callback factories, enabling the StreamingExecutor to load all callbacks at startup. Each callback then self-configures or no-ops based on the runtime environment.

Context
This is Part 3 of a four-part change set intended to resolve the issue described above.

Part 1 diff: #60480
Part 2 diff: #61293

Closes #60279.
ray-project/ray#61405.title: [Data] Make execution callback dataflow explicit to prevent state leakage

ray-project/ray#61464.body:
Auxiliary actors like `PrefixTreeActor` need to outlive any single replica but be cleaned up on deployment deletion and `serve.shutdown()`. The current `_register_shutdown_cleanup_actor` mechanism only handles full shutdown, not per-deployment deletion, and does not persist across controller restarts.

### Use Cases

1. **Prefix cache tracking** — `PrefixTreeActor` tracks prefix cache state across replicas for the `PrefixCacheAffinityRouter`. Must be a singleton shared by all replicas/routers of a deployment.  
2. **Routing token manager** — A central actor that manages routing tokens for a deployment. The request router consults this actor to acquire a token before dispatching each request and releases it on completion. Enables centralized concurrency/rate control across all proxies and replicas without distributed coordination.

## API

### Option \#1

Imperative

```py
from ray import serve
from ray.serve.config import DeploymentActorConfig

@ray.remote(num_cpus=1, ...)
# user can provide actor remote options like runtime_env here
# and that will take precedence over runtime_env of deployment
class PrefixTreeActor:
	pass

@serve.deployment(
    deployment_actors={
        "prefix_tree": DeploymentActorConfig(
            actor_class=PrefixTreeActor,
            init_kwargs={"max_depth": 100},
		# giving user the option to specify actor options
		# in serve deployment because its not necessary
		# that user always owns the actor code
		# in the case where serve ships some default actor
		# user should still be able to specify
		# actor options.
            actor_options={"num_cpus": 0.1},
        ),
    },
)
class MyDeployment:
    def __init__(self):
        self.tree = serve.get_deployment_actor("prefix_tree")

    def __call__(self, request):
        ray.get(self.tree.insert.remote(request.text))

```

Declarative (YAML)

```
applications:
  - name: my_app
    import_path: my_module:app
    deployments:
      - name: MyDeployment
        deployment_actors:
          prefix_tree:
            actor_class: my_module:PrefixTreeActor
            init_kwargs:
              max_depth: 100
            actor_options:
              num_cpus: 0.1
```

### Option \#2

Deployment actors in these cases exist solely to support their routing policy (e.g., `PrefixTreeActor` only serves `PrefixTreeRoutingPolicy`). Requiring users to declare the policy in `routing_policy_class` and again in `deployment_actors` is redundant and conceptually awkward—policy and actor form a single logical unit. However, keeping the actor declaration separate has practical benefits. The policy is decision logic, while the actor is a stateful store.

Creating actors directly from replica user code has the following quirks

1. All replicas need to make an RPC call to the controller with instructions to inject the actor in the deployment state. This is an odd design, nonetheless, we can guarantee atomicity by using `Locks` and `get_if_exists` API from core  
2. Instructions to place actor have to be a non-blocking and asynchronous call to the controller because of the reconsiler pattern used in the controller. Injecting an actor in the deployment state and creating an actor out of band from the reconsiler loop could have side effects that are difficult to reason about. Adding actor details to the deployment state is important to ensure FT.

```py
@serve.deployment_actor(...)
class PrefixTreeActor:
def _init_(self, ...)

class RoutingPolicy: # or deployment
	def __init__(self, ..., actor_init_kwargs, actor_options):
		# we need app and deployment name because actor can be called
		# from router in upstream deployment
		# dont call bind from driver
		tree_fut = PrefixTreeActor.options(
app_name=app_name,
deployment_name=deployment_name,
**actor_options
).bind(**actor_init_kwargs)

# bind returns a future that I can do ray.get() 
self.tree = ray.get(tree_fut)
```

Declarative (YAML)

```
applications:
  - name: my_app
    import_path: my_module:app
    deployments:
      - name: MyDeployment
        request_router_class: RoutingPolicy
	 request_router_kwargs:
        actor_init_kwargs:
          max_depth: 100
        actor_options:
          num_cpus: 0.1
```

## 

**Single Actor Limitations and Scalability**

1. A single, shared actor is not a scalable solution for all use cases.  
2. The introduction of deployment-scoped actors is intended to provide users with an option for use cases that have low throughput requirements and involve low-fidelity communication between replicas and the actor.  
3. For scenarios demanding high scale, such as routing policies for high throughput scenarios, the eventual transition would be to a distributed database, moving away from a shared single actor model.  
4. In the long run, this deployment-scoped actor API is anticipated to be most valuable for coordinating replicas within a deployment infrequently, or for handling background asynchronous operations.

## Core Data Structure

```py
# python/ray/serve/_private/config.py

class DeploymentActorConfig(BaseModel):
    """Configuration for a deployment-scoped actor."""

    actor_class: Union[type, str]            # Class ref or import path
    _serialized_actor_class: bytes = b""      # Set internally via cloudpickle
    init_args: Tuple[Any, ...] = ()
    init_kwargs: Dict[str, Any] = {}
    actor_options: Dict[str, Any] = {}       # Ray actor option overrides
```

New field on `DeploymentConfig`:

```py
class DeploymentConfig(BaseModel):
    ...
    deployment_actors: Optional[Dict[str, DeploymentActorConfig]] = Field(
        default=None,
        update_type=DeploymentOptionUpdateType.HeavyWeight,
    )
```

`HeavyWeight` ensures any change to deployment actors triggers a full redeployment.

## Actor Naming

```
SERVE_DEPLOYMENT_ACTOR::{app_name}::{deployment_name}::{actor_name}
```

All actors use `namespace=SERVE_NAMESPACE` (`"serve"`). Names are deterministic and derivable from `DeploymentConfig`, so no separate KV store checkpoint is needed.

## Lifecycle

### Creation

`DeploymentStateManager` creates actors **before** starting replicas (before STEP 4 of the update loop):

```py
actor_cls.options(
    name=deterministic_name,
    namespace=SERVE_NAMESPACE,
    lifetime="detached",
    get_if_exists=True,
    max_restart=-1,
    **merged_actor_options,
).remote(*init_args, **init_kwargs)
```

`get_if_exists=True` makes creation idempotent.

### Runtime Environment

The deployment's `runtime_env` is inherited by default. User-specified `actor_options` override:

```py
deployment_runtime_env = replica_config.ray_actor_options.get("runtime_env", {})
actor_runtime_env = actor_config.actor_options.get("runtime_env", {})
merged_runtime_env = {**deployment_runtime_env, **actor_runtime_env}
```

### Controller Crash Recovery

On controller restart:

1. `DeploymentStateManager` recovers `DeploymentTargetState` from checkpoint (existing behavior).  
2. Target state contains `DeploymentConfig` which contains `deployment_actors`.  
3. For each actor, attempt `ray.get_actor(deterministic_name, namespace=SERVE_NAMESPACE)`:  
   - **Found** → store handle, actor is alive.  
   - **Not found** → recreate (data loss accepted, same as replica recovery).

No additional checkpoint data required.

### Deletion

Actors are killed **after all replicas have stopped**, in STEP 9 of `DeploymentStateManager.update()`:

```py
# deployment_state.py — STEP 9: Cleanup
for deployment_id in deleted_ids:
    self._kill_deployment_actors(deployment_id)   # ← NEW
    self._deployment_scheduler.on_deployment_deleted(deployment_id)
    self._autoscaling_state_manager.deregister_deployment(deployment_id)
    del self._deployment_states[deployment_id]
    ...
```

Ordering guarantee: `deleted_ids` is only populated after `check_curr_status()` confirms all replicas are stopped (STEP 5), so actors outlive every replica.

### Shutdown

`DeploymentStateManager.shutdown()` calls `delete()` on all deployments, which flows through the same STEP 9 cleanup. No separate shutdown path needed.

### Redeployment (Actor Config Changes)

| Change | Action |
| :---- | :---- |
| Actor added | Create new actor |
| Actor removed | Kill old actor |
| Actor class changed | Kill old, create new |
| init\_args/kwargs changed | Kill and recreate (state loss expected) |

## Replica Access

```py
# python/ray/serve/context.py

def get_deployment_actor(actor_name: str) -> ray.actor.ActorHandle:
    """Get a handle to a deployment-scoped actor by name.

    Must be called from within a running Serve replica.
    """
    deployment_id = _get_current_deployment_id()
    full_name = (
        f"SERVE_DEPLOYMENT_ACTOR::{deployment_id.app_name}"
        f"::{deployment_id.name}::{actor_name}"
    )
    return ray.get_actor(full_name, namespace=SERVE_NAMESPACE)
```

Exposed as `serve.get_deployment_actor()`.

## Error Handling

- **Actor creation failure** → deployment marked `UNHEALTHY`. Controller retries with backoff.  
- **Actor death after creation** → replicas receive `ActorDiedError` and restart. On restart they call `get_deployment_actor()` again; the controller recreates the actor through its recovery path.

## Serialization

### Protobuf

New message in `src/ray/protobuf/serve.proto`:

```protobuf
message DeploymentActorConfig {
    bytes _serialized_actor_class = 1;
    bytes serialized_init_args = 2;
    bytes serialized_init_kwargs = 3;
    bytes serialized_actor_options = 4;
}

message DeploymentConfig {
    ...
    map<string, DeploymentActorConfig> deployment_actors = <next_field>;
}
```

All fields are cloudpickled bytes, matching how `ReplicaConfig` handles `serialized_deployment_def`.

### Schema (Declarative Config)

New schema in `python/ray/serve/schema.py`:

```py
class DeploymentActorSchema(BaseModel):
    actor_class: str         # Import path, e.g. "my_module:PrefixTreeActor"
    init_args: List = []
    init_kwargs: Dict = {}
    actor_options: Dict = {}

class DeploymentSchema(BaseModel):
    ...
    deployment_actors: Optional[Dict[str, DeploymentActorSchema]] = Field(
        default=DEFAULT.VALUE,
    )
```

`init_args` and `init_kwargs` must be JSON-serializable in declarative mode.
ray-project/ray#61464.title: [Serve][RFC] Controller-Managed Deployment-Scoped Actors

ray-project/ray#61474.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The bug: When generating TaskLifecycleEvent messages for the task lifecycle event export format, the node_id field is being populated with the emitting node's ID for all task status events. Currently, Ray unconditionally sets TaskLifecycleEvent.node_id to the emitting node, then overwrites it with the executor node only for SUBMITTED_TO_WORKER events. This also enables race conditions to occur where SUBMITTED_TO_WORKER state can be associated with the submitter node and not the executor node.

Expected Behavior: TaskLifecycleEvent.node_id should only ever be set to the executor's node ID, and not the submittor's node ID.

### Versions / Dependencies

Issue appears in Ray 2.54

### Reproduction script

Any script which uses tasks will reproduce this.

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#61474.title: [core] TaskLifecycleEvent.node_id populated with emitting node instead of just executor node

ray-project/ray#61493.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

With the upgrades of ray 2.54 to ray 2.53 I now have an issue with Algorithm.evaluate() from **ray/rllib/algorithms/algorithm.py**.

The error comes from a change in algorithm.py between 2.53 and 2.54 : 

  2.53 — the no_eval_results warning guard worked like this:

  ```
eval_results = self.metrics.peek(
      key=EVALUATION_RESULTS, default={}, latest_merged_only=True,
  )
  if log_once("no_eval_results") and not eval_results:
      logger.warning(...)
```

  2.54 — changed to:

```
  if log_once("no_eval_results") and not self.metrics.peek(
      (EVALUATION_RESULTS, ENV_RUNNER_RESULTS)  # ←  NO default
  ):
      logger.warning(...)

```

The default argument was dropped. When default=None, peek() calls _get_key(key, key_error=True), which raises on missing keys.

### Versions / Dependencies

ray 2.54

### Reproduction script

-

### Issue Severity

None
ray-project/ray#61493.title: [RLlib] KeyError: 'env_runners' in Ray 2.54 Algorithm.evaluate() | Regression from 2.53

ray-project/ray#61520.body:
When using the Ray Event Export API to export `TASK_PROFILE_EVENT` to an external HTTP endpoint, the exported profile events for each task attempt are **incomplete** compared to the Ray Dashboard `/api/v0/tasks/timeline` (Chrome tracing) output.

For the same task attempt:

- The **Dashboard timeline** shows the full set of phases:
  - `task:deserialize_arguments`
  - `task:execute`
  - `task:store_outputs`
  - `task::<func_name>` (overall task span)
- The **Event Export API** often exports only a single profile event per `(taskId, componentType)` in `taskProfileEvents.profileEvents.events`, e.g. only:
  - `task::my_task`, or
  - `task::Counter.increment`

This means the exported events are not sufficient to reconstruct the full task execution timeline, even though the Dashboard timeline is complete.

Driver-side `submit_task` profile events *are* present (with `componentType = "driver"`), but worker-side phases (`task:deserialize_arguments`, `task:execute`, `task:store_outputs`) are frequently missing.

  

### Versions / Dependencies

### Environment

- Ray version: **2.52.0**
- Deployment: **KubeRay RayJob** with inline `rayClusterSpec`
- Event Export configuration (on both head and worker pods):

  - name: RAY_enable_ray_event
    value: "true"
  - name: RAY_enable_core_worker_ray_event_to_aggregator
    value: "true"
  - name: RAY_DASHBOARD_AGGREGATOR_AGENT_EVENTS_EXPORT_ADDR
    value: "http://flask-event-receiver:8085/events"
  - name: RAY_DASHBOARD_AGGREGATOR_AGENT_EXPOSABLE_EVENT_TYPES
    value: "TASK_DEFINITION_EVENT,TASK_LIFECYCLE_EVENT,ACTOR_TASK_DEFINITION_EVENT,TASK_PROFILE_EVENT,DRIVER_JOB_DEFINITION_EVENT,DRIVER_JOB_LIFECYCLE_EVENT,ACTOR_DEFINITION_EVENT,ACTOR_LIFECYCLE_EVENT,NODE_DEFINITION_EVENT,NODE_LIFECYCLE_EVENT"

### Reproduction script

I use a simple Flask HTTP receiver (as a Deployment + Service), and a RayJob that embeds a RayCluster spec. The Ray Event Export API sends events to the Flask service.
## 1. Flask receiver (Deployment + Service)
```yaml
# flask-receiver.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: flask-event-receiver
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: flask-receiver
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: flask-receiver
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: flask
        image: python:3.9
        command:
          - /bin/sh
          - -c
          - |
            pip install flask
            python -c "
            from flask import Flask, request
            import json
            from datetime import datetime
            app = Flask(__name__)
            @app.route('/events', methods=['POST'])
            def receive_events():
                events = request.json
                print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Received {len(events)} events')
                for event in events:
                    if event.get('eventType') == 'TASK_PROFILE_EVENT':
                        print(json.dumps(event, indent=2))
                return {'status': 'ok'}, 200
            app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=8085)
            "
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8085
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: flask-event-receiver
spec:
  selector:
    app: flask-receiver
  ports:
  - port: 8085
    targetPort: 8085
```
## 2. RayJob with inline RayCluster spec
```yaml
# rayjob-test.yaml
apiVersion: ray.io/v1
kind: RayJob
metadata:
  name: rayjob
spec:
  entrypoint: |
    python -c "
    import ray
    import time
    from ray.util.placement_group import placement_group
    ray.init()

    @ray.remote(num_cpus=0.5)
    def my_task(x):
        print(f'Processing {x}', flush=True)
        return x * 2

    @ray.remote(num_cpus=0.5)
    class Counter:
        def __init__(self):
            self.count = 0

        def increment(self):
            self.count += 1
            return self.count

        def get_count(self):
            return self.count

    task_result = ray.get(my_task.remote(1))
    print(f'Task result: {task_result}')

    counter = Counter.remote()
    for i in range(1):
        count = ray.get(counter.increment.remote())
        print(f'Counter: {count}')

    final_count = ray.get(counter.get_count.remote())
    print(f'Final count: {final_count}')
    print(f'Cluster resources: {ray.cluster_resources()}')

    pg = placement_group([{'CPU': 0.5}], strategy='SPREAD', lifetime='detached', name='test_pg')
    ray.get(pg.ready())
    print(f'Placement group created: {pg.bundle_specs}')

    time.sleep(5)
    "
  rayClusterSpec:
    rayVersion: "2.52.0"
    headGroupSpec:
      rayStartParams:
        dashboard-host: 0.0.0.0
        num-cpus: "0"
      serviceType: ClusterIP
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: ray-head
            image: rayproject/ray:2.52.0
            imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
            env:
            - name: RAY_enable_ray_event
              value: "true"
            - name: RAY_enable_core_worker_ray_event_to_aggregator
              value: "true"
            - name: RAY_DASHBOARD_AGGREGATOR_AGENT_EVENTS_EXPORT_ADDR
              value: "http://flask-event-receiver:8085/events"
            - name: RAY_DASHBOARD_AGGREGATOR_AGENT_EXPOSABLE_EVENT_TYPES
              value: "TASK_DEFINITION_EVENT,TASK_LIFECYCLE_EVENT,ACTOR_TASK_DEFINITION_EVENT,TASK_PROFILE_EVENT,DRIVER_JOB_DEFINITION_EVENT,DRIVER_JOB_LIFECYCLE_EVENT,ACTOR_DEFINITION_EVENT,ACTOR_LIFECYCLE_EVENT,NODE_DEFINITION_EVENT,NODE_LIFECYCLE_EVENT"
            resources:
              limits:
                cpu: "2"
                memory: 4G
              requests:
                cpu: "100m"
                memory: 512Mi
    workerGroupSpecs:
    - groupName: cpu
      replicas: 1
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: ray-worker
            image: rayproject/ray:2.52.0
            imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
            env:
            - name: RAY_enable_ray_event
              value: "true"
            - name: RAY_enable_core_worker_ray_event_to_aggregator
              value: "1"
            - name: RAY_DASHBOARD_AGGREGATOR_AGENT_EVENTS_EXPORT_ADDR
              value: "http://flask-event-receiver:8085/events"
            - name: RAY_DASHBOARD_AGGREGATOR_AGENT_EXPOSABLE_EVENT_TYPES
              value: "TASK_DEFINITION_EVENT,TASK_LIFECYCLE_EVENT,ACTOR_TASK_DEFINITION_EVENT,TASK_PROFILE_EVENT,DRIVER_JOB_DEFINITION_EVENT,DRIVER_JOB_LIFECYCLE_EVENT,ACTOR_DEFINITION_EVENT,ACTOR_LIFECYCLE_EVENT,NODE_DEFINITION_EVENT,NODE_LIFECYCLE_EVENT"
            resources:
              limits:
                cpu: "1"
                memory: 2G
              requests:
                cpu: "100m"
                memory: 512Mi
```
## 3. Steps to reproduce
### 1. Apply the Flask receiver:
```
kubectl apply -f flask-receiver.yaml
```
### 2. Apply the RayJob:
```
   kubectl apply -f rayjob-test.yaml
```
### 3. Wait for the job to finish.
### 4. Collect Event Export API payloads from the Flask logs:
```
   kubectl logs -f deployment/flask-event-receiver
```
### 5. Collect the Dashboard timeline for the same job:
```
   # Port-forward the dashboard (replace with your head service name)
   kubectl port-forward svc/<ray-head-service-name> 8265:8265

   curl "http://127.0.0.1:8265/api/v0/tasks/timeline" > dashboard-timeline.json
```
### 6. Compare the two:
- dashboard-timeline.json contains, for each task, phases:
   - `task:deserialize_arguments`
   - `task:execute`
   - `task:store_outputs`
   - `task::<func_name>`
- Flask logs show, for the same taskId, only a single event under taskProfileEvents.profileEvents.events in many cases (e.g. only task::my_task), meaning earlier phases are missing from the exported events.

I traced the C++ code and my current hypothesis is:
- In `src/ray/core_worker/task_event_buffer.cc`, the method `TaskProfileEvent::ToRpcRayEvents(RayEventsTuple &ray_events_tuple)` uses `ray_events_tuple.task_profile_event.emplace()` unconditionally for each profile event of the same task attempt within a single flush.
- This overwrites the previous `taskProfileEvents.profileEvents.events` content in `RayEventsTuple` instead of appending, so only the last phase for that (taskId, attemptNumber, componentType) survives in the final exported batch.
- The Dashboard timeline path reads from GCS `TaskEvents`, where `profile_events` is an `optional ProfileEvents` field, and merging is done with `MergeFrom`, which for the same component_type appends `ProfileEventEntry` items to events. As a result, the Dashboard timeline retains all phases while the Event Export path drops earlier ones.

## Question
Is the current `TaskProfileEvent::ToRpcRayEvents` behavior (overwriting `task_profile_event` on each call within a flush) intended, or is this a bug?

## Expected behavior
- For each (taskId, attemptNumber, componentType), the Event Export API should export all profile phases that are present in the Dashboard timeline:
   - `task:deserialize_arguments`
   - `task:execute`
   - `task:store_outputs`
   - `task::<func_name>`
- `taskProfileEvents.profileEvents.events` should contain a list of all these phases, not just a single final phase per flush.
ray-project/ray#61520.title: [Bug] Missing task profile phases in Ray Event Export API (TASK_PROFILE_EVENT)

ray-project/ray#61551.body:
## Version

- Ray nightly
- `ray.__version__ = 3.0.0.dev0`
- `ray.__commit__ = 3956d0d7db31cd7769936f0d84856b44a41c0c11`

## Summary

I found a small high-level repro where a composed streaming Serve deployment stays at 2 backend replicas long after all real backend work has drained.

The suspicious part is that `ray_serve_autoscaling_total_requests` periodically jumps back up even when:

- backend inflight is `0`
- all requests have completed
- the deployment should already have downscaled according to `look_back_period_s + downscale_delay_s`

I also built a non-streaming control with the same structure and load shape. The non-streaming version downscales normally.

## Copy/paste repro

Save this as `server.py`:

```python
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import asyncio
import re
import sys
import threading
import time
import urllib.request

import ray
from ray import serve
from ray.serve.handle import DeploymentHandle
from ray.util.state import list_actors
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import StreamingResponse

sys.stdout.reconfigure(line_buffering=True)

APP_NAME = "autoscaling-streaming-repro"
BACKEND_NAME = "Backend"
ROUTE_PREFIX = "/repro"

N_INGRESS = 4
MIN_REPLICAS = 1
MAX_REPLICAS = 2
TARGET_ONGOING = 2
MAX_ONGOING = 4
NUM_CHUNKS = 20
CHUNK_DELAY_S = 0.15
UPSCALE_DELAY_S = 2
DOWNSCALE_DELAY_S = 8
METRICS_INTERVAL_S = 1
LOOK_BACK_PERIOD_S = 5
STREAM = True

@ray.remote
class StatsActor:
    def __init__(self):
        self.current_inflight = 0
        self.finished = 0

    def on_start(self):
        self.current_inflight += 1

    def on_finish(self):
        self.current_inflight -= 1
        self.finished += 1

    def snapshot(self):
        return {"current_inflight": self.current_inflight, "finished": self.finished}

@serve.deployment(
    name=BACKEND_NAME,
    autoscaling_config={
        "min_replicas": MIN_REPLICAS,
        "max_replicas": MAX_REPLICAS,
        "target_ongoing_requests": TARGET_ONGOING,
        "upscale_delay_s": UPSCALE_DELAY_S,
        "downscale_delay_s": DOWNSCALE_DELAY_S,
        "metrics_interval_s": METRICS_INTERVAL_S,
        "look_back_period_s": LOOK_BACK_PERIOD_S,
    },
    max_ongoing_requests=MAX_ONGOING,
    graceful_shutdown_timeout_s=1,
)
class Backend:
    def __init__(self, stats):
        self._stats = stats

    async def stream(self):
        await self._stats.on_start.remote()
        try:
            for i in range(NUM_CHUNKS):
                yield f"{i}\n".encode()
                await asyncio.sleep(CHUNK_DELAY_S)
        finally:
            await self._stats.on_finish.remote()

    async def __call__(self):
        await self._stats.on_start.remote()
        try:
            await asyncio.sleep(NUM_CHUNKS * CHUNK_DELAY_S)
            return {"ok": True}
        finally:
            await self._stats.on_finish.remote()

@serve.deployment(num_replicas=N_INGRESS, max_ongoing_requests=1000)
class Ingress:
    def __init__(self, backend: DeploymentHandle):
        self._stream = STREAM
        if self._stream:
            self._backend = backend.options(stream=True, method_name="stream")
        else:
            self._backend = backend

    async def __call__(self, request: Request):
        if self._stream:
            return StreamingResponse(self._backend.remote(), media_type="text/plain")
        return await self._backend.remote()

def count_backend_replicas() -> int:
    actors = list_actors(filters=[("state", "=", "ALIVE")])
    class_name = f"ServeReplica:{APP_NAME}:{BACKEND_NAME}"
    return sum(1 for actor in actors if actor["class_name"] == class_name)

def get_metric_value(metric_name: str) -> float:
    head = ray.nodes()[0]
    url = f"http://{head['NodeManagerAddress']}:{head['MetricsExportPort']}"
    with urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=2) as response:
        metrics_text = response.read().decode("utf-8")

    required_tags = {"application": APP_NAME, "deployment": BACKEND_NAME}
    total = 0.0
    for line in metrics_text.splitlines():
        if not line.startswith(metric_name):
            continue
        tags = {}
        if "{" in line:
            for pair in line.split("{", 1)[1].split("}", 1)[0].split(","):
                if pair:
                    key, value = pair.split("=", 1)
                    tags[key] = value.strip('"')
        if not required_tags.items() <= tags.items():
            continue
        match = re.search(r" (-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?)$", line)
        if match:
            total += float(match.group(1))
    return total

def monitor(stats):
    zero_since = None
    threshold_s = LOOK_BACK_PERIOD_S + DOWNSCALE_DELAY_S
    while True:
        snapshot = ray.get(stats.snapshot.remote(), timeout=2)
        inflight = snapshot["current_inflight"]
        if inflight == 0:
            if zero_since is None:
                zero_since = time.time()
            zero_for_s = time.time() - zero_since
        else:
            zero_since = None
            zero_for_s = 0.0

        autoscaling_total = get_metric_value("ray_serve_autoscaling_total_requests")
        replicas = count_backend_replicas()
        print(
            f"[{time.strftime('%H:%M:%S')}] replicas={replicas} "
            f"inflight={inflight} autoscaling_total={autoscaling_total:.1f} "
            f"finished={snapshot['finished']} zero_for_s={zero_for_s:.1f}/{threshold_s}",
            flush=True,
        )
        time.sleep(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--stream", action=argparse.BooleanOptionalAction, default=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()
    STREAM = args.stream

    ray.init(ignore_reinit_error=True)
    serve.start()
    stats = StatsActor.remote()
    serve.run(Ingress.bind(Backend.bind(stats)), name=APP_NAME, route_prefix=ROUTE_PREFIX)
    print(f"Serving on http://localhost:8000{ROUTE_PREFIX} stream={STREAM}", flush=True)
    threading.Thread(target=monitor, args=(stats,), daemon=True).start()
    while True:
        time.sleep(1)
```

Save this as `load.py`:

```python
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import asyncio
import time

import aiohttp

URL = "http://localhost:8000/repro"
STREAM = True

async def run_phase(session, qps, duration_s, inflight, counters):
    interval_s = 1.0 / qps
    deadline = time.monotonic() + duration_s

    async def one_request():
        counters["sent"] += 1
        try:
            async with session.get(URL, timeout=aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=120)) as resp:
                if STREAM:
                    async for _ in resp.content.iter_chunked(1024):
                        pass
                else:
                    await resp.read()
                counters["ok"] += 1
        except Exception:
            counters["errors"] += 1

    while time.monotonic() < deadline:
        task = asyncio.create_task(one_request())
        inflight.add(task)
        task.add_done_callback(inflight.discard)
        await asyncio.sleep(interval_s)

async def main():
    global STREAM
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--stream", action=argparse.BooleanOptionalAction, default=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()
    STREAM = args.stream

    inflight = set()
    counters = {"sent": 0, "ok": 0, "errors": 0}
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        await run_phase(session, qps=1.0, duration_s=6, inflight=inflight, counters=counters)
        await run_phase(session, qps=8.0, duration_s=12, inflight=inflight, counters=counters)
        await run_phase(session, qps=1.0, duration_s=10, inflight=inflight, counters=counters)
        print(f"Stopped sending at {time.strftime('%H:%M:%S')}")
        await asyncio.sleep(20)
        if inflight:
            await asyncio.gather(*list(inflight), return_exceptions=True)
        print(counters)

asyncio.run(main())
```

Run:

```bash
python server.py --stream
```

In another terminal:

```bash
python load.py --stream
```

Non-streaming control:

```bash
python server.py --no-stream
```

```bash
python load.py --no-stream
```

## Expected

Once real backend inflight reaches `0`, the backend should downscale from 2 replicas back to 1 within about:

`look_back_period_s + downscale_delay_s = 5 + 8 = 13s`

Also, `ray_serve_autoscaling_total_requests` should stay near `0` after inflight reaches `0`.

## Actual

In the streaming repro:

- backend inflight reaches `0`
- backend stays at `2` replicas far past the `13s` threshold
- `ray_serve_autoscaling_total_requests` periodically jumps back up while inflight is still `0`

Representative lines:

```text
[03:12:33] replicas=2 inflight=0 autoscaling_total=0.0 finished=112 zero_for_s=0.0/13
[03:12:34] replicas=2 inflight=0 autoscaling_total=380.6 finished=112 zero_for_s=1.0/13
[03:12:47] replicas=2 inflight=0 autoscaling_total=0.0 finished=112 zero_for_s=13.7/13
```

In longer runs I saw the deployment remain at 2 replicas for more than 5 minutes, with periodic `autoscaling_total` spikes like ~`1074`, ~`1257`, ~`1258` even while inflight stayed at `0`.

## Control

The same files also support a non-streaming control via `--no-stream`.

That non-streaming version does **not** reproduce:

- `autoscaling_total` stays at `0` after inflight drains
- deployment downscales normally to 1 replica

So the issue appears to be specific to the streaming request lifecycle / accounting path.
ray-project/ray#61551.title: Serve autoscaling can stay pinned at 2 replicas for streaming deployments after real inflight requests drain to 0

ray-project/ray#61560.body:
## Problem
- `test_stats.py` is brittle - breaks when formatting changes, new metrics added, or platform differences occur
- Tests rely on massive string literals + canonicalize() regex
- Any formatting change breaks tests which leads to high maintenance burden

```python
# Current brittle pattern from test_dataset_stats_range
stats = canonicalize(ds.materialize().stats())
assert stats == (
    f"Operator N ReadRange->Map(<lambda>): {EXECUTION_STRING}\n"
    f"* Remote wall time: T min, T max, T mean, T total\n"
    f"* Remote cpu time: T min, T max, T mean, T total\n"
    f"* UDF time: T min, T max, T mean, T total\n"
    f"* Peak heap memory usage (MiB): H min, H max, H mean\n"
    # ... 15 more lines of hardcoded strings
)
```
## Task
- Ensure `test_stats.py` is easy to read, maintain, and not brittle

## Solution

- Use existing `Dataset._get_stats_summary()` to return structured data
- Create high-level assertion helpers in `ray/python/ray/data/tests/test_stats_assertions.py`

```python
// Assertion Helpers
assert_operator_exists(stats_summary, operator_name_pattern=None, operator_index=None)
assert_basic_operator_metrics(op_stats, check_positive=True)
assert_output_row_count(op_stats, expected_total, tolerance=0.0)
assert_single_operator_pipeline(stats_summary, expected_operator_pattern=None)
assert_memory_stats_present(op_stats)
# ... more helpers
```

## Example
- Before (brittle):
```python
stats = canonicalize(ds.materialize().stats())
assert stats == (
    f"Operator N ReadRange->Map(<lambda>): {EXECUTION_STRING}\n"
    f"* Remote wall time: T min, T max, T mean, T total\n"
    # ... 18 more lines
)
```
- After (robust):
```python
stats_summary = ds._get_stats_summary()
op = assert_single_operator_pipeline(stats_summary, "ReadRange->Map")
assert_basic_operator_metrics(op)
assert_output_row_count(op, expected_total=1000)
```

## Implementation Plan
- [ ] Create assertion helpers
- [ ] Refactor simple name checks tests (test_dataset_stats_repartition, test_dataset_stats_union, etc.)
- [ ] Refactor already structured tests (test_calculate_blocks_stats, test_summarize_blocks)
- [ ] Refactor canonicalize(repr()) tests (test_dataset_stats_streaming_execution)
- [ ] Refactor most complex test (test_dataset_stats_range)
- [ ] Refactor remaining tests in batches
- [ ] Remove `canonicalize()`, `gen_expected_metrics()`, `EXECUTION_STRING` constants

@tianyi-ge and I will be collaborating on this issue.
ray-project/ray#61560.title: [Data] Refactor Ray Data stats tests

ray-project/ray#61569.body:
## Summary

Remove dead code `_validate_dag` from `streaming_executor.py` and its associated skipped/orphaned tests.

## Context

`_validate_dag` was disabled in [#37829](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/37829) (July 2023) because it false-positively rejected valid pipelines on autoscaling clusters. It has never been re-enabled, is never called, and has been dead code for 2 years.

## Proposed Changes

Remove:
- `_validate_dag` function from `streaming_executor.py`
- `_validate_dag` import from `test_streaming_executor.py`
- `test_validate_dag` test from `test_streaming_executor.py`
- Skipped `test_e2e_option_propagation` from `test_streaming_integration.py`
- Skipped `test_configure_execution_options` from `test_new_dataset_config.py`

---
cc @bveeramani
ray-project/ray#61569.title: [Data] Remove _validate_dag and its skipped tests

ray-project/ray#61594.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

**1. The bug**
I'm running into a behavior that completely bricks my model composition deployment. Whenever a `RayActorDied` error happens in an earlier part of the chain 1) awaiting a result later on in the chain hangs and 2) its causing the calling actor to consider alive replicas as dead. This last issue is causing a deadlock: the calling actor has considers an entire deployment to be dead whereas the controller thinks everything is doing fine, so no restart is triggered. This blocks any execution of tasks on the replica, all while the originally erroring deployment is perfectly restarted.

**2. Expected behavior**
Below I provide a minimal example based on [chaining_example.py](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/releases/2.51.2/doc/source/serve/doc_code/model_composition/chaining_example.py). What I expect from the example is two things:

1. For the request that triggers the `ActorDiedError` I expect it to immediately return with said `ActorDiedError`, just like if it were a different/normal error, and not hang indefinitely until a timeout is reached.
2. For the `Ingress` router not to consider the `Multiplier` to be dead based on the `ActorDiedError` that happened inside of `Adder`. As the controller thinks the `Multiplier` is alive and healthy, this causes a deadlock.

**3. Logs**
 We start by sending a request that will trigger the `ActorDiedError`. We immediately see:

> (raylet) **A worker died or was killed** while executing a task by an unexpected system error. To troubleshoot the problem, check the logs for the dead worker.
> (ServeReplica:default:Ingress pid=141940) WARNING 2026-03-09 15:25:07,291 **default_Ingress** i52m4etr -- Replica(id='ctgdzotw', deployment='**Adder**', app='default') **will not be considered for future requests because it has died.**
> (ServeReplica:default:Ingress pid=141940) WARNING 2026-03-09 15:25:07,294 **default_Ingress** i52m4etr e9847fa1-666d-4237-a0ae-4aaf018545e0 -- Replica(id='qsp8g3w6', deployment='**Multiplier**', app='default') **will not be considered for future requests because it has died.**

So the Ingress replica immediately considers **both the Adder as well as the Multiplier** to be dead. We then see that the request times out and that the controller restarts the Adder replica:

> (ServeController pid=141933) WARNING 2026-03-09 15:25:16,727 controller 141933 -- Actor for Replica(id='ctgdzotw', deployment='Adder', app='default') crashed, marking it unhealthy immediately.
> (ServeController pid=141933) WARNING 2026-03-09 15:25:16,727 controller 141933 -- **Replica** Replica(id='ctgdzotw', deployment='**Adder**', app='default') **failed health check, stopping it**.
> (ServeController pid=141933) INFO 2026-03-09 15:25:16,729 controller 141933 -- Replica(id='ctgdzotw', deployment='Adder', app='default') is stopped.
> (ServeController pid=141933) INFO 2026-03-09 15:25:16,729 controller 141933 -- **Adding 1 replica to** Deployment(name='**Adder**', app='default').

In the meantime we send a second request that is now hanging as well. However, we do see it successfully running the first Adder step:

> (ServeReplica:default:Adder pid=141936) INFO 2026-03-09 15:25:17,585 default_Adder 81sv031u 47a3f901-3fac-4533-8142-8614c7967518 -- **CALL __call__ OK 2.4ms**

In order to prove that it's the 2nd step, i.e. Multiplier step, that is causing the issue and not the Ingress, we kill the actor for the Multiplier:

> (ServeController pid=141933) WARNING 2026-03-09 15:25:26,421 controller 141933 -- Actor for Replica(id='qsp8g3w6', deployment='Multiplier', app='default') crashed, marking it unhealthy immediately.
> (ServeController pid=141933) WARNING 2026-03-09 15:25:26,421 controller 141933 -- Replica Replica(id='qsp8g3w6', deployment='Multiplier', app='default') failed health check, stopping it.
> (ServeController pid=141933) INFO 2026-03-09 15:25:26,422 controller 141933 -- **Replica**(id='qsp8g3w6', deployment='**Multiplier**', app='default') **is stopped**.
> (ServeController pid=141933) INFO 2026-03-09 15:25:26,423 controller 141933 -- **Adding 1 replica to** Deployment(name='**Multiplier**', app='default').

If we now request the result of the previous call, that previously timed out, we find that we actually get the result:

> (ServeReplica:default:Multiplier pid=141939) INFO 2026-03-09 15:25:**27,278** default_Multiplier **ok3qboem** 47a3f901-3fac-4533-8142-8614c7967518 -- **CALL __call__ OK 2.4ms**
> (ServeReplica:default:Ingress pid=141940) INFO 2026-03-09 15:25:27,279 default_Ingress i52m4etr 47a3f901-3fac-4533-8142-8614c7967518 -- **CALL __call__ OK 15002.1ms**
> Now it worked!

So indeed the call couldn't finish because Ingress didn't find any ALIVE replicas of the Multiplier deployment. As soon as we added a new one (by removing the old one), it immediately finished. However, unfortunately it also started to consider the new replica as dead, just a few milliseconds before the above call completed:

> (ServeReplica:default:Ingress pid=141940) WARNING 2026-03-09 15:25:**27,276** default_Ingress i52m4etr e9847fa1-666d-4237-a0ae-4aaf018545e0 -- Replica(id='**ok3qboem**', deployment='Multiplier', app='default') **will not be considered for future requests because it has died**. 

Which means we end up with still a bricked deployment (Adder runs, but Multiplier doesn't):

> (ServeReplica:default:Adder pid=141936) INFO 2026-03-09 15:25:30,356 default_Adder 81sv031u f80a2e7f-3dfe-4d83-9d3c-2b6195d5a247 -- CALL __call__ OK 1.0ms
> Again it times out.

Whenever this issue happens and a single replica considers _all_ follow-up replicas to be dead, it will completely stop processing requests until it times out (if implemented). This is therefore unfortunately preventing us from deploying to prod.

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.54.0

### Reproduction script

```python
import os
import time

import ray
from ray import serve
from ray.serve.handle import DeploymentHandle, DeploymentResponse
from ray.util.state import list_actors


@serve.deployment
class Adder:
    def __init__(self, increment: int):
        self._increment = increment

    def __call__(self, val: int) -> int:
        if val == 0:
            os._exit(0)

        return val + self._increment


@serve.deployment
class Multiplier:
    def __init__(self, multiple: int):
        self._multiple = multiple

    def __call__(self, val: int) -> int:
        return val * self._multiple


@serve.deployment
class Ingress:
    def __init__(self, adder: DeploymentHandle, multiplier: DeploymentHandle):
        self._adder = adder
        self._multiplier = multiplier

    async def __call__(self, input: int) -> int:
        adder_response: DeploymentResponse = self._adder.remote(input)
        # Pass the adder response directly into the multiplier (no `await` needed).
        multiplier_response: DeploymentResponse = self._multiplier.remote(
            adder_response
        )
        # `await` the final chained response.
        return await multiplier_response


app = Ingress.bind(
    Adder.bind(increment=1),
    Multiplier.bind(multiple=2),
)

# Test that initial call works as expected
handle: DeploymentHandle = serve.run(app)
response = handle.remote(5)
assert response.result() == 12, "(5 + 1) * 2 = 12"

# Trigger ActorDiedError
response = handle.remote(0)
try:
    response.result(timeout_s=5.0)
except TimeoutError:
    print("\033[31mThis one runs out of time.\033[0m")

# Another request also hangs / times out
response = handle.remote(2)
try:
    response.result(timeout_s=5.0)
except TimeoutError:
    print("\033[31mConsequtive requests also run out of time.\033[0m")

# Let's kill the ALIVE Multiplier actor
for actor in list_actors():
    if "Multiplier" in actor.name and actor.state == "ALIVE":
        print(f"\033[31mKilled actor {actor.name} that used to be {actor.state}\033[0m")
        ray.kill(ray.get_actor(actor.name, namespace="serve"))
        time.sleep(10)

# And try again
assert response.result() == 6, "(2 + 1) * 2 = 6"
print("\033[31mNow it worked!\033[0m")
time.sleep(3)

# The restart made it work for the old call, but a new one unfortuntaly errors too
response = handle.remote(3)
try:
    response.result(timeout_s=5.0)
except TimeoutError:
    print("\033[31mAgain it times out.\033[0m")
```

### Issue Severity

High: It blocks me from completing my task.
ray-project/ray#61594.title: [Serve] Ray serve replica deadlock when actor dies earlier in a chain of model composition

ray-project/ray#61678.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Hi！We are trying to embed Ray into OceanBase DataBase. Our goal is to initialize Ray once by calling `ray.init()` during the OBServer process startup (OBServer::init()), and then allow individual SQL execution threads to submit Python tasks via `py_task_xxx.remote()`. We expected Ray to support task submission from other thread within the same process after initialization.

However, when a task is submitted from a child thread (i.e., not the startup thread that called `ray.init()`, but other SQL execution threads in the same OBServer process), Ray crashes with a mutex deadlock error and aborts the entire process.

A minimal reproduction script is attached below, which initializes Ray in the main thread and then attempts to submit a remote task from a separate child thread, mirroring our OceanBase integration scenario. Running this script would result in a fatal crash with the following error messages, indicating that Ray’s internal mutex locking logic detects a potential deadlock.

Is this an intentional safety mechanism in Ray, which prevents multi-threaded task submission? If so, are there any alternative approaches to achieve our goal—initializing Ray once in the main thread and allowing other threads to safely submit remote tasks?

ray-project/ray#61689.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Recently (with Ray 2.54.0) I have observed nodes from autoscaling worker groups considered Active and not being deleted, even though no job is running.

This happened after running a Ray Train job. I noticed the problematic nodes still had active PlacementGroupCleaner actors on them (sometimes multiple such actors on a single node). I know the placement group cleaner was added recently, so I wonder if that's the main culprit.

This is a significant issue for me, because it wastes money on nodes that are doing no work.

### Versions / Dependencies

Ray 2.54.0
Kuberay 1.5.0

Not reliably reproducible. Try running some Ray Train jobs that scale up the cluster by using a placement group.

ray-project/ray#61714.body: ### Description Currently, observing data backlog in Ray Data usually requires checking logs or Grafana metrics, which is less convenient during debugging or routine monitoring. Ray Data already exposes the queued blocks metric through the `/api/data/datasets/{dataset_id}`. This metric reflects the number of blocks waiting to be processed and can be a useful signal for identifying data backlog or pipeline bottleneck. It would be helpful if this metric could also be surfaced in the Ray Data Dashboard so users can directly observe it during dataset execution. ### Use case _No response_
ray-project/ray#61714.title: [Dashboard]  Add Queued Blocks metric to Ray Data Dashboard

ray-project/ray#61810.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

Due to [this PR](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/57610), running `ray down` will delete any attached MSIs in Azure when a cluster is taken down. This causes issues when the user is intentionally sharing an MSI between cluster (as allowed in https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/43897) since taking down one cluster deletes the shared identity.

Likely what should happen instead is that the cleanup call should first check whether the msi_name and msi_resource_group are specified in the provider config. If so, then we should not delete the MSI.

### Versions / Dependencies

ray version: 2.54.0
python 3.11

### Reproduction script

ray up clusters/azure.yaml
ray up clusters/azure2.yaml 
ray down clusters/azure.yaml <-- deletes MSI for both clusters!

With the following inside azure.yaml and azure2.yaml:
```
provider:
    type: azure
    location: westus2
    resource_group: test-group
    msi_name: ray-cluster-msi
    msi_resource_group: msi-group
```


### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#61810.title: [Cluster Launcher] [azure] ray down deletes shared MSIs

ray-project/ray#61836.body:
### What happened
                                                                                                                                               
When starting a worker node with `ray start --address=...`, `Node.__init__()` spawns processes before `check_version_info()` runs.  If a Ray/Python version mismatch is detected, `RuntimeError` is raised but the already-started processes are never cleaned up.                                                                                                              
                                                                                                                                               
### How to reproduce
1. Start a head node with Ray version X
2. On another machine with Ray version Y, run `ray start --address=<head_address>`
3. Version mismatch error is raised
4. `ps aux | grep raylet` — orphan raylet process is still running
ray-project/ray#61836.title: [Core] orphan processes when version check fails

ray-project/ray#61922.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

The FuncWrapper class defined inside `ActorMethod.options()` captures the ActorMethod via a closure cell (`func_cls = self`).  The ActorMethod holds a strong reference to the ActorHandle (`self._actor`).  After `.remote()` returns, Ray internals keep the FuncWrapper alive, which keeps the ActorMethod alive, which keeps the ActorHandle alive — forming a cycle that CPython's reference counting cannot break.

This reference cycle has caused delayed garbage collection of `ActorHandles` and causes problems for Ray Actors to clean up properly with `__ray_shutdown__`. Details are in the description of [this PR](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/61700).

### Versions / Dependencies


Python | 3.10.19 (conda-forge, Clang 19.1.7)
-- | --
OS | macOS Darwin 24.6.0 (arm64 / Apple Silicon)
Ray | 3.0.0.dev0 (local dev build)
PyArrow | 19.0.1
Pandas | 2.3.3
NumPy | 1.26.4
gRPC | 1.74.0
aiohttp | 3.13.3



### Reproduction script

```python
"""Reproduction: reference cycle in ActorMethod.options()

Run:
    python python/ray/data/tests/repro_actor_method_options_ref_cycle.py
"""

import gc
import sys
import weakref

import ray


@ray.remote
class MyActor:
    def __ray_shutdown__(self):
        print("  >> __ray_shutdown__ called!")

    def task(self, x):
        return x


def trace_chain_from_handle(handle):
    """Walk the reference chain: ActorHandle ← ActorMethod ← cell ← FuncWrapper."""
    seen = {id(handle)}

    # Step 1: Find the ActorMethod holding this handle via ._actor
    actor_method = None
    for ref in gc.get_referrers(handle):
        if type(ref).__name__ == "frame" or id(ref) in seen:
            continue
        # ActorMethod stores handle in its __dict__ as '_actor'
        if isinstance(ref, dict) and ref.get("_actor") is handle:
            seen.add(id(ref))
            # Find the object owning this __dict__
            for owner in gc.get_referrers(ref):
                if (
                    type(owner).__name__ == "ActorMethod"
                    and getattr(owner, "_actor", None) is handle
                ):
                    actor_method = owner
                    seen.add(id(owner))
                    break
            break

    if actor_method is None:
        print("    ActorHandle  ←  (no ActorMethod found)")
        return

    method_name = getattr(actor_method, "_method_name", "?")
    print(f"    ActorHandle")
    print(f"      ▲")
    print(f"      └── ActorMethod._actor  (method={method_name!r})")

    # Step 2: Find the closure cell holding this ActorMethod
    cell = None
    for ref in gc.get_referrers(actor_method):
        if type(ref).__name__ == "cell" and id(ref) not in seen:
            try:
                if ref.cell_contents is actor_method:
                    cell = ref
                    seen.add(id(ref))
                    break
            except ValueError:
                pass

    if cell is None:
        print("            ▲")
        print("            └── (no closure cell found)")
        return

    print("            ▲")
    print("            └── closure cell  (func_cls = ActorMethod)")

    # Step 3: Find the function whose __closure__ contains this cell
    func = None
    for ref in gc.get_referrers(cell):
        if id(ref) in seen:
            continue
        if isinstance(ref, tuple):
            seen.add(id(ref))
            for owner in gc.get_referrers(ref):
                if callable(owner) and hasattr(owner, "__closure__"):
                    if owner.__closure__ is ref:
                        func = owner
                        seen.add(id(owner))
                        break
            if func:
                break

    if func is None:
        print("                  ▲")
        print("                  └── (no enclosing function found)")
        return

    qn = getattr(func, "__qualname__", repr(func))
    print("                  ▲")
    print(f"                  └── {qn}()")

    # Step 4: Find what holds the function (FuncWrapper instance, etc.)
    for ref in gc.get_referrers(func):
        if id(ref) in seen or type(ref).__name__ == "frame":
            continue
        rtype = type(ref).__name__
        if "FuncWrapper" in rtype:
            print("                        ▲")
            print(f"                        └── {rtype} instance")
        elif isinstance(ref, dict):
            # Might be a FuncWrapper's __dict__ or a class __dict__
            for owner in gc.get_referrers(ref):
                otype = type(owner).__name__
                if id(owner) not in seen and otype != "frame":
                    if "FuncWrapper" in otype:
                        print("                        ▲")
                        print(f"                        └── {otype} instance")
                    elif otype == "mappingproxy":
                        # Class __dict__ is a mappingproxy
                        for cls_ref in gc.get_referrers(owner):
                            ctype = type(cls_ref).__name__
                            if ctype == "type":
                                print("                        ▲")
                                print(
                                    f"                        └── class {cls_ref.__name__}"
                                )
                                break


def check(weak_handle, label):
    handle = weak_handle()
    if handle is None:
        print(f"  ActorHandle: collected  ✓")
        print()
        return
    print(f"  ActorHandle: ALIVE  ✗  (refcount={sys.getrefcount(handle)})")
    print()
    trace_chain_from_handle(handle)
    print()


def demo_options_remote():
    print("=" * 70)
    print("CASE 1: actor.method.options(num_returns=1).remote(42)")
    print("        Uses FuncWrapper closure → REFERENCE CYCLE")
    print("=" * 70)

    actor = MyActor.remote()
    weak = weakref.ref(actor)

    ref = actor.task.options(num_returns=1).remote(42)
    ray.get(ref)

    del actor
    print("\n--- After `del actor` (gc disabled, ref-counting only) ---")
    print("    Expecting __ray_shutdown__ to fire... but it does NOT.")
    check(weak, "options().remote()")

    del ref
    print("--- After also `del ref` (still no gc.collect) ---")
    print("    Still no __ray_shutdown__. The cycle keeps the handle alive.")
    check(weak, "options().remote()")

    gc.collect()
    print("--- After gc.collect() (cyclic GC breaks the cycle) ---")
    check(weak, "options().remote()")


def demo_underscore_remote():
    print("=" * 70)
    print("CASE 2: actor.method._remote(args=[42], num_returns=1)")
    print("        Direct call, no FuncWrapper → NO CYCLE")
    print("=" * 70)

    actor = MyActor.remote()
    weak = weakref.ref(actor)

    ref = actor.task._remote(args=[42], kwargs={}, num_returns=1)
    ray.get(ref)

    del actor
    print("\n--- After `del actor` (ref-counting frees it immediately) ---")
    check(weak, "_remote()")

    del ref
    print("--- After `del ref` ---")
    check(weak, "_remote()")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    gc.disable()
    ray.init(num_cpus=1)
    print()

    demo_options_remote()
    print()
    demo_underscore_remote()

    ray.shutdown()
```

### Issue Severity

Medium: It is a significant difficulty but I can work around it.
ray-project/ray#61922.title: [Data] Reference Cycle in ActorMethod.options()

ray-project/ray#61961.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

1. When I run `algo.evaluate()`, if I log `print(self.training)` inside my RLModule's forward function, it prints `True`. This makes me think that `model.eval()` was never called.
2. When you are running evaluation, shouldn't `self.training` be set to `False` (which implies that `model.eval()` was called).

### Versions / Dependencies

ray==2.43.0
torch==2.6.0

MacOS 15.7.3
Python 3.11.5

### Reproduction script

I create the following algorithm:
```
agentConfig = (
        PPOConfig()
        .environment(env)
        .api_stack(
            enable_rl_module_and_learner=True, enable_env_runner_and_connector_v2=True
        )
        .rl_module(
            rl_module_spec=RLModuleSpec(
                module_class=module,
                model_config={
                    "hiddenSize": args.hiddenSize,
                    "numLayers": args.numLayers,
                    "inputSize": visionRange * 2 + 1 if args.fogged else mazeSize,
                    "max_seq_len": args.memoryLen,
                    "mazeSize": mazeSize,
                },
            ),
        )
        .learners(num_gpus_per_learner=1 if torch.cuda.is_available() else 0)
        .evaluation(
            evaluation_num_env_runners=1 if args.debug else 8,
            evaluation_duration_unit="episodes",
            evaluation_duration=1 if args.debug else 128,
        )
        .training(
            lr=args.lr,
            entropy_coeff=0.01,
            learner_class=PPOTorchLearnerWithSelfPredLoss,
            learner_config_dict=config,
        )
    )
agent = agentConfig.build_algo()
agent.evaluate()
```

### Issue Severity

Low
ray-project/ray#61961.title: [RLlib] RLModule self.training is set to True even during evaluation

ray-project/ray#62228.body:
## Summary

Streaming SSE responses from Ray Serve LLM deployments emit `data: [DONE]` twice at the end of the stream. This affects **all** deployment types (basic, DP, and PD).

## Reproduction

```python
from ray import serve
from ray.serve.llm import LLMConfig, build_openai_app

config = LLMConfig(
    model_loading_config={"model_id": "microsoft/Phi-tiny-MoE-instruct"},
    engine_kwargs={"max_model_len": 1024, "max_num_seqs": 32},
)

app = build_openai_app({"llm_config": config})
serve.run(app, blocking=False)
```

```bash
curl -s http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model": "microsoft/Phi-tiny-MoE-instruct", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}], "max_tokens": 32, "stream": true}'
```

**Actual output** (end of stream):
```
data: {"id":"...","choices":[{"index":0,"delta":{"content":""},"finish_reason":"stop",...}]}

data: [DONE]

data: [DONE]

```

**Expected output:**
```
data: {"id":"...","choices":[{"index":0,"delta":{"content":""},"finish_reason":"stop",...}]}

data: [DONE]

```

## Root Cause

Two layers independently append `data: [DONE]\n\n`:

1. **vLLM engine**: `vllm/entrypoints/openai/chat_completion/serving.py` and `completion/serving.py` yield `"data: [DONE]\n\n"` as the final chunk in their streaming generators.

2. **Ray Serve ingress**: `ray/llm/_internal/serve/core/ingress/ingress.py` line 355 in `_openai_json_wrapper` unconditionally appends `yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"` after exhausting the generator.

The vLLM `[DONE]` passes through the engine wrapper (`vllm_engine.py`) and `LLMServer` unmodified, then the ingress adds a second one.

## Suggested Fix

Either:
- Filter out `data: [DONE]` from the vLLM engine output before it reaches the ingress, or
- Check in `_openai_json_wrapper` whether `[DONE]` was already yielded before appending it

## Versions

- Ray: 2.55 (nightly)
- vLLM: 0.18.0
ray-project/ray#62228.title: [llm][serve] Duplicate 'data: [DONE]' in streaming SSE responses

ray-project/ray#62250.body:
### What happened + What you expected to happen

## What happened + What you expected to happen
When using Ray in **manual cluster setup** (i.e., with `ReadOnlyProvider`), the autoscaler v2 throws a `TypeError` when it attempts to terminate instances:
```
TypeError: ReadOnlyProvider.terminate() got an unexpected keyword argument 'ids'
```
**Full traceback:**
```
ERROR autoscaler.py:228 -- ReadOnlyProvider.terminate() got an unexpected keyword argument 'ids'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../ray/autoscaler/v2/autoscaler.py", line 212, in update_autoscaling_state
  File ".../ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/reconciler.py", line 126, in reconcile
  File ".../ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/reconciler.py", line 302, in _step_next
  File ".../ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/reconciler.py", line 1263, in _terminate_instances
  File ".../ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/reconciler.py", line 635, in _update_instance_manager
  File ".../ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/instance_manager.py", line 126, in update_instance_manager_state
  File ".../ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/subscribers/cloud_instance_updater.py", line 46, in notify
  File ".../ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/subscribers/cloud_instance_updater.py", line 63, in _terminate_instances
    self._cloud_provider.terminate(
TypeError: ReadOnlyProvider.terminate() got an unexpected keyword argument 'ids'
```

## Root Cause
`ReadOnlyProvider.terminate()` (`python/ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/cloud_providers/read_only/cloud_provider.py:64`) has an **incorrect method signature** that does not match the `ICloudInstanceProvider` abstract interface (`python/ray/autoscaler/v2/instance_manager/node_provider.py:223`).
**Abstract interface (correct):**
```python
@abstractmethod
def terminate(self, ids: List[CloudInstanceId], request_id: str) -> None: ...
```
**`ReadOnlyProvider` implementation (incorrect):**
```python
def terminate(self, instance_id: CloudInstanceId) -> None:
    raise NotImplementedError("Cannot terminate instances in read-only mode.")
```
Two mismatches:
1. Parameter name is `instance_id` (single value) instead of `ids` (list).
2. `request_id: str` parameter is missing entirely.
The caller in `cloud_instance_updater.py:61-63` correctly follows the interface:
```python
self._cloud_provider.terminate(
    ids=cloud_instance_ids, request_id=str(uuid.uuid4())
)
```

## Suggested Fix
```python
def terminate(self, ids: List[CloudInstanceId], request_id: str) -> None:
    raise NotImplementedError("Cannot terminate instances in read-only mode.")
```
The `NotImplementedError` is semantically correct for a read-only provider — only the signature needs fixing.
## Other information
- `NodeProviderAdapter.terminate()` and `KubeRayProvider.terminate()` both correctly implement the interface.
- Affects autoscaler v2 with `ReadOnlyProvider` (laptop mode / manual cluster setup).

### Versions / Dependencies

ray 2.54.0
python 3.10

### Reproduction script

Start a local ray cluster and see ./monitor.log.

### Issue Severity

Low: It annoys or frustrates me.
ray-project/ray#62250.title: [autoscaler][v2] ReadOnlyProvider.terminate() signature mismatch with ICloudInstanceProvider interface causes TypeError

### 🐛 Describe the bug Introduced in https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/29621/, unpickling`MediaWithBytes` leads to `RecursionError`s. This is seen when using vLLM with Ray. `pickle` attempts to access members like `__setstate__` while re-instantiating objects. These `__getattr__`s are redirected to the `MediaWithBytes`'s `__getattr__` override. Since `self.media` is not set, another `__getattr__` will be triggered to find it, leading to infinite recursion. For example: ``` E File "/home/ray/anaconda3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ray/air/util/object_extensions/arrow.py", line 96, in as_py E return pickle.load(pa.BufferReader(self.value.as_buffer())) E ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ E File "/home/ray/anaconda3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/vllm/multimodal/base.py", line 38, in __getattr__ E return getattr(self.media, name) E ^^^^^^^^^^ E File "/home/ray/anaconda3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/vllm/multimodal/base.py", line 38, in __getattr__ E return getattr(self.media, name) E ^^^^^^^^^^ E File "/home/ray/anaconda3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/vllm/multimodal/base.py", line 38, in __getattr__ E return getattr(self.media, name) E ^^^^^^^^^^ E [Previous line repeated 980 more times] E RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded ``` Repro: ```python """Minimal reproducer for pickle + __getattr__ recursion bug.""" import pickle from dataclasses import dataclass @dataclass class BrokenWrapper: """Wrapper that delegates attribute access - but breaks during unpickling.""" media: object original_bytes: bytes def __getattr__(self, name: str): # This causes infinite recursion during unpickling! # When 'media' isn't set yet, accessing self.media triggers __getattr__('media') return getattr(self.media, name) @dataclass class FixedWrapper: """Same wrapper, but pickle-safe.""" media: object original_bytes: bytes def __getattr__(self, name: str): if "media" not in self.__dict__: raise AttributeError(name) return getattr(self.media, name) class FakeImage: """Fake image object with some attributes.""" width = 100 height = 200 if __name__ == "__main__": img = FakeImage() # Test the broken version print("=== BrokenWrapper ===") broken = BrokenWrapper(media=img, original_bytes=b"fake") print(f"Created: {broken}") print(f"Accessing .width: {broken.width}") # Works - delegates to img.width pickled = pickle.dumps(broken) print(f"Pickled successfully ({len(pickled)} bytes)") try: unpickled = pickle.loads(pickled) print(f"Unpickled: {unpickled}") except RecursionError as e: print(f"RecursionError during unpickle: {e}") # Test the fixed version print("\n=== FixedWrapper ===") fixed = FixedWrapper(media=img, original_bytes=b"fake") print(f"Created: {fixed}") print(f"Accessing .width: {fixed.width}") pickled = pickle.dumps(fixed) print(f"Pickled successfully ({len(pickled)} bytes)") unpickled = pickle.loads(pickled) print(f"Unpickled: {unpickled}") print(f"Accessing .width after unpickle: {unpickled.width}") ``` ### Before submitting a new issue... - [x] Make sure you already searched for relevant issues, and asked the chatbot living at the bottom right corner of the [documentation page](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/), which can answer lots of frequently asked questions.
vllm-project/vllm#30818.title: [Bug]: Unpickling MediaWithBytes dataclass leads to RecursionError

vllm-project/vllm#31181.body:
### Name of failing test

`pytest -v -s tests/models/test_initialization.py -k 'not (Gemma3 or ModernBert or Qwen2_5_VL or Qwen2_5vl or Qwen2VL or TransformersMultiModalEmbeddingModel or TransformersMultiModalForSequenceClassification or Ultravox or Phi4Multimodal or LlavaNextVideo or MiniCPMO or Lfm2Moe or PaliGemma or RobertaForSequenceClassification or Ovis2_5 or Fuyu or DeepseekOCR or KimiVL)'`

### Basic information

- [ ] Flaky test
- [x] Can reproduce locally
- [x] Caused by external libraries (e.g. bug in `transformers`)

### 🧪 Describe the failing test

There is an issue with latest transformers version, where the test triggers:
```
ImportError while loading conftest '/vllm-workspace/tests/conftest.py'.
tests/conftest.py:48: in <module>
    from tests.models.utils import TokensTextLogprobs, TokensTextLogprobsPromptLogprobs
tests/models/utils.py:13: in <module>
    from vllm.config.model import ModelConfig, ModelDType, RunnerOption
/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/vllm/config/__init__.py:18: in <module>
    from vllm.config.model import (
/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/vllm/config/model.py:14: in <module>
    from transformers.configuration_utils import ALLOWED_LAYER_TYPES
E   ImportError: cannot import name 'ALLOWED_LAYER_TYPES' from 'transformers.configuration_utils' (/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/transformers/configuration_utils.py)
```

Issue on upstream transformers API: https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/issues/43009
This affects both NVIDIA and AMD CI.

### 📝 History of failing test

Example CI logs from AMD CI: https://buildkite.com/vllm/amd-ci/builds/1981/steps/canvas?sid=019b4751-fd00-4c11-880e-60ab16072fa5#019b4751-fd5a-4fa3-a9cd-fcff5c2d1067/L450

### CC List.

_No response_
